1.1: Joshua's Hebrew name (Yehoshua) means 'YHWH saves' — the same name as Jesus (Greek Iēsous, from the LXX transliteration of Yehoshua). The name-identity is not coincidental: Hebrews 4:8 uses the identity of names to make a theological argument ('if Joshua [Iēsous] had given them rest ...'). Joshua is Israel's deliverer who leads the people into the promised land after Moses; Jesus is the deliverer who leads his people into the ultimate rest that Canaan only shadowed. The structural parallel is complete: Moses (law) could not bring Israel into rest; Joshua/Jesus (the one YHWH saves) is the one who completes the journey.
1.2: Moshe avdi met — "Moses my servant is dead." The title eved (servant) is not withdrawn at Moses's death; he is still "my servant" even as his death is announced. The perfect met (he died) states a completed fact. The transition is abrupt: YHWH does not pause to grieve before giving Joshua his commission. The brevity models how covenant purpose continues uninterrupted through the death of individuals — a pattern the NT applies to Christ's risen commissioning of the disciples immediately after the resurrection.
1.3: kol makom asher tidrok kaph reglekhem bo natati lakhem — "every place where the sole of your foot treads I have given to you." The perfect natati (I have given) describes a future gift as already accomplished — the Hebrew prophetic perfect. In God's decree the land is already given before the foot treads; the conquest is the temporal enactment of an eternal decision. The foot-treading idiom (possession by physical presence) is ancient Near Eastern legal language for land-grant confirmation.
1.4: The boundaries — wilderness, Lebanon, Euphrates, Hittite land, Great Sea — define the maximal extent of the Abrahamic land promise (cf. Gen 15:18-21: from the River of Egypt to the Euphrates). The fourfold boundary formula is a formal legal description, not geographic hyperbole. The actual settlement of the judges period fell far short of these limits; the promise's full scope remains eschatological in the NT reading (Rom 4:13: heir of the world).
1.5: lo yityatsev ish lefanekha — "no man shall take a stand before you." Nitsav (hitpael of ytsb) means to station oneself, take a firm stand in combat. The negated promise reverses this: no enemy shall successfully hold position against Joshua. The grounds follow: ki asher hayiti im Moshe ehyeh imakh — "as I was with Moses, ehyeh (I will be) with you." The verb ehyeh is the first-person form of the divine name YHWH (I AM / I WILL BE, Exod 3:14) — the promise of divine presence carries the weight of the divine name.
1.6: hazaq we'emats — "be strong and courageous." This two-word imperative is the key command of Joshua 1, appearing three times (vv. 6, 7, 9) plus a fourth in v.18 from the people. Hazaq (from hzq) is physical-moral strength; amats (from 'mts) is resolution, determination that does not waver. Neither is an emotion to manufacture — they are commanded responses to a stated ground: YHWH's presence and faithfulness to the promise. The inheritance is given; courage means acting on what God has already secured.
1.7: raq hazaq we'emats me'od lishmos la'asot kekol hatorah — "only be very strong and courageous to observe to do according to all the law." Raq introduces a qualification or restriction: the one caveat on the promise is covenant faithfulness. lo tasur mimenu yamin usmol — "do not turn from it right or left" — the idiom for complete, bilateral fidelity (Deut 5:32; 17:11). Obedience is not the condition of salvation but the structure of covenant life in the land.
1.8: lo yamush sefer hatorah hazeh mipicha — "this Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth." Mush = to depart, remove; the negative command pictures Torah as a permanent resident on the lips — the orality of the ancient scroll tradition. vehagita bo yomam valailah — hagah (to meditate/murmur) is an oral word: it describes the low vocalization of one reviewing a text, the same term used in Ps 1:2. The Law is not a file to consult but a text to speak continuously. ki az tatsliyach et derakekha — then you will make your way prosperous; tsalah (hiphil) = cause to succeed/prosper — covenant success attached to Torah-immersion.
1.9: The third repetition of hazaq we'emats is paired with the strongest ground: ki imkha YHWH elohekha bekhol asher telekh — "for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go." The ki (for/because) makes divine presence the logical basis of courage, not a reward for it. The phrase bekhol asher telekh (wherever you go) is unlimited — not only in battle but in every domain of life. The NT applies this same principle to Christian confidence through Christ's permanent presence (Matt 28:20; Heb 13:5, quoting Josh 1:5).
1.10: vayyetsav Yehoshua et shoterei ha'am — "Joshua commanded the officers of the people." Shotrim (officers/scribes) were the administrative tier of Israelite governance, responsible for organizing and communicating commands to the community. The smooth handover from Moses to Joshua is institutional as well as personal — Joshua inherits both the prophetic and administrative roles.
1.11: ivru beqerev hamachaneh — "pass through the midst of the camp." The military camp of Israel in the wilderness period had a defined organization (Num 2). The three-day notice before the Jordan crossing (od shloshet yamim) creates space for preparation and communal readiness. Tsiydah = provisions/supplies for the journey — the same word used in the Passover preparation language.
1.12: Joshua's address to the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh recalls their land grant east of the Jordan (Num 32). Their situation is legally complex: they have their inheritance already but are obligated to help their brothers take theirs. The address tests whether tribal self-interest will override covenant solidarity.
1.13: zakhor et hadavar asher tsivah etkhem Moshe eved YHWH — "remember the word that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you." Zakhor (remember) in Hebrew means more than mental recall — it means to act in accordance with what is remembered. Menuchah (rest) here is used of the Transjordanian settlement: YHWH has given them rest. The same term becomes the key word in Hebrews 3-4 for the eschatological rest Christ provides.
1.14: nashekhem tap umiknekhem — "your wives, your little ones, and your livestock." The listing distinguishes those who stay from those who must fight. gibborei hachayil — "the mighty men of valor" — the military elite; chayil is the same word used for Boaz and for the virtuous woman of Prov 31 (strength, worth, excellence — context determines which dimension is primary).
1.15: ad asher yaniyach YHWH la'acheikhem kakhem — "until the LORD gives your brothers rest (haniyach) as he has given you." The verb nwach (hiphil: give rest to) echoes through Joshua as the fulfillment vocabulary for the land-promises. Covenant solidarity means the Transjordanian tribes cannot enjoy their rest while their brothers are still in the wilderness.
1.16: kol asher titsavenu na'aseh vekhol asher tishlacheinu nelekh — "all that you command us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go." The people's response mirrors the unconditional obedience they pledged to Moses. The pairing of tsivah (command) and shalach (send) covers both instruction and commission — total availability to Joshua's authority.
1.17: raq yihyeh YHWH elohekha imakh ka'asher hayah im Moshe — "only may the LORD your God be with you as he was with Moses." The people's obedience is conditioned not on Joshua's own qualities but on his having what Moses had: the divine presence. This is perceptive theology: the continuity of authority is the continuity of the ehyeh imakh promise, not of the person's gifts.
1.18: kol ish asher yamreh et picha — "any man who rebels against your command." Marah = rebel, be rebellious/defiant — the same word used for Israel's wilderness rebellion. The death penalty for rebellion against Joshua's authority means that Joshua's command carries covenant weight — to reject him is to reject the YHWH-appointed leader. raq chazaq we'emats — the fourth repetition of the command, this time from the people to their leader: an act of encouragement and covenant commitment simultaneously.

2.18: et tikvat chut hashani hazeh tiksheri bevad haChalon: 'this scarlet thread [tikvat chut hashani] you shall tie in the window.' Tikvah can mean both 'cord/thread' and 'hope' — some patristic interpreters (Justin Martyr, Origen) noted that the word for Rahab's hope (tikvah) is the same as the word for her scarlet cord; her hope and the scarlet cord are linguistically the same thing. Whether or not this wordplay is original, the typological point is clear: the scarlet cord marks the household for salvation as the Passover blood marked the doorposts; those inside are safe, those outside are not. Rahab's inclusion in Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:5) is a Gentile woman's faith being woven into the Messiah's line.
2.1: vayyishlach Yehoshua ben-Nun min-hashittim shnayim anashim meraggelim chashah — "Joshua sent two men secretly as spies." Meraggelim (spies) comes from ragal (to walk about, to spy out); the earlier spying mission in Numbers 13-14 had catastrophic results from twelve men. Two men, sent secretly, parallels the earlier two faithful spies (Caleb and Joshua). Zonah — the word for Rahab's profession can mean either prostitute or innkeeper in some ANE contexts; the LXX uses pornē (prostitute), Heb 11:31 and Jas 2:25 follow this, and the context implies the same.
2.2: The king of Jericho's intelligence network is immediately active: the spies are known to have entered the city. The verb lun (to lodge/spend the night) indicates the spies stayed overnight — heightening the urgency of discovery. The rapid information flow reveals a city on high alert, aware that Israel has camped across the Jordan.
2.3: hotsi'i et ha'anashim haba'im elayikh asher ba'u leveitakh — "bring out the men who came to you, who entered your house." The king's phrasing treats Rahab as complicit by virtue of hospitality — her house is the site of the security breach. Lasur et kol ha'arets ba'u — "for they have come to spy out all the land" — the same infinitive construct used of the earlier Mosaic spying mission.
2.4: vatiqach ha'ishah et shnei ha'anashim vatitspenehu — "the woman took the two men and hid them." The perfect vatiqach and vatitspenehu describe her quick action before the king's messengers arrived. Her lie to the king (lo yada'ati me'ayin hema — "I did not know where they were from") is the text's ethical crux: she deceives the king to protect the spies. The narrator presents her action as faith-driven without commenting on the ethics of the deception.
2.5: Rahab's misdirection — the men left at dusk when the city gate was closing, pursue them quickly — is precisely timed and plausible. Bishca'or lisgor hashaar vahachoshekh — as darkness and gate-closing coincide, the window for pursuit seems narrow. Her detailed false testimony serves the spies' safety. The Talmud later debated whether her lie was justified by the greater good of Israel's mission.
2.6: vehi he'eletatam hagagah vatitmenehem bepishte ha'ets — "she had taken them up to the roof and hidden them under the stalks of flax she had laid out." Pishta (flax) — stalks of harvested flax laid out to dry on the flat roof — was common in ancient Canaan. The detail is realistic: the roof was a work space. The flax concealment is a practical detail that also carries symbolic weight: flax was used to make linen, which is associated with priestly garments.
2.7: The king's men pursue the spies toward the Jordan fords; hama'abarot (the fords) are the crossing points on the Jordan — the same topographic feature Joshua will exploit in chapters 3-4. The city gate shuts behind the pursuers, sealing the spies' escape route — but also, unknowingly, completing the city's own isolation before judgment.
2.8: veheimah terem yishkavun vehi al'ta alehem al hagag — "before they lay down, she came up to them on the roof." The timing — before the spies settle for sleep — shows Rahab's urgency. She knows that what she is about to say will determine her fate and her family's. Her initiative frames the following speech as her own act of faith, not merely a diplomatic negotiation.
2.9: yada'ti ki natan YHWH lakhem et ha'arets — "I know that the LORD has given you the land." Her confession uses the Hebrew perfect natan — already given, from God's side, before the conquest begins. Napal ematkhem aleinu — "your terror has fallen on us" — emah (terror/dread) is a specifically covenantal terror: YHWH promised to send divine dread before Israel (Exod 23:27; Deut 2:25). Rahab recognizes this terror as divine action.
2.10: shama'nu et asher hovish YHWH et mei yam-suph — "we heard how the LORD dried up the Red Sea." The Exodus events (forty years prior) are still the defining news of the region. Hekharam (devoted them to destruction) — the same cherem root that governs Jericho's treatment. Rahab's knowledge is secondhand but sufficient: hearing produces faith, the same pattern Paul describes in Rom 10:17.
2.11: vayimmas levavenu velo qama od ruach be'ish mippeneikhem — "our hearts melted and no spirit remained in anyone because of you." Mamas = to melt, dissolve (the same word for the wax-melting of Ps 68:2). ki YHWH elohekhem hu elohim bashamayim mima'al — "for the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above and on earth below." This is a Gentile woman's monotheistic confession — structurally identical to the Shema's implications — drawn from observing YHWH's acts in history rather than from receiving the Torah.
2.12: weatta hishavi'u na li beYHWH — "now swear to me by the LORD." Rahab invokes YHWH's name for the oath — not Baal or any Canaanite deity. Her appeal is to the one she has just confessed as the God of heaven and earth. va'asitem gam atem im beit avi chesed — "and you will also show chesed (faithful love) with my father's household." She uses the covenant term chesed — the steadfast love that characterizes YHWH's dealings with his people — in her negotiation. A Canaanite prostitute appropriates Israel's covenant vocabulary.
2.13: vehichyitem et avi ve'et immi — "and you will spare my father and my mother." Hichyitem (hiphil of chayah) = cause to live, preserve alive. The scope of the request — father, mother, brothers, sisters, and all who belong to them — mirrors the Passover pattern: those inside the marked household are protected; the community of the rescued is defined by the sign (the scarlet cord/blood on the doorposts).
2.14: naphshenu tachteikhem lamut — "our life for yours to die." The idiom pledges the spies' lives as surety for Rahab's. Im lo taggidu et davarenu zeh vehayah ka'asher yitaneha YHWH lanu et ha'arets — when YHWH gives us the land, we will deal faithfully with you. The oath is conditional: Rahab must not disclose their mission. Chesed we'emet — the same paired virtues that describe YHWH's character (Exod 34:6); the spies promise YHWH-like faithfulness.
2.15: vatoridem becheevel derech hachalon — "she let them down by a rope through the window." The rope is the physical connection between Rahab's world and Israel's. ki veitah beqir hachomah uveqir hachomah hi yoshevet — "her house was in the city wall and she lived in the wall." A house built into the city wall itself: her position is architecturally exposed, yet the most secure location for the escape — the wall is also the place of greatest danger when the city falls, but the scarlet cord marks her window for protection.
2.16: Three days in the hill country — the same three-day period that appears throughout Joshua's early chapters (cf. 1:11; 3:2). Pen tipge'u bam — "lest you meet them" — the pursuit is real and ongoing. The instruction to hide until pursuit ceases is tactically precise. The three-day structure has liturgical resonance: three days of hiddenness followed by emergence/deliverance.
2.17: neqiyim anachnu mishvu'ateikh hazeh — "we will be free of this oath you have bound us to." The men qualify the oath before departing: the scarlet cord must be in the window at the time of Israel's arrival, and the family must be inside the house. If Rahab fails to maintain these conditions, the oath is voided. The precise legal language of the oath mirrors Israelite covenant structure: conditions, signs, and consequences.
2.19: vekhol asher yetse midaltei veitakh hachutsa damo beroshon — "blood will be on his head." The idiom of blood-guilt (damo beroshon) is standard legal Hebrew for self-incurred responsibility: those who leave the marked house during the attack die by their own choice. The inverse is equally precise: those inside are under the oath's protection regardless of their personal standing. The house, not personal merit, is the determining factor.
2.20: The oath-release condition — if Rahab reveals the mission — uses the same legal language as the previous verse. Neqiyim anachnu mishvu'ateikh = free from your oath. The careful delineation of conditions shows that this is a formal covenant arrangement, not merely a promise. Both parties have obligations; both parties bear consequences for breach.
2.21: vatiqshor et tikvat hashani bachalon — "she tied the scarlet cord in the window." Tikvah means both "cord/thread" and "hope" — Rahab's hope is the scarlet cord; the thing that marks her household for salvation is simultaneously the word for expectation. The wordplay was noted by patristic interpreters (Justin Martyr, Origen) as a type of faith. The cord goes up immediately — she does not wait to see if the promise will be honored.
2.22: The three days of hiding in the hill country while the pursuit searches the road and the Jordan fords — the classic military misdirection. Vayashovu haradephim veyvaqshu velo matse'u — "the pursuers searched but did not find." The passive-voice absence of finding suggests divine concealment rather than mere luck. The three-day period mirrors the three-day notice given to Israel before the Jordan crossing.
2.23: vayashuvu sheney ha'anashim vayyerdu min hehur vaya'avoru — "the two men returned, came down from the hills, crossed over." The return journey is reported tersely: three verbs, no difficulty. The spies' mission succeeded; they carry intelligence and a covenant. They report to Joshua vet kol ha'arets — all that had happened to them.
2.24: vayyomeru el Yehoshua ki natan YHWH veyadenu et kol ha'arets — "the LORD has given all the land into our hands." The spies' report is pure faith-affirmation, using the same prophetic perfect the spies from Numbers 13 failed to speak: not "this is the land; the inhabitants are fearsome" but "YHWH has given it." Gam namagu kol yoshevei ha'arets mippaneinu — "all the inhabitants of the land have melted before us" — namagu = they have dissolved. Rahab's testimony (2:11) is confirmed as the state of the entire land.

3.1: vayyashkem Yehoshua baboqer — "Joshua rose early in the morning." The waw-consecutive imperfect signals sequential narrative past; the early rising (hashkem) is a recurring marker of urgent, divinely-directed action throughout Joshua (3:1; 6:12; 7:16; 8:10). Shittim to the Jordan bank: the final staging camp before the crossing. The people yalinu sham lifnei aboram — lodged there before crossing — a further three-day wait (cf. 1:11).
3.2: miqets shloshet yamim vayyaaveru hashoterim beqerev hamachaneh — "after three days the officers passed through the midst of the camp." The three-day structure closes; the moment of crossing has arrived. The officers' circuit through the camp is the same administrative procedure as ch. 1:10-11 — Joshua's command structure operates through shotrim (officers/scribes).
3.3: keraotekhem et aron berit YHWH Elohekhem vehakohanim haLeviyim nosim oto — "when you see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God carried by the Levitical priests." The ark's appearance is the signal for the march. The full title — ark of the covenant of YHWH your God — emphasizes the covenant, the divine name, and the people's relational identity as "your God." The ark had led in wilderness marching (Num 10:33); now it leads the land-entry.
3.4: ach rachov yihyeh veineikhem uveno alfahim ba'ammah — "but there shall be a distance of about two thousand cubits between you and it." Two thousand cubits (approximately 1 km) — a buffer zone of reverence. Lo tiqrevu eilav — do not come near it. Lema'an tishdeu et haderekh asher telechu vah — so you may know the way to walk in — the ark's lead position makes it the pathfinder for ground the people have never entered.
3.5: hithqaddeshu ki machar ya'aseh YHWH beqirbekhem nifla'ot — "consecrate yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do wonders among you." Hithqaddeshu is the hitpael of qadash — reflexive sanctification, preparing oneself for encounter with the holy. The wonders (nifla'ot) are announced before they occur, so that when they happen Israel knows the source. Nifla'ot is the word for the plagues and the Exodus miracles — Joshua uses it to frame the Jordan crossing as a new Exodus event.
3.6: seu et aron haberit veivru lifnei ha'am — "take up the ark of the covenant and pass on before the people." The ark's front position is the key spatial arrangement: it enters the river first, demonstrating that YHWH goes ahead. The priests are the ark's carriers, maintaining the Levitical requirement (Num 4:15; Deut 10:8). The physical arrangement embodies theology: YHWH in the vanguard, the people following.
3.7: bayyom hazeh achel gedallekha be'einei kol Yisrael — "today I will begin to make you great in the sight of all Israel." Gadal (piel: to make great, to magnify) is the same verb used for YHWH's exaltation of the patriarchs. Ba'asher heyiti im Moshe ehyeh imakh — the promise of ch. 1:5 returns: as I was with Moses I will be with you. The Jordan crossing will be Joshua's investiture — the moment all Israel sees that YHWH's presence has passed to him.
3.8: ke'amdekhem al qetzeh mei hayarden taamodun — "when you stand at the edge of the Jordan's waters, stand firm." Amadon (qal imperfect) — the priests are to take their stand at the water's edge. They must step in before the miracle occurs. The sequence matters: obedient entry into the water precedes the divine response. This is not magic but faith-action — the miracle is attached to the priests' prior obedience, not to any ritual formula.
3.9: geshu hena ushme'u et divrei YHWH Eloheikhem — "come close here and hear the words of the LORD your God." The summons to draw near (geshah) and listen (shema') precedes the miraculous announcement. The Sinai pattern is inverted: at Sinai the people kept their distance from the divine presence; here they are summoned to approach and hear. The Jordan crossing begins with a congregational sermon, not a military order.
3.10: be'zot tede'un ki El chai beqirbekhem — "by this you will know that the living God is among you." El chai — the living God — is a divine title that emphasizes divine vitality and power in contrast to the dead idols of Canaan. The Jordan crossing is designed as a theodicy: the nations of Canaan (vv. 10-11 lists seven) will see that the God who acts is the living God, not a regional deity with limited power.
3.11: hineh aron berit Adon kol ha'arets — "the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth." Adon kol ha'arets — Lord of all the earth — is a title that asserts YHWH's universal sovereignty over the land about to be entered. The Canaanite claim to the land is implicitly invalidated: the one who owns all the earth is disposing of a portion of his own property according to his own covenant purposes.
3.12: ve'attah qechu lakhem shnei asar anashim — "now take for yourselves twelve men, one from each tribe." The twelve men are appointed before the crossing, their purpose initially unstated. The number twelve (one per tribe) signals a representatively complete act: what these men do will be on behalf of all Israel. Their specific task (taking stones) is explained after the crossing in ch. 4.
3.13: veha'ya kenuach kapot raglei hakohanim nosay aron YHWH Adon kol ha'arets bemei hayarden — "when the feet of the priests who carry the ark of the LORD of all the earth rest in the waters of the Jordan." The verb nuach (to rest, plant) is the same word used for Elijah's spirit resting on Elisha and for the Spirit resting on the Messiah (Isa 11:2). The feet of the ark-bearers resting in the water triggers the cutting off of the upstream flow: yikratun (they will be cut off) from krt — the same word for covenant-making (karat berit), covenant-cutting.
3.14: vayyehi binso'a ha'am me'oholeihem la'avor et hayarden — "when the people broke camp to cross the Jordan." The waw-consecutive narration accelerates: the people break camp, the priests carry the ark ahead, they approach the Jordan. The participial phrase vehakohanim nosim et ha'aron berit lifnei ha'am (the priests carrying the ark of the covenant before the people) emphasizes the spatial arrangement throughout the action.
3.15: keha'aron kanahalei ha'ayovim — "as those who carry the ark came to the Jordan and the feet of the priests carrying the ark were dipped (nitbelu) in the edge of the water." The Jordan was in flood (male al gevotav kol yemei qatsir — full to its banks all the days of harvest). The harvest-flood detail is precise: late March/April barley harvest is when the Jordan swells from snowmelt in Hermon. The priests enter at the most dangerous moment, when the river is highest. Faith is maximally tested before it is maximally confirmed.
3.16: vayaamedu hamayim hayordim — "the waters coming down stood." The verb amad (stand, stop) is the same word used for taking a stand in battle. The waters, which had been flowing, take their stand — a reversal of nature. Nema rabba meod ad Adam ha'ir asher mitslad Tsaretan — they piled up in a heap at Adam, a town near Zarethan. The water-wall at Adam is geographically specific: about 16-17 miles upstream from the crossing point, near the confluence with the Jabbok. The downstream waters tammu nikhratu — were completely cut off.
3.17: vayaamedu hakohanim nosim et aron berit YHWH beyabasha betokh hayarden hakun — "the priests who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of the Jordan." Yakhin (firm/established) — the same root as the name of one of Solomon's temple pillars (1 Kgs 7:21). The dry-ground crossing repeats the Exodus sea-crossing pattern (Exod 14:22, 29); the repetition is structurally deliberate. The new generation entering the land is a new Exodus generation, born of the same divine act.

4.1: vayyehi ka'asher tamu kol hag'oi la'avor et hayarden — "when all the nation had finished crossing the Jordan." The perfect tamu marks completion; the entirety of Israel has crossed. YHWH's instruction now follows the event, not before — the twelve men were appointed before the crossing (3:12) but their task is disclosed here. The divine speech pattern is commissioning-then-explanation.
4.2: qechu lakhem min ha'am shnei asar anashim — the twelve men (one per tribe) appointed in 3:12 now receive their specific task: to take twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan, from the place where the priests' feet stood. The stones come from the sacred space — the dry ground created by the divine act. They carry the site's holiness into the camp as perpetual memorial.
4.3: me'atsem asher amadu sham raglei hakohanim hekhinu shenem asar avanim — "from the very place where the priests' feet stood." The phrase etsem (bone/very substance) emphasizes the precise location: not near the spot but from the exact dry ground where the ark-bearers stood. The stones are material testimony to the precise location of the miracle.
4.4: Joshua called the twelve pre-appointed men and gave them the stone-gathering command. The sequence demonstrates Joshua's administrative competence: he pre-appointed the men before the crisis, disclosed their task at the right moment, and directed the operation efficiently. This is narrative evidence of the divine wisdom YHWH promised to give him (1:7-8).
4.5: ivru lifnei aron YHWH Eloheikhem el tokh hayarden — "cross before the ark of the LORD your God into the middle of the Jordan." The twelve men must return to the dry ground of the still-standing miracle to gather their stones. The act of going back into the riverbed requires faith — the waters are held by divine power, and gathering the stones means being present in the middle of what would normally be a flooded river.
4.6: lema'an tihyeh zot ot beqirbekhem — "so that this may be a sign among you." Ot (sign/signal) is the covenant term for a memorial act or object: circumcision is an ot, the rainbow is an ot, the Sabbath is an ot. The stone pile is designed for the question it will provoke: mah ha'avanim ha'elleh lakhem — "what are these stones to you?" A teaching-sign that requires a question to activate.
4.7: vehigadtem lahem asher nikhretu mei hayarden mipnei aron berit YHWH — "tell them that the Jordan's waters were cut off before the ark of the covenant of the LORD." Nikhretu (niphal of krt) — were cut off — repeats the 3:13 verb. The cutting-off language is the covenantal verb used throughout: covenant cutting (karat berit) and water-cutting are lexically linked. The stones tell the covenant story.
4.8: The people obeyed; twelve stones from the Jordan's middle were brought to the camp at Gilgal. ka'asher tsivah Yehoshua — "as Joshua commanded" — the narration confirms exact compliance. The memorial-building is communal: twelve men, twelve tribes, twelve stones — a full-quorum act of covenant memory.
4.9: veshetem asar avanim hechin Yehoshua betokh hayarden — "Joshua also set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan." A second stone monument, separate from the Gilgal one: this one remains submerged, visible only at low water. The doubling of memorials means the miracle is commemorated both at the destination (Gilgal) and at the site itself (the river bottom). The verb hechin (from kwn, to establish/fix) is the same root as yakhin — established, firm.
4.10: vehakohanim nosim et ha'aron amdu betokh hayarden — "the priests who carried the ark stood in the midst of the Jordan." The priests stood until everything YHWH had commanded was complete — the stones taken, the people across. ka'asher tsivah Mosheh et Yehoshua — "as Moses had commanded Joshua" — Moses's preparatory instructions (Deut 27:2-8) are being fulfilled through Joshua. The chain of command (YHWH → Moses → Joshua) is maintained even after Moses's death.
4.11: vayyaavar aron YHWH vehakohanim lifnei ha'am — "the ark of the LORD and the priests crossed over before the people." The ark's crossing happens after all the people have crossed — the priests who entered first leave last. The ark's rear-guard position on exit mirrors its vanguard position on entry: YHWH surrounds the people front and back in the transition. The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe (v.12) cross armed — military readiness accompanies the sacral procession.
4.12: vayyaavru vnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachatsi shevet haMenasheh chamushim lifnei bnei Yisrael — the Transjordanian tribes cross armed (chamushim = equipped in battle array, or possibly by fifths — the etymology is debated). They fulfill the covenant solidarity Joshua required in 1:12-15: their own land is secured but they march with their brothers. The promise has been honored.
4.13: kearba'im eleph chalutsei hatsava averu lifnei YHWH lamishpat learvot Yericho — "about forty thousand armed for battle crossed before the LORD to the plains of Jericho." The number forty thousand corresponds roughly to the Transjordanian tribes' fighting force (Num 26:7, 18, 34). lifnei YHWH — before the LORD — the crossing is a liturgical act as well as a military one; the ark's presence means crossing before YHWH himself.
4.14: bayyom hahu giddel YHWH et Yehoshua be'einei kol Yisrael — "that day the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel." The promise of 3:7 (achel gedallekha) is now fulfilled. Vayyire'u oto ka'asher yare'u et Moshe kol yemei chayav — they revered him as they had revered Moses all the days of his life. The reverence-transfer is complete: Joshua has Moses's authority because he has Moses's God. The measure of leadership is the divine presence, not personal charisma.
4.15: Brief divine speech: vaydabber YHWH el Yehoshua — "the LORD spoke to Joshua." The speech pattern throughout Joshua 1-5 is consistent: YHWH commands, Joshua transmits, the people obey. The divine speech has been ongoing: 1:1, 3:7, 4:1, and now 4:15. The ongoing divine address to Joshua is the authorization behind every act.
4.16: tsav et hakohanim nosay aron ha'edut — "command the priests who carry the ark of the testimony." Ha'edut (testimony) is the term for the law tablets inside the ark (Exod 25:16, 21) — the ark is the container of the covenantal testimony. The priests carry both the visible ark and the invisible law it contains.
4.17: vayyetsav Yehoshua et hakohanim lemor alu min hayarden — "Joshua commanded the priests, 'Come up out of the Jordan.'" The terse command mirrors the priestly entry (3:8: stand at the edge). The same obedience that produced the miracle now triggers its reversal: the priests' exit is as the priests' entry — the miracle brackets their obedience.
4.18: vayyehi ke'alot hakohanim nosay aron berit YHWH mitokh hayarden — "as the priests who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD came up out of the Jordan." Nitteku (niphal of ntq) — the waters were loosed, restored to their place. The restoration is instantaneous: the moment the priests' feet leave the water, the river returns to flood stage. The miracle has no inertia — it exists exactly as long as divine power sustains it and no longer.
4.19: veha'am alu min hayarden be'asor lachdesh harishon — "the people came up from the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month." The date is precise: Nisan 10 — the day the Passover lamb was selected (Exod 12:3). The land-entry is timed to the Passover preparation. Vayachanu baGilgal biqtseh mizrach Yericho — they camped at Gilgal on the east border of Jericho. Gilgal becomes the base camp for the conquest.
4.20: ve'et shetem asar ha'avanim ha'elleh asher lachu min hayarden hechin Yehoshua baGilgal — "Joshua set up at Gilgal the twelve stones they had taken out of the Jordan." The Gilgal monument is the visible, permanent reminder in the camp. The verb hechin (he established) echoes the earlier use: both the underwater monument (v.9) and the Gilgal monument are hekhino — firmly placed, established. The dual memorials are twin witnesses to the same event.
4.21: ma ha'avanim ha'elleh — "what are these stones?" The anticipated question and the prescribed answer follow. The teaching-sign functions pedagogy: the monument raises a question the next generation will ask, creating a moment of intergenerational transmission of covenant history. The parent's answer is not a theological abstraction but a historical narrative — "Israel crossed the Jordan here on dry ground."
4.22: hodi'u et bneikhem lemor beyabasha avar Yisrael et hayarden hazeh — "tell your children that Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground." The historical narration is the catechetical content: not a doctrine but an event. Bayabasha — on dry ground — repeats the key miracle-word from 3:17. The dryness of the ground in the midst of a flooded river is the point to be communicated.
4.23: ki hovish YHWH Eloheikhem et mei hayarden mipneikhem — "for the LORD your God dried up the waters of the Jordan before you." The explicit comparison follows: ka'asher asah YHWH Eloheikhem leyam suph asher hovish mippaneinu — as YHWH your God did to the Red Sea. The Jordan crossing is a second Exodus, and the lesson is the same: YHWH acts in history for his people by controlling the waters.
4.24: lema'an da'at kol amei ha'arets et yad YHWH ki chazaqah hi — "so that all the peoples of the earth may know that the hand of the LORD is powerful (chazaqah)." The adjective chazaqah (strong, mighty) echoes the repeated command of ch. 1 — hazaq we'emats. YHWH's strength, not Joshua's, is the ground of Israel's courage. Ulema'an yire'item et YHWH Eloheikhem kol hayyamim — "and so that you may fear the LORD your God at all times." Two audiences, one miracle: the nations learn YHWH's power; Israel learns reverence. The Jordan crossing is an act of universal theodicy.

5.1: vayyehi kishmo'a kol malkei ha'Emori asher be'ever hayarden yammah vekhol malkei hakeana'an asher al hayam et asher hovish YHWH et mei hayarden mipnei vnei Yisrael — the news of the Jordan crossing reaches the Canaanite and Amorite kings. Vayyimmas levavam — "their hearts melted" — mamas (the verb also used in 2:11). Velo hayah vahem ruach od mipnei vnei Yisrael — "there was no spirit left in them because of the sons of Israel." Rahab's description of Canaanite morale (2:9-11) is confirmed as the condition of all the kings — the divine terror has preceded Israel's army.
5.2: ba'et hahee amar YHWH el Yehoshua asah lekha charvot tsurim — "at that time the LORD said to Joshua: make flint knives." Charvot tsurim — flint (literally "rock") knives. The use of flint rather than metal may reflect the archaic character of the circumcision rite (cf. Zipporah in Exod 4:25 using a flint). Vemuol shuv et bnei Yisrael shenit — "circumcise the sons of Israel again, a second time." The phrasing is unusual: not a second circumcision of individuals but a circumcision of the next generation, which is in this sense a second national circumcision.
5.3: Giv'at ha'aralot — "the hill of foreskins." The place name is etiology: the site is named for the rite performed there. Flint-knife circumcision at the threshold of the conquest is both a covenant renewal and a commissioning: Israel enters battle as a circumcised (covenanted) people. The mass circumcision at Gilgal before any military action makes the point that covenant identity precedes military effectiveness.
5.4: vezeh hadavar asher mal Yehoshua — "and this is the reason why Joshua circumcised them." The explanatory pericope of vv. 4-7 is an anachronistic aside within the narrative. All the men who came out of Egypt and had been circumcised had died in the wilderness — the entire Sinai generation. The wilderness wandering had suspended circumcision practice for the second generation.
5.5: kol ha'am hayotsei nimolu vekhol ha'am hayyullud bamidbar baderekh betzetam mimitsrayim lo malu — "all the people who came out had been circumcised, but all the people born in the wilderness on the journey had not been circumcised." The legal and covenantal problem is stated precisely: there is a generation of Israelite men who bear the identity of Israel but not its sign. The conquest cannot proceed with an uncircumcised army — covenant integrity must precede covenant action.
5.6: ki arba'im shanah halekhu vnei Yisrael bamidbar — "the sons of Israel walked in the wilderness forty years." The reason for the wilderness-period suspension of circumcision is the judgment of 14:29-34 — those men would die in the wilderness; their sons would enter the land. Asher lo sheme'u beqol YHWH — "who did not listen to the voice of the LORD" — the parental disobedience caused the children's covenantal gap. The forty years is the measure of the judgment on unbelief.
5.7: ve'et bnehem heqim tachtam otam mal Yehoshua ki arelim hayu — "and their children he circumcised in their place, for they were uncircumcised." Arelim (uncircumcised) in Hebrew has a range: literally foreskinned, but metaphorically it describes anything closed, blocked, or unresponsive — uncircumcised lips (Exod 6:12), uncircumcised heart (Jer 9:26). The mass circumcision at Gilgal removes the literal condition; the prophets call for the heart-circumcision that the literal rite signifies (Deut 10:16; Jer 4:4; cf. Col 2:11).
5.8: vayyihyu veshivtam tachtam bamachaneh ad cheyotam — "they remained in camp until they had healed." Chayotam from chayah — to live, recover, be restored to health. The verb carries the connotation of reviving. The period of healing in the camp is one of Israel's few moments of military vulnerability in the conquest narrative — no fighting while circumcised and recovering — an act of faith that God would protect them during their incapacity.
5.9: vayyomer YHWH el Yehoshua hayyom galoti et cherpat Mitsrayim me'aleikhem — "today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you." The paronomasia is explicit: galoti (I have rolled) gives the name Gilgal (rolling/wheel). The cherpa (reproach/shame) of Egypt is the condition of being an uncircumcised, landless, slave people — now removed. In the cultural context of the ancient Near East, the lack of a settled inheritance was a source of shame; the land-entry removes it.
5.10: vayyachanu vnei Yisrael baGilgal vaya'asu et haPesach be'arba'ah asar yom lachodesh — Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month, in the plains of Jericho. The timing is covenant-precise: the first Passover in the land, forty years after the first Passover in Egypt. The dates bracket the wilderness period: Passover exits Egypt, Passover enters Canaan. The manna-to-produce transition follows (vv. 11-12), completing the structural reversal from Exodus.
5.11: vayyokelu me'avur ha'arets mimochorat haPesach matsot uqali — "the day after the Passover they ate unleavened bread and roasted grain from the produce of the land." Me'avur ha'arets — from the produce/yield of the land. The word avur (produce) is connected to avor (to cross over) — they eat the fruit of the land they have just crossed into. The transition from manna to land-produce marks the completion of the wilderness phase.
5.12: vayishbot haman mimochorat — "the manna ceased the next day." Shavat — stopped/rested — the same verbal root as Shabbat. The manna's cessation is its Sabbath: having served its purpose for forty years, it rests. Ve'od lo hayah livnei Yisrael man vayyokelu mitvu'at erets Kena'an — "there was no more manna for the sons of Israel; they ate from the produce of the land of Canaan." Wilderness sustenance gives way to land sustenance; the grace of the desert becomes the grace of the promised inheritance.
5.13: vayyehi bihyot Yehoshua biYricho vayissa einav vayar vehineh ish omed lenegdo vecherbo shelufah beyado — "there was a man standing before him with his drawn sword in his hand." The drawn sword (cherbo shelufah) is the posture of a warrior ready for combat — neither sheathed nor used. The ambiguity of his stance (neither attacking nor retreating) frames Joshua's question: "Are you for us or against us?" The question assumes the man is human; the answer reveals he is not.
5.14: vayyomer lo ani sar-tseva YHWH atta vati — "as commander of the army of the LORD I have now come." Sar-tseva YHWH — commander of YHWH's army. The divine army (tseva) is the heavenly host; this is not an Israelite officer but the leader of the angelic armies. His "I have come" (atta vati) is an arrival announcement, not a recruitment pledge — he does not join Israel's mission; Israel joins his. Vayipol Yehoshua el panav artsah vayishtachu — Joshua fell facedown and worshiped, recognizing the divine character of the encounter.
5.15: shal na'alekha me'al raglekha ki hamaqom asher atta omed alav qadosh hu — "take off your sandal from your foot, for the place where you are standing is holy." The command directly echoes Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:5) — word for word. The holiness of the ground (qadosh) is the ground's proximity to divine presence. Vaya'as Yehoshua ken — "Joshua did so." The terse compliance mirrors Moses's response; Joshua is being consciously invested with Moses's role. The one who stood at the burning bush is now standing before Joshua at the threshold of conquest — the same divine presence, the same demand of reverent awe.

9.1: The coalition of Canaanite kings is formulaic — wayĕhî kišĕmōaʿ ('when they heard') introduces a chain of reactions to Israel's advance. The geographic sweep ('hill country, western foothills, and along the entire coast') frames the total political landscape Israel now faces. The verb wayyiqqāhălû ('gathered/assembled') carries the sense of formal military muster, not spontaneous assembly.
9.2: ləlāḥam ʿim-yĕhôšuaʿ wĕʿim-yiśrāʾēl yāḥdāw — 'to fight with Joshua and with Israel as one.' The adverb yāḥdāw (as one, together) signals the unprecedented solidarity of the Canaanite response; seven distinct peoples now act as a single force. The narrative's tension: will this coalition, unlike the Gibeonites, choose war over treaty?
9.3: The Gibeonites receive the same report the kings did (wayišmĕʿû) but respond differently. The contrast is deliberate: hearing the same news, one group forms a war coalition, another seeks survival through treaty. ʿāśû gam-hēmmāh — 'they also did' — introduces the alternative response with a particle of ironic echo.
9.4: wayyishtaddĕlû — they acted, provisioned themselves cunningly. The key term is ʿormāh (v. 4's cognate ʿārûm), meaning cunning or craftiness. The same root describes the serpent in Gen 3:1 (ʿārûm); in Proverbs, cunning (ʿormāh) can be a virtue (Prov 8:5; 12:23) — wisdom applied strategically — but here it is deployed deceptively. The Gibeonites are clever; whether their cleverness is condemned depends on what Israel should have done at v.14.
9.5: The staging of deception is precise: worn sandals, patched, dry crumbling bread — each detail is verifiable at a glance. niqqudîm ('crumbly' or 'spotted' bread) is used only here and in 1 Sam 2:36 in a different sense; the bread's state signals great age. The Gibeonites have thought through every element of a convincing disguise.
9.6: They come to Joshua at Gilgal — Israel's base camp throughout the initial conquest phase (Josh 4:19; 5:10; 10:6). ʾanašîm mērāḥôq bāʾnû — 'men from a distant country we have come.' Their opening claim is the lie around which the entire chapter turns; the Hebrew word order puts 'from a distance' first, fronted for emphasis.
9.7: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾîš-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-haḥiwwî — 'the men of Israel said to the Hivite.' The Hivites are specifically named — the narrative is already giving the reader information the Israelite leaders lack. Israel's question — 'perhaps you live among us' — reflects awareness of the ḥērem command: the nearby peoples must be destroyed (Deut 7:2; 20:17); treaties with them are forbidden (Deut 7:2). The question is therefore legally crucial, not merely polite.
9.8: ʿăbādêkā ʾănaḥnû — 'your servants we are.' The idiom is diplomatic submission language, placing themselves under Joshua's protection. The question 'who are you and where do you come from?' follows diplomatic protocol for treaty negotiation. The Gibeonites' repeated use of 'servant' (ʿebed) throughout the chapter eventually shapes their permanent status at v.23.
9.9: The Gibeonites claim they came 'because of the name of YHWH your God' — a remarkable confession from Canaanites. They cite Sihon and Og (Num 21) but notably stop before mentioning Jericho and Ai — recent victories they had certainly heard about (v.3, wayišmĕʿû). This selective knowledge claim is part of the deception; they let Israel believe the Gibeonites are too remote to know the latest news.
9.10: The Gibeonites claim knowledge only of the trans-Jordan victories (Sihon and Og), implicitly denying knowledge of the Cisjordan campaigns. This is calculated omission — they knew (v.3) but pretend ignorance. ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕšĕnê malkê hāʾĕmōrî — 'what he did to the two kings of the Amorites': Sihon and Og were paradigmatic proof of YHWH's power (cited repeatedly in Deut and Ps 135-136), the minimum a distant nation might believably know.
9.11: The Gibeonites claim their elders and all inhabitants sent them. kol-yōšĕbê ʾarṣênû — 'all the inhabitants of our land' — they present themselves as official envoys, not individuals. The appeal to 'elders' (zĕqēnîm) establishes civic legitimacy, making the treaty a formal treaty with a people, not an ad hoc agreement.
9.12: The theatrical evidence: zeh laḥmênû ḥam hillāʾnûhû — 'this bread of ours was hot [when] we took it.' They present the physical props of deception, inviting Israel to examine the evidence and draw the wrong conclusion. The detail that the bread was 'our provision' (ṣēdāh) frames this as journey-supply, making the claim of distant origin seem verifiable.
9.13: Wineskins cracked from the journey, sandals worn out — the deception complete. The irony is that Israel is about to make a covenant based on evidence they handled but did not think to question spiritually (v.14). The Gibeonites provide exactly enough physical 'proof' to forestall skepticism.
9.14: wayyiqĕḥû hāʾănāšîm miṣṣēdām wĕʾet-pî YHWH lōʾ šāʾālû — 'the men took from their provisions and the mouth of YHWH they did not ask.' This is the narrative's hinge. šāʾal ʾet-pî YHWH ('ask the mouth of YHWH') is the idiom for oracular inquiry via the high priest's Urim and Thummim (Num 27:21; 1 Sam 23:2). The phrase lōʾ šāʾālû ('they did not ask') indicts the leaders directly — they examined the physical evidence but skipped the theological check. The same verb root is embedded in the name 'Saul' (Šāʾûl, 'asked-for'), whose career is defined by the same failure.
9.15: wayyikrōt lāhem yĕhôšuaʿ šālôm wayyikrōt lāhem bĕrît — 'Joshua cut peace with them and cut a covenant.' Both verbs are kārat (cut), the standard covenant-making idiom ('cut a covenant'), heightened here by double use. Once sworn before YHWH (wayyiššābĕʿû lāhem nĕśîʾê hāʿēdāh), the oath is binding — the leaders of the congregation have staked their word before God. The rest of the narrative turns on whether an oath made under deception remains binding; the answer is consistently yes (v.19-20).
9.16: miqṣēh šĕlōšet yāmîm — 'at the end of three days.' Three days is significant: not immediately (which would excuse a hasty treaty), not years later, but a short enough interval that the leaders cannot claim ignorance was unavoidable. The cities are named: Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, Kiriath-jearim — all in the territory of Benjamin, close to Jerusalem. The deceivers were neighbours.
9.17: The cities are Hivite (ḥiwwî), specifically listed, all within the central hill country. Their proximity to Jerusalem (Gibeon is ~9 km north) explains why their survival created long-term political tensions: these were not distant peoples but embedded enclaves in Israelite territory. The Benjaminite towns Beeroth and Kiriath-jearim figure in later narratives (the ark's resting places, 1 Sam 7:1; 2 Sam 6:2).
9.18: wayyillōnû kol-hāʿēdāh ʿal-hannĕśîʾîm — 'all the congregation grumbled against the leaders.' The verb lûn/lîn (grumble, murmur) is the characteristic word for Israel's wilderness rebellion (Exod 15:24; 16:2; Num 14:2). The community's first instinct is violence — 'strike them down' — and the leaders' restraint preserves the oath's integrity over popular sentiment.
9.19: nišbaʿnû lāhem bYHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'we have sworn to them by YHWH the God of Israel.' The oath sworn in YHWH's name cannot be rescinded even though it was obtained by deception; violating it would be profaning YHWH's name. This becomes the foundation for Saul's later violation and the three-year famine (2 Sam 21:1-2): YHWH holds Israel to an oath sworn in his name regardless of how it was obtained.
9.20: The formula wĕlōʾ yihyeh ʿālênû qeṣep — 'lest wrath come upon us' — frames oath-violation as a theological risk: YHWH's qeṣep (wrath, burning anger) would fall on Israel, not the Gibeonites, if Israel broke the oath. The leaders understand the covenant structure: once sworn before YHWH, the commitment runs toward God, not just toward the Gibeonites.
9.21: yihyû ḥōṭĕbê ʿēṣîm wĕšōʾăbê mayim lĕkol-hāʿēdāh — 'they will be woodcutters and water-carriers for all the congregation.' The permanent servile role preserves life within the covenant community. The phrase reappears at v.23 and v.27, where it is given a cultic dimension: service 'for the house of my God.' The Gibeonites are incorporated as a distinct service class — protected but not assimilated as equals.
9.22: Joshua's question is direct: lāmmāh rimmîtĕm ʾōtānû — 'why did you deceive us?' The verb rāmāh (deceive, betray) is strong, used of planned deception (Ps 78:57; Lam 1:19). Joshua does not nullify the treaty but demands an accounting; the covenant stands, its terms redrawn.
9.23: wĕʿattāh ʾărûrîm ʾattem — 'and now cursed are you.' The ʾārûr formula is a formal covenant curse, not a personal imprecation. Yet the curse is immediately filled with specific content: perpetual servitude as woodcutters and water-carriers for 'the house of my God.' The curse is constructive — it assigns a permanent role within Israel's worship life. The Gibeonites are cursed into service, not cursed into destruction.
9.24: The Gibeonites' confession is remarkably candid: kî hoggēd hoggad lāʿăbādêkā ʾēt ʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet-mōšeh ʾabdô — 'because it was told to your servants what YHWH your God commanded his servant Moses.' They acted in self-preservation, knowing the ḥērem applied to them. Their fear was based on accurate theological knowledge: 'we were very afraid for our lives.'
9.25: wĕʿattāh hinnênû bĕyādekā kattôb wĕhayyāšār bĕʿênêkā laʿăśôt lānû ʿăśēh — 'now we are in your hand; do to us as it is good and right in your eyes.' Total submission. The phrase kattôb wĕhayyāšār ('good and right') is a covenant-loyalty formula, appealing to Joshua's integrity. They are throwing themselves on his character.
9.26: Joshua rescues them (wayyaṣṣēl ʾōtām) — the same verb used of divine rescue throughout the OT. The irony: the Gibeonites deceived their way into the covenant, yet the covenant's protection functions exactly as promised — Israel cannot harm them, and Joshua actively defends them. In ch.10, this protection obligation will require a costly all-night military march.
9.27: wayyittēn ʾōtām yĕhôšuaʿ bayyôm hahûʾ ḥōṭĕbê ʿēṣîm wĕšōʾăbê mayim lāʿēdāh wĕlĕmizbaḥ YHWH — assigned for 'the congregation and for the altar of YHWH, at whatever place he should choose.' The phrase ʾel-hamāqôm ʾašer yibḥar ('the place he will choose') is Deuteronomy's formula for the central sanctuary (Deut 12:5,11,14). The Gibeonites' future is tied to the future of Israelite worship — wherever the ark goes, they serve. This explains their presence at Nob (1 Sam 22:19) and their later connection to Gibeon's high place (1 Kgs 3:4).

10.1: Adonizedek ('my lord is righteous') king of Jerusalem responds to Gibeon's treaty. kî lāqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-hāgibʿôn wayyaḥărîmehā — the king conflates Jericho's destruction with Gibeon's capitulation; from Jerusalem's perspective, both represent existential threats. The name Adonizedek echoes Melchizedek ('king of righteousness') from Gen 14:18 — a Jerusalem king whose name invokes righteousness now stands opposed to Israel.
10.2: Gibeon is described as 'like one of the royal cities, greater than Ai' — geographic and strategic context. hî ʿîr gĕdôlāh kĕʾaḥat hāʿārîm hammamlākāh: Gibeon's size and political status made its defection to Israel a seismic event. All its fighting men (kol-ʾănāšêhā gibbōrîm) had been Israel's potential adversaries; now they are treaty partners. Jerusalem's alarm is geopolitical, not merely religious.
10.3: The coalition of five Amorite kings — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon — represents a major southern military alliance. These cities controlled the Shephelah access routes; their cooperation signals coordinated Canaanite resistance to what they recognize as an existential threat. Adonizedek's role as initiator establishes Jerusalem as the political center of the resistance.
10.4: ʿălû ʾēlay wĕʿizerûnî wĕnakkeh ʾet-gibʿôn — 'come up to me and help me, and let us strike Gibeon.' The military verb nākāh (strike down) is the same used of YHWH striking the Egyptians; punishing Gibeon is the coalition's immediate goal, but the deeper target is reversing the breach in the Canaanite defensive line.
10.5: The five kings encamp against Gibeon. The siege is the direct cause of the all-night march in v.9; Gibeon's treaty with Israel now triggers Israel's military obligation. YHWH's sovereignty is working through the coalition's aggression: by attacking Gibeon, they bring Israel's army to their doorstep.
10.6: Gibeon's appeal to Joshua is grounded in the treaty: ʾal-terep yādêkā meʿăbādêkā — 'do not relax your hand from your servants.' The phrase 'your servants' (ʾăbādîm) is the covenant-treaty language of ch.9; they invoke their status to claim Israel's military protection. The appeal is legally correct: the treaty obligates Israel to defend Gibeon.
10.7: wayyaʿal yĕhôšuaʿ min-haggillgāl hûʾ wĕkol-ʿam hammiḥāmāh ʿimmô — 'Joshua went up from Gilgal, he and all the people of war with him.' The entire fighting force moves out; this is not a relief party but a full military campaign. The rapid response signals Joshua's seriousness about treaty obligations — he does not hesitate despite the deception that created the obligation.
10.8: YHWH's direct word of assurance: ʾal-tîrāʾ mēhem kî bĕyādĕkā nĕtattîm — 'do not fear them, for into your hand I have given them.' The perfect tense is prophetic: the victory is treated as already accomplished. This formula ('do not fear; I have given them') is the standard divine war oracle that inaugurates YHWH's battles (Deut 3:2; Josh 6:2; Judg 4:7).
10.9: wayyābōʾ ʾălêhem yĕhôšuaʿ pitʾōm kol-hallaylāh ʿālāh min-haggillgāl — 'Joshua came upon them suddenly, having gone up all night from Gilgal.' The tactical detail is remarkable: a forced night march (~35 km, 1,000m elevation gain) to achieve surprise. pitʾōm ('suddenly') is the key tactical word; surprise was the advantage Israel's smaller force needed. The speed echoes YHWH's mode of operation: he comes suddenly, when not expected.
10.10: wayhummēm YHWH lifnê yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH threw them into confusion before Israel.' Hûm/hāmam is divine-panic vocabulary: the same verb YHWH used at the Red Sea (Exod 14:24) and will use in later battles (1 Sam 7:10). The 'great slaughter' at Gibeon gives way to pursuit; YHWH's confusion rout precedes human sword-work — the victory is his before it is Israel's.
10.11: wayyaṣṣēl YHWH ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt min-haššāmayim ʿălêhem — 'YHWH rained large stones from heaven upon them.' The hailstones are described as lethal divine artillery; wayyāmutû rabbîm mēhem bĕʾabnê habbārād mēʾašer hēmîtû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl leḥāreb — 'more died from the hailstones than Israel killed with the sword.' The divine contribution exceeds the human. The same combination — human army plus divine bombardment — recurs in the eschatological battles of Revelation (Rev 16:21: 100-lb hailstones).
10.12: ʾāz yĕdabbēr yĕhôšuaʿ lYHWH — 'then Joshua spoke to YHWH.' What follows (v.12b-13) is a poetic quotation, set apart from the narrative. The address is to the sun and moon directly: šemeš bĕgibʿôn dôm wĕyārēaḥ bĕʿēmeq ʾayyālôn — 'Sun, in Gibeon, be silent/still; and moon, in the Valley of Aijalon.' The verb dôm (be still, be silent) is used of hushed awe before God (Ps 37:7; Hab 2:20); it may carry a sense of the cosmic realm falling silent before YHWH's battle-action, not merely of mechanical pause.
10.13: wayyiddōm haššemeš wĕyārēaḥ ʿāmād — 'the sun stood still and the moon stopped.' The Hebrew verbs dāmam (be still) and ʿāmad (stood, halted) both describe cessation of motion. The duration: kĕyôm tāmîm — 'like a complete/perfect day.' The Book of Jashar (sēper hayyāšār, 'Book of the Upright') is cited as the source, a now-lost anthology also referenced in 2 Sam 1:18. The narrator invokes an external corroborating source for the most extraordinary claim in the chapter.
10.14: wĕlōʾ hāyāh kayyôm hahûʾ lĕpānāyw wĕʾaḥărāyw lišmōaʿ YHWH bĕqôl ʾîš — 'there was no day like it before or since, in YHWH listening to the voice of a man.' The superlative formula ('never before or since') marks a unique event. The theological point: YHWH listened to a human voice and acted cosmically. kî YHWH nilḥam lĕyiśrāʾēl — 'for YHWH fought for Israel' — the divine warrior formula; this is YHWH's battle before it is Joshua's.
10.15: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô ʾel-hammaḥăneh haggillgālāh — 'Joshua returned, all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.' Text critics note this verse seems out of place — the five kings are still hiding (v.16-17) and the southern campaign continues through v.43. Some manuscripts (LXX) omit it here. The verse may be a dislocation or an editorial notice marking the end of the poetic insert.
10.16: The five kings flee and hide in a cave at Makkedah. wayyimāsʾû nistarim bammĕʿārāh bĕmakkēdāh — hiding in a cave mirrors the imagery of enemies cowering before YHWH's advance (Isa 2:19: 'men will enter caves... before the terror of YHWH'). Their capture and execution will become a teaching moment for Israel's commanders.
10.17: The cave is found and reported to Joshua. The rapid intelligence (wayyuggad lĕyĕhôšuaʿ lēʾmōr) and Joshua's immediate response demonstrate coordinated campaign management; the main pursuit continues while the cave is secured — priorities correctly ordered.
10.18: gōllû ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ʾel-pî hamĕʿārāh — 'roll large stones to the mouth of the cave.' The stones serve dual purpose: prevent escape and mark the spot. The language of rolling stones into the mouth of a cave — sealing royalty inside — echoes in reverse in Gospel resurrection narratives (Mark 16:3; Matt 27:60).
10.19: wĕʾattem ʾal-taʿamĕdû raddĕpû ʾaḥărê ʾōyĕbêkem — 'you, do not stand still; pursue after your enemies.' The imperative rādap (pursue) is urgent; half-measures in battle allow regroupment. The theological principle is embedded in the tactical instruction: YHWH has given them; do not leave the work half-done.
10.20: The slaughter is described as ʿad-tummām mĕʾōd — 'very great, until they were finished/consumed.' The remnant who survived reached their fortified cities. The two outcomes (total annihilation of those caught in the open; survival of those who reached fortified cities) explain why the south still required the mopping-up campaign of vv.28-43.
10.21: wayāšubû kol-hāʿām ʾel-hammaḥăneh ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ bĕmakkēdāh bĕšālôm — 'all the people returned safely to Joshua in the camp at Makkedah.' Bĕšālôm ('in peace/wholeness') marks the tactical return; no Israelite casualty is mentioned. lōʾ ḥāraṣ lĕbĕnê yiśrāʾēl lĕʾîš ʾet-lĕšōnô — 'not a man sharpened his tongue against Israel' — an idiom for hostile complaint or threat; the enemies are silenced.
10.22: Joshua commands the kings brought out. pĕtĕḥû ʾet-pî hamĕʿārāh wĕhōṣîʾû ʾēlay ʾet-ḥamēšet hamĕlākîm — 'open the mouth of the cave and bring out to me the five kings.' The verb pāttaḥ ('open the mouth') reverses the sealing of v.18; this is deliberate ceremony, not mere practicality.
10.23: The five kings are named again — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon. The geographic spread (north to south, hill country to Shephelah) encapsulates the southern region. Their naming here prepares for the teaching moment in v.24-25 and the subsequent campaign that conquers their cities individually (vv.28-39).
10.24: wayyiqrāʾ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-kol-ʾîš yiśrāʾēl wayyōʾmer ʾel-qĕṣînê ʾanšê hammiḥāmāh — 'Joshua called all the men of Israel and said to the commanders.' The ritual of placing feet on the necks of defeated kings (śîmû ʾet-raglêkem ʿal-ṣawwĕrê hammĕlākîm) is documented in ANE victory iconography; Egyptian pharaohs and Assyrian kings are shown with feet on prostrate enemies. Paul applies the image to Christ in 1 Cor 15:25 ('until he has put all his enemies under his feet'; cf. Ps 110:1).
10.25: ʾal-tîrāʾû wĕʾal-tēḥāttû ḥizqû wĕʾimṣû — 'do not be afraid and do not be dismayed; be strong and courageous.' This formula appears throughout Joshua (1:6,7,9; 10:25) and is explicitly God's word to Joshua in ch.1 — here Joshua passes it to his commanders. kî-kāzōt yaʿăśeh YHWH lĕkol-ʾōyĕbêkem — 'for this is what YHWH will do to all your enemies' — the military lesson is theological: the subjugated kings are a pledge of future victories, enacted by YHWH.
10.26: wayyakkēm yĕhôšuaʿ ʾaḥărê-kēn wayyĕmîtēm wayyitlēm ʿal ḥāmēšāh ʿēṣîm — 'Joshua struck them, killed them, and hanged them on five trees.' Hanging on trees/poles was post-mortem humiliation (Deut 21:22-23) — it declared the executed person under God's curse (qilĕlat ʾĕlōhîm tālûy, Deut 21:23). Paul quotes this in Gal 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse for us — for it is written, cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.'
10.27: At sunset the bodies are removed and placed in the cave — the same cave that had been their hiding place. wayyaggillû ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ʾal-pî hamĕʿārāh — the stones seal the cave permanently; 'they are there to this day' (ʿad-hayyôm hazzeh) is an etiological formula marking a visible landmark. The cave becomes both grave and memorial.
10.28: The southern mopping-up campaign begins with Makkedah. kĕkōl ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕmelek yĕrîḥô — 'as he did to the king of Jericho.' The Jericho formula becomes the standard comparison for complete destruction (ḥērem); each city's destruction is measured against the paradigmatic first conquest. The refrain structures vv.28-39.
10.29: Israel moves to Libnah. The formulaic repetition ('then Joshua passed and all Israel with him') tracks the campaign's rapid tempo. Libnah will later figure in Isaiah's narrative of Sennacherib's campaign (Isa 37:8).
10.30: Libnah destroyed — king, inhabitants, all. The formula lōʾ hiśʾîr bāh śārîd ('he left no survivor') appears throughout the southern campaign. The LXX occasionally varies this formula; the MT is consistent. The refrain emphasizes totality, not body-count precision.
10.31: Lachish — strategically the most significant Shephelah fortress. Three days of siege (v.32) versus one-day victories elsewhere suggests genuine military resistance. Lachish's later prominence (Jer 34:7; 2 Kgs 14:19; the Lachish Letters) confirms its regional importance. Horam of Gezer comes to aid Lachish and is destroyed — relief forces are as futile as the besieged garrisons.
10.32: wayyitten YHWH ʾet-lākîš bĕyad yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH gave Lachish into Israel's hand.' The divine passive appears at each major victory; the narrative refuses to attribute conquest to military skill alone. The two-day timeline for Lachish (compared to one-day or all-at-once for others) may indicate a more formidable defense requiring full siege tactics.
10.33: Horam king of Gezer comes in relief. wayyak ʾōtô yĕhôšuaʿ wĕʾet-ʿammô ʿad-bilti hišʾîr-lô śārîd — 'Joshua struck him and his people until none remained.' The formula absorbs the relief force into the same annihilation refrain. Gezer itself will not be fully conquered (Josh 16:10; 1 Kgs 9:16) — an anticipation of the incomplete conquest acknowledged in ch.13.
10.34: Eglon taken in one day. The speed contrasts with Lachish's two-day siege; different cities present different levels of resistance. The campaign maintains momentum — no pauses for consolidation that might allow regroupment.
10.35: The Eglon formula: king, every person in it, all (kol-nepeš). Nepeš (soul/person/life) is used as 'every living person' in destruction formulas — it is not a theological claim about eternal souls but the standard Hebrew idiom for 'every inhabitant.'
10.36: Hebron — city of Abraham (Gen 13:18; 23:2), later David's first capital (2 Sam 2:1). Its capture here is temporary; it reverts (Josh 14:12-15; 15:13-14) as Caleb's allotted city. The formulaic destruction here and the later re-engagement with Hebron illustrate how the conquest summary in ch.10-11 is idealized: total victory is asserted even as subsequent chapters show it was partial.
10.37: Hebron: king, all its cities, all persons. The mention of 'all its cities' (kol-ʿārêhā) signals Hebron's regional control. The pattern of total destruction followed by resettlement (and eventual Canaanite persistence) is a recurring tension in the book.
10.38: Debir — mentioned in the conquest summary and later in Caleb's daughter's request (Josh 15:15-17; Judg 1:11-13). The repeated conquest tradition (here in ch.10; again in ch.15 via Othniel) reflects either variant traditions or a reconquest after Canaanite resettlement.
10.39: The Debir formula completes the southern sweep. kĕkōl ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕḥebrôn — 'as he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir.' The southern campaign is complete; each city measured by the same standard of total destruction.
10.40: wayyak yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hāʾāreṣ hāhār wĕhannegeb wĕhašŝĕpēlāh wĕhāʾăšēdôt — 'Joshua struck all the land: the hill country, the Negev, the western foothills, and the slopes.' The geographic summary covers the complete southern zone. kāʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'as YHWH God of Israel commanded' — the obedience formula anchors the destruction in divine mandate, not military ambition.
10.41: From Kadesh-barnea (the boundary of the wilderness) to Gaza (the coast) and from Goshen (not the Egyptian Goshen but a Negev region) to Gibeon — this is the full south-to-north extent of the southern campaign. The geographic markers frame the victory as comprehensive.
10.42: wĕʾet-kol-hamĕlākîm hāʾēlleh wĕʾet-ʾarṣām lāqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ paʿam ʾeḥāt — 'all these kings and their land Joshua took at one time.' Paʿam ʾeḥāt ('one time/one stroke') is emphatic — the rapidity of the campaign is a theological point: YHWH's hand moved swiftly. kî YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl nilḥam lĕyiśrāʾēl — 'because YHWH the God of Israel fought for Israel.' The campaign's summary is theological before it is military.
10.43: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô ʾel-hammaḥăneh haggillgālāh — 'Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.' The return to base closes the southern campaign. Gilgal was Israel's cultic center and base of operations; the return marks the formal end of each campaign phase before the next begins (ch.11's northern campaign).

11.1: Jabin king of Hazor triggers the northern coalition. Hazor is identified in v.10 as 'the head of all those kingdoms' — the dominant city-state of the north, confirmed by archaeology (Tell el-Qedah, the largest Bronze Age site in Canaan, ~200 acres). Jabin's name recurs in Judges 4 — either a dynastic name or a second Jabin after Israel's withdrawal. The name yābîn means 'he understands/discerns'; the name signals intelligence in the ruler, not just martial force.
11.2: The geographic spread — northern hill country, Arabah south of Kinnereth (Sea of Galilee), the western foothills, and the heights of Dor — covers the entire northern Levant. The campaign will be qualitatively different from the south: larger distances, different terrain, and a cavalry-heavy enemy (v.4).
11.3: The coalition includes Hivites (ḥiwwî, here specifically at Mizpah) — the same people group as the Gibeonites. The enumeration of peoples (Canaanites east/west, Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Jebusites) mirrors the standard Deuteronomy list (Deut 7:1) of the seven nations to be dispossessed. The totality of the opposition emphasizes YHWH's total victory.
11.4: wayyēṣĕʾû hēm wĕkol-maḥănêhem ʿimmāhem ʿam rab kaḥôl ʾašer ʿal-śĕpat hayyām lārōb — 'they came out, they and all their forces with them, a vast people, as the sand on the shore of the sea.' The sand simile (kaḥôl ʾašer ʿal-śĕpat hayyām) is the standard biblical idiom for an uncountable multitude (Gen 22:17; Judg 7:12; 1 Sam 13:5). The force includes cavalry (sûs wārekem) and chariots — weaponry Israel lacks and has been told not to desire (v.6).
11.5: Assembly at the Waters of Merom — likely modern Meiron in Upper Galilee, a natural assembly point in the hill country. The coalition chooses defensible terrain; YHWH instructs a surprise attack (v.7) that neutralizes the positional advantage.
11.6: wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ ʾal-tîrāʾ mippĕnêhem — 'YHWH said to Joshua, do not fear them.' The reassurance is necessary because the northern coalition (chariots, horses, vast numbers) represents a military capability far beyond anything Israel has faced. The specific command: ʾet-sûsêhem tĕʿaqqēr wĕʾet-merkĕbōtêhem tiśrōp bāʾēš — 'you shall hamstring their horses and burn their chariots.' This deliberately deprives Israel of captured military technology; they must not build their security on horses and chariots (Ps 20:7; Deut 17:16). The command enforces dependence on YHWH rather than military parity.
11.7: wayyābōʾ yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-ʿam hammiḥāmāh ʿimmô ʾălêhem ʿal-mê mērôm pitʾōm — 'Joshua and all the people of war with him fell upon them at the Waters of Merom suddenly.' Pitʾōm (suddenly) is the same tactical surprise used in ch.10:9. The surprise assault disrupts the enemy's formation before chariots and cavalry can be deployed — YHWH's strategy compensates for Israel's technological disadvantage.
11.8: YHWH gave them into Israel's hand (wayyittēm YHWH bĕyad-yiśrāʾēl). The divine passive precedes the tactical pursuit. The pursuit extends in four directions — Sidon (northwest coast), Misrephoth-maim (northern Phoenicia), the Valley of Mizpah (east) — showing that the coalition scattered in all directions, indicating a rout rather than a fighting retreat.
11.9: wayyaʿas lāhem yĕhôšuaʿ kaʾašer ʾāmar lô YHWH ʾet-sûsêhem ʿiqqēr wĕʾet-merkĕbōtêhem śārap bāʾēš — 'Joshua did to them as YHWH had said: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots.' Exact obedience to the divine command, even when it meant destroying valuable military assets. The verse establishes the pattern for all of ch.11: YHWH commanded → Joshua obeyed completely (v.15: 'he left nothing undone').
11.10: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ bāʿēt hahîʾ wayyilkōd ʾet-ḥāṣôr wayyak ʾet-malkāh beḥāreb — 'Joshua turned at that time, captured Hazor, and struck its king with the sword.' Hazor is identified as rōʾš kol-hammamlākôt hāhēnnāh — 'head of all those kingdoms.' Its special destruction (burning, v.11) sets it apart from other northern cities; it is the northern equivalent of Jericho — the symbolic capital whose fall represents the collapse of the whole region.
11.11: wayyakû ʾet-kol-hannepeš ʾašer-bāh lĕpî-ḥereb haḥărēm ʾōtāh lōʾ niśʾar kol-nĕšāmāh — 'they struck every person in it with the sword, devoting it to destruction; not a single living thing was left.' The ḥērem formula at full intensity; nĕšāmāh (breath/living thing) is used alongside nepeš (person) for emphasis. Hazor alone is burned (wĕʾet-ḥāṣôr śārap bāʾēš) — the same fate as Jericho (Josh 6:24), Ai (8:28), and the chariots (v.9). Archaeological layers at Tell el-Qedah confirm a major destruction in the Late Bronze Age.
11.12: All the cities of the northern kings were taken. wayyak kol-malkām lĕpî-ḥereb haḥărēm ʾōtām — 'he struck all their kings with the sword, devoting them to destruction.' The obedience formula: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh mōšeh ʿebed YHWH — 'as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded.' The Mosaic mandate (Deut 7:1-5; 20:16-18) is the explicit grounding for the ḥērem; Joshua acts under Mosaic authority, not personal initiative.
11.13: Cities standing on their mounds (ʿārîm hāʿōmĕdôt ʿal-tillām) were not burned by Israel — only Hazor among the north is burned. This observation has archaeological significance: it explains why most northern sites show continuity layers rather than destruction layers in the relevant strata. The 'tells' (mound-cities) were preserved for Israelite habitation; only Hazor, as the symbolic capital, was destroyed.
11.14: wĕkol-šĕlal hehārîm hāʾēlleh wĕhabbĕhēmāh bāzĕzû lāhem bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'all the spoil of these cities and the livestock, Israel plundered for themselves.' The distinction is explicit: livestock and goods may be taken, but the ḥērem applies to persons. This matches the Deuteronomic law (Deut 20:14) for distant cities, even though the northern campaign was against cities that fell under the total-destruction command.
11.15: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh ʿabdô kēn ṣiwwāh mōšeh ʾet-yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkēn ʿāśāh yĕhôšuaʿ lōʾ-hēsîr dābār mikkōl ʾašer-ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh — 'as YHWH commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua, and so Joshua did — he left nothing undone of all that YHWH commanded Moses.' This is the book's highest obedience formula: a three-link chain (YHWH→Moses→Joshua) with the explicit affirmation that nothing was omitted. This verse anchors the entire conquest as an act of covenantal obedience, not aggressive expansion.
11.16: The geographic summary of ch.11's conquest echoes the southern summary of ch.10:40-41, now extended north. The full schema: hill country, Negev, Goshen, the lowlands, the Arabah, hill country and foothills of Israel. The idealized totality ('all this land Joshua took') is the summary perspective; chs.13-19 will detail what remains.
11.17: From Mount Halak (southern boundary) to Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley (northern boundary) — the full north-south axis of the Promised Land. The formula kol-malkêhem lāqaḥ wayyakkēm wayyĕmîtēm — 'he took all their kings, struck them, and put them to death' — applies to the entire campaign arc. The comprehensive victory claim is the narrator's theological assessment: YHWH gave what he promised.
11.18: yāmîm rabbîm ʿāśāh yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hamĕlākîm hāʾēlleh miḥāmāh — 'Joshua waged war against all these kings for a long time.' This verse is often overlooked but is crucial: the conquest was not rapid or magical. Yāmîm rabbîm ('many days') indicates years of ongoing warfare — archaeology supports a prolonged process, not a seven-year blitzkrieg. The theological point stands regardless: YHWH's faithfulness does not always mean immediate completion.
11.19: lōʾ hāyĕtāh ʿîr ʾašer hiśslîmāh ʾel-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl biltî haḥiwwî yōšĕbê gibʿôn — 'there was not a city that made peace with Israel, except the Hivites who lived in Gibeon.' The Gibeonite exception is the only successful treaty; every other city chose war and faced destruction. The Gibeonites' survival is explicitly grounded in their unique decision for peace — a note that has canonical weight: the door was open, but only one people walked through it.
11.20: kî mēʾēt YHWH hāyĕtāh lĕḥazzēq ʾet-libbām liqraʾt hammiḥāmāh ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'for it was from YHWH that it was to harden their hearts to meet Israel in war.' The ḥizzēq (harden) construction mirrors Exodus Pharaoh texts precisely: in Exodus, YHWH hardens Pharaoh's heart after Pharaoh first hardens his own; here the hardening is YHWH's initiative applied to the Canaanite kings. The purpose: lemaʿan haḥărîmām — 'in order to devote them to destruction.' The Canaanites' resistance is not presented as unfortunate obstruction but as divinely ordered — they chose war, and YHWH confirmed that choice so the judgment would be complete.
11.21: The Anakites (ʿănāqîm) — the giants who terrified the spies in Num 13:33 ('we seemed like grasshoppers to ourselves'). Their elimination from Israelite territory is presented as the resolution of Numbers' crisis: what the first generation feared and could not conquer, Joshua's generation destroys. The survivors are confined to Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod — Philistine cities. The Goliath of 1 Sam 17 (gat) is the Anakite remnant the next generation will face; David will finish what Joshua started.
11.22: lōʾ-nôtar ʿănāqîm bĕʾereṣ bĕnê yiśrāʾēl raq bĕʿazzāh bĕgat ûbĕʾašdôd niśʾārû — 'no Anakites were left in Israelite territory; only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod did any remain.' The specificity of Gaza, Gath, Ashdod as Philistine cities is prospective: these are not just geographical notes but narrative setup for the Philistine conflicts and Goliath (1 Sam 17:4 — 'a Philistine... from Gath'). The seed of the next crisis is planted in the cleanup statement of the previous victory.
