11.23: wayyiqqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕkōl ʾašer dibber YHWH ʾel-mōšeh — 'Joshua took the entire land, according to all that YHWH had spoken to Moses.' This verse closes both ch.11 and the conquest narrative (chs.1-11). Three affirmations: (1) the land was taken completely; (2) it matches YHWH's word to Moses (Gen 15:18-21 through Deut 34); (3) wayyanhilāh lĕyiśrāʾēl kĕmahălĕqōtām lĕšibṭêhem — 'he gave it as inheritance to Israel according to their tribal divisions.' The word naḥălāh (inheritance, portion, allotment) is the key term of the land theology; it implies permanent gift, not merely military conquest. Wattišqōt hāʾāreṣ mimmilḥāmāh — 'and the land had rest from war.' Šāqaṭ (rest, quietness) is the Deuteronomistic rest-formula (Deut 12:10); the land's rest is the partial fulfilment of the Sabbath-rest promised to Israel, which Hebrews 4:1-11 argues points toward a greater and permanent rest in Christ.

6.1: viYricho sogeret umusegeret: ‘Jericho was shut up and barred’ — the doubled construction (sogeret participle + musegeret passive) is emphatic: utterly closed, impenetrably locked. The city that no human force could open is the very city YHWH declares already given (natatih) in v2 — a divine perfect treating the future as accomplished fact. The gap between the city’s locked state and YHWH’s already-given declaration frames the entire chapter as a lesson in what faith receives that sight cannot yet see.
6.2: reeh natatih beyadecha: ‘See — I have given it into your hand’ — the Hebrew perfect (natatih) is past tense: YHWH speaks of Jericho’s fall as already accomplished. This is the divine perspective from outside time applied to events that Israel must still walk out through seven days of marching. The same tension appears throughout OT and NT: God speaks of the elect as “foreknown,” of justification as already complete, of the resurrection as certain — while the historical unfolding remains ahead. Joshua 6:2 is the OT basis for speaking of future realities in the past tense of divine certainty.
6.3: usbavtem et hair kol anshei hamilchamah: ‘march around the city, all the men of war’ — military language (anshei hamilchamah, men of war) surrounds an operation involving no weapons: walking in circles while priests blow rams’ horns. The deliberate incongruity between military personnel and non-military action signals the point: this is YHWH’s battle, not Israel’s. The armies are present as participants in a divine act, not as agents of the victory. The same principle governs apostolic ministry: “we do not wage war according to the flesh” (2 Cor 10:3), but the weapons are not less powerful for being spiritual.
6.4: shiva kohanim yisu shiva shofarot hayovelim: ‘seven priests shall carry seven rams’ horns’ — the shofar hayovel (Jubilee horn) is distinct from the chatsotsrot (straight silver trumpets of Num 10). The yovel root connects to Jubilee (yovel, Lev 25) — the year of liberty and land-return. Whether intentional or not, the instrument announcing Jericho’s fall is the instrument of liberation; YHWH’s conquest is framed as a Jubilee-act, a reclaiming of the land from its temporary occupants. The sevenfold repetition (seven priests, seven horns, seven days, seven circuits) saturates the narrative with sabbatical completion.
6.5: bimshoch beqeren hayovel bishmaachem et qol hashofar yaria chol haam: ‘when they draw out the blast on the ram’s horn, the whole people shall shout with a great shout’ — the sustained blast (bimshoch, drawing out the sound) precedes the corporate shout (yaria). The mechanics require the people to wait in silence through six days and then coordinate a single simultaneous shout. The obedient synchrony of the people’s shout with the divine signal is the human act corresponding to divine action: the shout does not cause the walls to fall, but the walls fall at the shout.
6.6: Joshua’s direct communication with the priests and army implements YHWH’s command precisely. The detailed delegation — priests carry the ark, priests blow the horns, armed men march before and behind — creates a liturgical procession: ark-centered, priest-mediated, encompassed by armed men carrying no weapons against the city. This is the structure of biblical worship translated into military terms: the holy object at the center, priestly mediation, and the people surrounding it in ordered attention.
6.7: ivru usubvu et hair: ‘advance and march around the city’ — the Hebrew avar (cross over, advance) is the same verb used for Israel’s crossing of the Jordan and for Israel’s entry into the land generally. Every march around Jericho is a small avar — an act of crossing, passage, committed movement. The narrative is full of crossings: Jordan, Jericho, the land. Each individual crossing participates in the larger crossing-dynamic of the entire conquest.
6.8: The priests with their rams’ horns march before the ark, blowing continuously (tamid taqea bataqoa hashofar). The continual sounding is both a sonic claim on the territory — declaring YHWH’s sovereignty by the Jubilee-instrument — and a liturgical frame within which the silent army marches. The silence of the army against the continual priestly music creates an unusual soundscape: worshipful proclamation surrounding military formation. This is not primarily a siege but a liturgy.
6.9: hame'aseph holech acharei haaron: ‘the rear guard came after the ark’ — the ark travels in the middle of the procession, with armed men ahead and a rear guard behind. This is the opposite of a normal military formation where the ark would be guarded at the rear; here the ark leads, and the military presence surrounds it as an escort. The spatial arrangement communicates: YHWH leads the battle; human forces are his retinue.
6.10: lo taria velo tashmiu et qolchem: ‘you shall not shout or make any sound with your voice’ — six days of absolute silence, broken only by priestly horns. The commanded silence is a discipline of waiting: Israel cannot shout until YHWH says to shout. The prohibition on premature noise is a prohibition on premature action — the very temptation Israel succumbed to at Massah and Meribah and at the golden calf. Silence before YHWH is a form of trust that his word and timing are sufficient.
6.11: Joshua executed the command precisely: a single circuit, return to camp, silence. The single-circuit days build expectation without providing discharge. Six days of partial action — enough to establish the pattern but not enough to produce the result. The daily manna, the weekly Sabbath, the annual Passover — all are repetitions building toward a final completion. Jericho’s six days build toward the seventh day’s seven circuits.
6.12: vayashkem Yehoshua baboker: early rising — the same phrase used of Abraham rising early to obey YHWH’s command regarding Isaac (Gen 22:3). Early rising as indicator of readiness and obedience is a minor but consistent motif: the one who has truly received a divine command does not delay its execution. The priests and ark are ready; the process begins again with the same fidelity as day one.
6.13: The repetition of seven priests with seven horns marching and blowing continually establishes the narrative as a liturgical cycle. The reader must inhabit the repetition, not skip past it. Six times the narrative rehearses the march, horn-blowing, silence, and return; the sixth repetition makes the seventh day’s deviation — seven circuits instead of one — structurally explosive. The storyteller uses liturgical repetition to generate narrative momentum.
6.14: One circuit per day, silence, return to camp — the formula exactly repeated. The six-day structure recalls the six days of creation followed by the seventh day of completion. Jericho’s fall on the seventh day after six days of preparation is structurally a Sabbath-pattern: what the preparation builds toward arrives on the day of rest and completion.
6.15: bayom hashvii vayashkimu kaolot hashachar: ‘on the seventh day they rose at the dawning of the day’ — the earliest possible start, the longest possible day. Seven circuits instead of one, beginning at dawn. The seventh day breaks the pattern established by days one through six in every dimension: earlier start, seven circuits, the shout that was forbidden now commanded. The Sabbath is the day when the pattern that seemed locked is broken open.
6.16: bitoqa hashofar yaria hadam: ‘at the trumpet blast, shout!’ — Joshua’s command at the seventh blast of the seventh circuit connects action (shout) to divine signal (horn). The timing matters: not premature, not delayed, but synchronized with the divine signal. The shout is Israel’s act of faith that YHWH’s word has been fulfilled — they shout before the walls fall, trusting that the walls will fall. This is the structure of Heb 11:30: “by faith the walls of Jericho fell down after they were encircled for seven days.”
6.17: tihyeh hair cherem hi vekol asher bah laYHWH: ‘the city shall be a cherem — devoted to YHWH’ — cherem is the chapter’s central theological term. It means something set apart entirely for YHWH, irrevocably consecrated, not for human use. The same root is used of things offered to the sanctuary that cannot be redeemed (Lev 27:28-29). For Jericho, cherem means total destruction: no survivors, no plunder, everything given to YHWH — destroyed (living things) or placed in the sanctuary treasury (precious metals). Jericho as the firstfruits city receives firstfruits treatment: everything holy, nothing retained.
6.18: rak shemertu mihaacherem: ‘but keep away from the devoted things’ — the warning about cherem contamination: taking any cherem object makes the entire camp cherem. The contamination is moral, not physical — the sin of treating what belongs to YHWH as available for personal use. The same principle governs the Lord’s Supper: “whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of profaning the body and blood of the Lord” (1 Cor 11:27) — treating the holy as ordinary brings judgment.
6.19: kol kesef vezahav uklei nechoshet uvarzel qodesh hem laYHWH: ‘all silver and gold and vessels of bronze and iron are sacred (qodesh) to YHWH’ — the precious metals are not destroyed but consecrated; they pass into YHWH’s treasury as firstfruits of the conquest. Consistent with the principle that the first of everything belongs to YHWH (Exod 13:2): Jericho as the first city has its wealth wholly consecrated. Later cities allow plunder; this one does not.
6.20: vayyari ham vayitqeu bashofarot vatipol hachomah tachhteha: ‘the people shouted and the wall fell flat’ — grammatically, the people’s shout and the wall’s fall are juxtaposed but the wall’s fall is in the passive: it fell, was caused to fall. The human action (shout) and the divine action (wall falls) are presented together without causal explanation. The gap between human act and divine result is preserved deliberately: this was YHWH’s act in response to Israel’s faith-act.
6.21: vayacharimu et kol asher bair: ‘they devoted to destruction all that was in the city’ — the execution of cherem against Jericho’s population is among the most difficult passages in the OT. Several observations: (1) The Canaanite nations had been warned through four centuries of continued iniquity (Gen 15:16); (2) the narrative shows the way of escape was open — Rahab’s household is saved; (3) the cherem is explicitly not ethnic but covenantal — the command targets religious contamination, not ethnicity (Deut 20:17-18). The theological issues require sustained engagement with divine character and purpose, not quick harmonization.
6.22: The spies are given their assignment: rescue Rahab as promised. The keeping of the oath given to a prostitute in a city subjected to cherem is itself remarkable: YHWH’s people honor their word even in the chaos of total destruction. The specificity of the rescue (bring out the woman and all who belong to her, just as you swore) demonstrates covenant faithfulness in action: promises made are promises kept, regardless of circumstances.
6.23: vayotziu et Rachav ve et aviha ve et imah ve et acheiha: ‘they brought out Rahab, her father, mother, brothers, and all who belonged to her’ — the household salvation is complete. The promise in 2:18-19 was precisely this: all who are in your house will be saved. The inclusion of the entire household under her faith-act illustrates the household-salvation principle that reappears in Acts: “believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household” (Acts 16:31).
6.24: veha'ir sarefu ba'esh: ‘they burned the city with fire’ — the burning after the cherem is the physical completion of the consecration: what cannot be taken cannot remain. Archaeological evidence at Tell es-Sultan (likely ancient Jericho) includes extensive burning evidence at levels corresponding to the Late Bronze Age, though the exact stratigraphy remains contested. The silver, gold, and metals go to YHWH’s treasury; everything else becomes ash.
6.25: ve et Rachav hazonah ve et beit aviha ve et kol asher lah hecheyah Yehoshua: ‘Joshua spared Rahab the prostitute, her father’s household, and all who belonged to her’ — the explicit mention of her profession (ha-zonah) in the rescue verse is pointed: what determined her salvation was not social respectability but faith-expressing-itself-in-risk. Hebrews credits her by faith (Heb 11:31); James by works (Jas 2:25) — both are correct because her faith was expressed through her works. Matthew places her in the Messiah’s genealogy (Matt 1:5).
6.26: arur ha'ish lifnei YHWH asher yaqum uvana et hair hazot et Yericho: ‘cursed before YHWH is the man who rises up and rebuilds this city Jericho’ — the rebuilding curse extends the cherem: because Jericho was devoted to YHWH, it must not be reclaimed for ordinary use. The specific curse formula (at the cost of his firstborn he lays its foundation, at the cost of his youngest son he sets up its gates) was fulfilled four centuries later: “In his days Hiel of Bethel built Jericho. He laid its foundation at the cost of Abiram his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of his youngest son Segub, according to the word of the LORD that he spoke by Joshua” (1 Kings 16:34).
6.27: vayhi YHWH et Yehoshua vayhi shime'o bechol haAretz: ‘the LORD was with Joshua, and his fame spread throughout the land’ — the divine presence formula used of Moses and of Israel generally. The spread of Joshua’s fame is the reverse of Rahab’s report (2:9-11): the nations heard and their hearts melted; after Jericho, that melting intensifies. The same power that kept YHWH’s promise now makes YHWH’s purpose known even to those who resist it.

7.1: vayim'alu vnei Yisrael ma'al bachareim: ‘the sons of Israel committed a trespass (ma'al) regarding the devoted things’ — ma'al is a technical term for breach of faith specifically regarding sacred things: misappropriating what belongs to YHWH (cf. Lev 5:15). What makes this verse theologically striking is that YHWH’s anger burned against Israel before the military defeat, before any human awareness of the sin. The hidden sin had already compromised the covenant relationship. Sin’s effects are not dependent on its discovery.
7.2: Joshua sends spies to Ai (ha-Ai, the ruin) — the same intelligence-gathering approach as Jericho. The city’s name (Ai = ruin) anticipates its fate: what is already a ruin will be made a permanent ruin after Achan’s sin is dealt with. The scouts recommend a limited force — the same self-confidence that failed at Kadesh (Num 14:40-45) when Israel presumed on divine favor without divine command. Human military assessment without divine consultation is the structural error that Jericho’s victory might have generated as a temptation.
7.3: al yaal sham kol haam: ‘not all the people need to go up’ — reasonable military calculation: why exhaust the whole army against a small target? But the calculation omits the variable that matters: YHWH’s presence is not automatic. The assumption that victory follows from previous victory without consulting YHWH is the error. This is the warning of Deut 32:15 — prosperity can generate the self-reliance that forfeits divine presence.
7.4: vayanusu mipnei anshei haAi: ‘they fled before the men of Ai’ — described with the same vocabulary used of Canaanite fear before Israel (2:11; 5:1). Now the Israelites experience what they produced in their enemies: routed flight, melting hearts. The reversal is exact and painful. Israel defeated without YHWH is no different from any other nation; the divine presence, not ethnic identity or military prowess, was the only thing that made Israel different.
7.5: vayyimas levav haam vayhi lemayim: ‘the hearts of the people melted and became like water’ — this is the exact phrase Rahab used of Jericho (2:11: vayimass levavchem) and Joshua used of Canaanites (2:9). Israel is now experiencing what they inflicted on their enemies. The narrative symmetry is jarring: the people of God, living outside the covenant relationship, are as vulnerable to fear and defeat as the nations they were supposed to drive out.
7.6: vayiqra Yehoshua simlotav vayipol al panav artzah lifnei aron YHWH: ‘Joshua tore his garments and fell facedown before the ark of YHWH’ — mourning rites before the ark are appropriate responses to apparent divine abandonment, and they position Joshua correctly: before YHWH, seeking understanding. The elders pour dust on their heads — intensified mourning. The lament that follows is not faithless complaint but covenant-based questioning: YHWH brought us here; why would he abandon us? This is the form of Ps 44 and Lamentations — bold covenant appeal in the face of apparent divine failure.
7.7: lama he'avarta he'avir et ha'am hazeh et hayarden: ‘why did you bring this people across the Jordan at all?’ — Joshua’s lament echoes the wilderness generation’s complaint (Num 11:15; 14:3: “it would have been better to die in Egypt”). The similarity is deliberate: Joshua the leader, in his despair, sounds like the faithless people he replaced. Crisis produces regression to earlier complaints; even the greatest leaders can momentarily sound like those they lead.
7.8: Joshua’s second lament: what can I say after Israel has turned tail? The concern is for the divine reputation — if Israel is defeated, what will YHWH’s standing among the nations be? This is the same argument Moses used at the golden calf (Exod 32:12). The concern for divine honor in the eyes of the nations is not political spin but theological conviction: YHWH’s name and Israel’s fate are linked — when Israel fails, YHWH’s faithfulness appears to fail with it.
7.9: mah ta'aseh leshimcha hagadol: ‘what will you do for your great name?’ — the appeal to divine self-interest is audacious but theologically precise: YHWH bound himself to Israel by covenant; Israel’s destruction would reflect on the covenant-maker. Moses and Joshua both understand that covenant prayer can appeal to YHWH’s own character and reputation. This is not manipulation but faith in the weight of the covenant bond.
7.10: qum lecha lamah zeh attah nophel al panecha: ‘Get up! Why are you lying on your face?’ — YHWH’s response is brisk: the time for lament is over; the problem has a solution; stop grieving and act. The divine call from lament to action appears in Elijah’s cave encounter (1 Kings 19:5) and in the NT: “awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you” (Eph 5:14). Mourning without moving is not faithfulness.
7.11: Yisrael chata vegam avru et briti asher tziviti otam: ‘Israel has sinned and transgressed my covenant that I commanded them’ — YHWH’s diagnosis is precise: the sin is corporate, it is a specific act (lakchu min hacherem, they took from the devoted things), and it is a covenant transgression. The corporate guilt for one man’s sin raises the theological problem of collective responsibility (cf. Num 16:22: “shall one man sin and you be angry at the whole congregation?”). The community had not built in accountability structures to prevent cherem violation.
7.12: lo tuchal bnei Yisrael laqum lifnei oyveihem: ‘Israel cannot stand against their enemies’ — the connection between covenant sin and military inability is explicit. The cherem that was to be kept outside Israel has come inside it through Achan’s act: Israel itself has become cherem (hayiti lecharaim). Sin makes the holy community its own enemy by importing into itself the thing it was commanded to destroy.
7.13: qum qadesh et haam: ‘arise, consecrate the people’ — YHWH’s first command is preparation: consecrate before the investigation. The holiness-before-process sequence is consistent throughout: Sinai’s consecration before the theophany (Exod 19:10-11), the priests’ purification before ministry. Access to divine process requires preparation of the community.
7.14: veqaravtem laboqer leshivteihem: ‘in the morning you shall be presented by tribes’ — the investigative lot-process narrows from tribe to clan to household to individual. The procedure is meticulous: YHWH does not simply name the culprit but walks Israel through a process that makes the identification certain and impossible to dispute. The lot system is not randomness but divine revelation (Prov 16:33: “the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH”). Every level is exonerated by the process as it narrows.
7.15: venivlah asah beYisrael: ‘he has done folly (nevalah) in Israel’ — nevalah is a strong term typically associated with sexual immorality and sacrilege (Gen 34:7; 2 Sam 13:12). Its application to covenant theft is striking: taking the cherem is morally equivalent to sexual violation of the covenant community. The same category-mixing (sacrilege = violation) reveals how the OT understands the covenant as intimate relationship: breach of its terms is something close to sexual betrayal — hence the prophets’ repeated use of marital infidelity as the metaphor for idolatry.
7.16: The early-morning process begins: tribe by tribe before YHWH. The tribe of Judah is identified — the royal tribe from which the messianic line will come. The identification of the sinner within Judah does not taint the tribe permanently; David will come from Judah, and Achan’s sin does not disqualify Judah from its messianic vocation. Individual sin does not determine tribal or corporate identity eternally.
7.17: vayiqrev et mishpachat Yehudah vayilkod et mishpachat haZarchi: tribe (Judah), clan (Zerah), household (Zabdi), individual (Achan). The Zerah connection is pointed: Zerah was the twin son of Judah and Tamar (Gen 38:30) whose birth was marked by a scarlet thread — echoing Rahab’s scarlet cord. In Judah’s line, scarlet threads mark both salvation (Rahab, Zerah) and now judgment (Achan of Zerah’s clan). The same family connection can carry either promise or condemnation depending on the individual’s faithfulness.
7.18: vayaqrev et beito lagvarim vayilkad Achan ben Carmi: ‘he brought his household forward man by man, and Achan was taken’ — the final identification. The name Achan is connected by the narrative to achar (trouble) in v25 and to the Valley of Achor. The lot falls on one man after careful elimination, creating a publicly witnessed, undeniable identification: no appeal is possible — the process itself was YHWH’s revelation.
7.19: ten na kavod laYHWH Elohei Yisrael veten lo todah: ‘give glory to YHWH God of Israel and praise him’ — Joshua’s invitation to confess is framed as an act of worship: confessing sin is giving glory to God, acknowledging that YHWH is just and his judgment is right. The same formula appears in John 9:24 (Pharisees demand to the healed blind man: “give glory to God”) and Rev 14:7 (“give glory to him, for the hour of his judgment has come”). Confession as gloria is a consistent biblical structure.
7.20: omen anochi chatati laYHWH Elohei Yisrael: ‘it is true — I have sinned against YHWH God of Israel’ — the confession is direct and complete: omen anochi (truly it is I), chatati (I have sinned), addressed to YHWH. The sin is named theologically before it is described materially. This formal structure of confession — acknowledgment, theological framing, directed to God — is the model for genuine repentance throughout Scripture.
7.21: va'ere bish'al adderet Shinar achat tovah vachmadem va'eqqachem: ‘I saw… I coveted… I took’ — Achan’s account follows the same three-step sequence as the first sin in Eden: saw (vaere), coveted (vachmadem), took (va'eqqachem). The verb chamad (covet) is the exact word of the tenth commandment (Exod 20:17: “you shall not covet”). Achan was not ignorant of the law; he performed the act prohibited in the commandment against coveting. The Babylonian cloak (adderet Shinar) — Shinar being Babylon — anticipates the Babylonian exile that will ultimately destroy what Jericho’s fall began.
7.22: Joshua’s men ran to the tent (vayarutzu, they ran) — urgency. Everything is exactly as Achan described: the cloak, the silver, the gold wedge, hidden in the tent with the silver underneath. The confession is verified by the evidence; the evidence matches the confession perfectly. The hidden things are found when sought: “be sure your sin will find you out” (Num 32:23) is the pattern operating here as throughout Scripture.
7.23: vayiqachem mittoch ha'ohel vayavium el Yehoshua: ‘they took the things from the tent and brought them to Joshua and all the Israelites’ — the public display of the hidden goods restores the contamination to visibility. The community must see what was hidden; the hidden sin must become public before the community can be restored. There is a consistent OT principle that sin’s communal consequences require communal disclosure and judgment, not just private dealing.
7.24: emek Achor: the transfer to the Valley of Achor (trouble) is precise: everything connected to the sin — the man, his household, his goods, and the goods he stole — is brought together for judgment. The naming of the valley creates a permanent memorial. Hosea later transforms this valley: “I will make the Valley of Achor a door of hope” (Hos 2:15) — the place of Israel’s greatest corporate shame becomes the place of future restoration.
7.25: mah avar'tanu: ‘why have you troubled us?’ — the wordplay: akar (trouble) and emek Achor (Valley of Trouble). The name-to-fate technique creates a permanent linguistic memorial in the land’s geography. Achan’s execution by stoning and burning follows the cherem-contamination principle: what is contaminated by contact with cherem must be destroyed as cherem.
7.26: vayashov YHWH mechamat apo: ‘YHWH turned from his burning anger’ — the language of divine wrath and its turning is anthropopathic but structurally consistent: covenantal violation produces divine anger; covenantal justice produces the turning of that anger. The heap of stones over Achan creates a monument that will stand throughout Israel’s history — and the valley that marked sin’s judgment becomes, through divine grace, the threshold of restored covenant (Hos 2:15).

8.1: al tira ve'al techat: ‘do not fear or be dismayed’ — YHWH’s opening words to Joshua are the exact formula of the original commission (1:9). The repetition is deliberate: after the defeat at Ai and the Achan crisis, Joshua needs the commission renewed. Every new task in Joshua receives this divine declaration; the formula is not a one-time grant but a renewable resource. The same formula reaches its NT form in John 14:27: “let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid.”
8.2: raq shallah uvehamatah tavzu lachem: ‘only their plunder and livestock you shall take for yourselves’ — the key difference from Jericho: Ai is not cherem. The livestock and goods may be kept. This is YHWH’s liberality after judgment: Jericho was the firstfruits city requiring total consecration; subsequent cities allow Israel to benefit. The first belongs entirely to YHWH; subsequent gifts may be used by the recipient.
8.3: Joshua chose capable warriors for the night ambush. The precision of the ambush strategy contrasts sharply with the cavalier two-to-three-thousand approach of the first Ai expedition (7:3-4). After Jericho and Achan, Joshua does not economize on military preparation. The strategy is explicitly commanded by YHWH (v2: “set an ambush”) — not improvised but divinely prescribed. Divine guidance does not replace careful planning but directs it.
8.4: u'atem ta'arvu le'ir: ‘set an ambush against the city’ — erev (ambush/lying in wait) in holy war is a legitimate instrument. The contrast with Jericho is complete: there, the conquest required seven days of public liturgical marching; here, it requires careful concealment and tactical surprise. The God who prescribed the Jericho liturgy also prescribes the Ai ambush — both are divine strategies.
8.5: Joshua’s tactical explanation: they will advance toward Ai provoking the same response as before. The word yaratzu (they will run out) predicts the enemy’s response precisely — not just tactical prediction but covenantal certainty: YHWH has said he will hand Ai over; the tactical plan operates within that certainty. Military planning and divine promise are not competing categories.
8.6: ki yomeru nasoh nivchal milefaneihem: ‘for they will say, “They are fleeing before us, as before”’ — the planned feigned retreat exploits Ai’s memory of their previous victory. The king of Ai will repeat his successful strategy without recognizing that circumstances have changed. Those who have defeated God’s people once become overconfident, and their overconfidence becomes the means of their destruction: Pharaoh pursued Israel into the Red Sea on the strength of his previous success.
8.7: veatem taqumu min hama'arav: ‘then you shall rise from the ambush and seize the city’ — the rising of the ambush force is timed to the moment when Ai’s army is committed and cannot retreat. The ambush must hold still until the right moment; premature action would ruin the trap. Patience in waiting and decisiveness in acting are both required.
8.8: ketsav et hair baesh ki dabar YHWH tasunu: ‘set the city on fire — do this according to the word of YHWH’ — the fire is YHWH’s command, not tactical improvisation. The burning of Ai is an act of covenantal judgment, not military vandalism.
8.9: The ambush force positioned itself between Bethel and Ai to the west. The specific geographic reference locates the action precisely in the central hill country. The ambush waits through the night — another discipline of delayed action: the force that will seize the city must wait in concealment until the tactical moment arrives. Premature action would have been as fatal as Achan’s premature taking.
8.10: vayashkem Yehoshua baboqer: early rising again (cf. 6:12; 7:16) — the consistent pattern of early morning readiness for divine action. Joshua reviewed the troops (vayipqod, he mustered them) then led the elders of Israel at the head of the army: the covenant leadership goes to battle together, not from the rear.
8.11: The main army took up position north of Ai with a valley between them and the city. The tactical geography is precise: the valley makes a direct assault obvious while requiring Ai’s army to cross it to engage. The narrative attention to geography in chapters 6-12 is characteristic: the land is not a generic backdrop but a specific, named, remembered territory whose geography is woven into the memory of divine acts.
8.12: Five thousand men placed in ambush between Bethel and Ai to the west — a second ambush unit. The west/east positioning creates a pincer: the main army draws Ai eastward while the ambush takes the city from the west. The coordination required speaks to the discipline of the conquest force after the Achan crisis.
8.13: The positioning is complete: main force north, ambush west. Joshua went that night into the valley between the forces — commanders’ presence in the operational zone before the battle. Leadership is proximate to risk, not insulated from it.
8.14: vayashkem melech haAi vayimalaru vayetze: ‘when the king of Ai saw them, he hurried and went out’ — the verb yishkimu (they rose early, same root as Joshua’s early rising) is pointed: Ai’s king rises early to meet the threat, mirroring Joshua’s early rising for obedience. The enemy is energetic and responsive — but his energy is deployed in the wrong direction because he does not know the ambush is behind him. Asymmetric knowledge of the full situation is what Joshua has and the king of Ai lacks.
8.15: vayitnakkel Yehoshua vekol Yisrael lifneihem: ‘Joshua and all Israel made themselves appear beaten before them’ — vayitnakkel is reflexive: they made themselves appear beaten. Deliberate performance, a tactical fiction in the service of YHWH’s strategy. The tradition regards tactical stratagems as categorically different from prohibited deception; the Rahab narrative handled deception in service of YHWH’s purposes without moral condemnation.
8.16: vayizaqeu kol haam asher bair lirdof achareihem: ‘all the people who were in the city were called out to pursue them’ — the complete emptying of Ai is the trap springing shut. The city is evacuated of its defenders; the ambush will walk into an undefended position. The king’s initial success (routing the feint) becomes the mechanism of his total defeat.
8.17: velo nish'ar ish beAi uveVeit El asher lo yatza acharei Yisrael: ‘not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who had not gone out after Israel’ — Bethel’s troops also committed to the pursuit. Total commitment to the wrong direction is total vulnerability to the right one. Forces totally committed to pursuing apparent advantage are exposed to the power that works precisely through what appears defeated.
8.18: nteh bakidon asher beyadechu el haAi: ‘point the javelin in your hand toward Ai’ — the extended spear deliberately echoes Moses’s extended staff at Rephidim (Exod 17:11-12): “whenever Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed.” Joshua’s kidon (javelin) points toward Ai as Moses’s staff pointed toward the battle. Both gestures are not magical but covenantal — visible expressions of dependence on YHWH’s agency in the battle.
8.19: vayaqumu hama'arav bimheram: ‘the ambush rose quickly from their position’ — the force that waited through the night in disciplined stillness now acts with maximum speed (bimheram). Patience in waiting and decisiveness in acting are both required; the one who cannot hold still is as dangerous as the one who acts too late.
8.20: vayyipenu anshei haAi achareihem vayyiru vehinneh alah ashan hair hashamayyim: ‘the men of Ai looked back and saw the smoke from the city rising to the sky’ — the smoke is the visible sign that everything has gone wrong simultaneously: the city is captured behind them, they are committed too far forward to retreat, the feigned retreat is wheeling around to become an actual attack. Judgment often works this way: the moment of apparent escape is the moment the trap is most completely closed.
8.21: vayar Yehoshua vekol Yisrael ki lakad hama'arav et hair: ‘Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had captured the city’ — the smoke visible to both sides works differently on each: for Israel, the signal to turn and fight; for Ai, the signal that all is lost. The same event produces opposite responses depending on which side one is on. This is the consistent biblical pattern: divine acts are simultaneously redemptive and judgmental, life and death (2 Cor 2:15-16).
8.22: vayihu Yisrael betoch: ‘Israel was in their midst’ — the double encirclement is complete: main army attacking from the front and sides, ambush attacking from the rear, the men of the city trapped between. The word betoch (in the midst of) is the same used of YHWH’s presence among Israel; the reversal is complete — Israel was surrounded and routed at 7:5; now Ai is surrounded and destroyed.
8.23: ve et melech haAi tafsu chai: ‘they captured the king of Ai alive’ — the single exception to total destruction: the king is taken alive for public execution by Joshua (v29). The king’s public death before all Israel is both military demonstration and theological statement: the one who organized resistance to YHWH’s purpose is publicly judged, not merely eliminated.
8.24: vayyishvu kol Yisrael el haAi vayyakohu leftzi chariv: ‘all Israel returned to Ai and struck it with the edge of the sword’ — the systematic completion of the battle follows the ambush’s seizure of the city. What the first expedition failed to achieve through presumption is accomplished through obedience, divine strategy, and patient execution.
8.25: kol hanoflim bayom hahu me'ish ve'ad ishah shemeim ashar alef: ‘twelve thousand men and women fell that day — all the people of Ai’ — the death toll at Ai (12,000) far exceeds the initial Israelite defeat (36 dead, 7:5). The scale of the reversal is precise. The number twelve thousand — twelve tribes, thousands as the unit of military organization — may carry symbolic weight as representing a full tribal force.
8.26: veYehoshua lo hashiv yado asher natah bakidon ad asher hechrim et kol yoshvei haAi: ‘Joshua did not draw back his hand… until he had devoted all the inhabitants of Ai to destruction’ — the sustained extended arm is the explicit echo of Moses at Rephidim (Exod 17:12: “his hands were steady until the going down of the sun”). Moses held up his hands throughout the battle with Amalek; Joshua holds his spear extended throughout the battle with Ai. The sustained physical posture of the leader signifying sustained divine authority is a deliberate typological parallel: Joshua inheriting Moses’s role enacted through the same bodily posture.
8.27: raq habehemah ushlal hair hazot bazazu lahem Yisrael kadvar YHWH: ‘only the livestock and plunder of Ai Israel took for themselves — as YHWH commanded Joshua’ — the explicit contrast with Jericho: the same act that was cherem-violation at Jericho is covenant-obedience at Ai. The moral valence of an action depends not only on the action itself but on whether YHWH has permitted or prohibited it. Achan took what YHWH had kept sacred; Israel takes at Ai what YHWH has released.
8.28: vayyisrof Yehoshua et haAi vaysiymehah tel olam shemamah: ‘Joshua burned Ai and made it a tel olam — a permanent ruin’ — tel olam (forever-mound) is the origin of the archaeological term tell: a mound of accumulated ruins marking a destroyed city. The word olam (forever) makes the destruction permanent and deliberate; Ai will not be rebuilt for ordinary habitation. The destroyed cities leave permanent marks in the terrain as ongoing testimonies to the conquest.
8.29: ve et melech haAi talah al ha-etz: ‘the king of Ai he hanged on a tree’ — execution by hanging invokes Deut 21:23: “a hanged man is cursed by God.” The king dies under the covenant curse. The sunset-burial requirement (Deut 21:23) is observed. Paul applies the Deut 21:23 curse-formula to Christ’s death (Gal 3:13: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree’”). The king of Ai died as a cursed man; Christ died as the one who became the curse for others.
8.30: az yivneh Yehoshua mizbeach laYHWH Elohei Yisrael behar Eval: ‘then Joshua built an altar to YHWH God of Israel on Mount Ebal’ — the altar-building on Ebal immediately follows the conquest of Ai, before the conquest of the southern or northern territories. The sequence is deliberate: conquest leads to covenant renewal, not to further conquest. The altar on Ebal was commanded by Moses (Deut 27:4-8) as the first act upon entering the land.
8.31: kazasher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH livnei Yisrael kakatuv besefer torat Moshe: ‘as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded… as written in the Book of the Law of Moses’ — the altar is built exactly as specified: unhewn stones, no iron tool (cf. Exod 20:25: hewn stone would impose human craft on what is offered to YHWH). The altar’s simplicity is theological: YHWH does not require human artistry to make his altar acceptable. Contrast with the elaborate metalwork of the tabernacle; the Ebal altar is deliberate simplicity for a specific purpose.
8.32: vayiktov sham al ha'avanim et mishneh torat Moshe: ‘he wrote on the stones a copy of the Law of Moses’ — the Torah inscribed on stones at the moment of land-entry creates a visible, permanent record of the covenant obligation governing the land’s use. The inscribed Torah on Ebal’s stones anticipates the new covenant’s inscription: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts” (Jer 31:33).
8.33: vehager: ‘and the resident alien’ — the covenant assembly at Ebal and Gerizim explicitly includes the ger (resident alien) alongside the native-born Israelite. The inclusion of sojourners in the covenant assembly is remarkable: the Sinai covenant was given to Israel, but at the first renewal ceremony in the land, non-Israelite residents are included. The universality latent in the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 12:3) begins to manifest at Ebal: the covenant community is expanding, not contracting, in the land.
8.34: veachar qara et kol divrei hatorah habrachah vehaqelalah: ‘afterward he read all the words of the Law, the blessings and the curses’ — the full public reading of Deut 27-28 in the land, with the entire community assembled, creates a formal covenant ratification. The blessings and curses are read aloud before all Israel: the community enters the land as a covenant community fully informed of its obligations and consequences. The transparency of the covenant terms — public, inclusive, comprehensive — means no one can claim ignorance of what was at stake.
8.35: lo hayah davar mikol asher tzivah Moshe asher lo qara Yehoshua: ‘there was not a word of all that Moses commanded that Joshua did not read before the assembly’ — total comprehensiveness: every word, nothing omitted. Women, little children, and resident aliens were all included — every member of the community heard the complete Torah. The fullness-of-revelation principle: the covenant community receives the full disclosure of what God requires and what God promises. The church’s obligation is the same: “the whole counsel of God” (Acts 20:27), not a curated selection.

12.1: wĕʾēlleh malkê hāʾāreṣ ʾašer hikkû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'these are the kings of the land that the Israelites struck.' The list form (king-roster) is a known genre in ANE victory texts; Assyrian and Egyptian annals feature comparable king-lists. Its function here is theological: the God who promised Abraham this land (Gen 15:18-21) has delivered 31 rulers, beginning with the two trans-Jordan paradigm cases (Sihon and Og) established in Numbers 21.
12.2: Sihon king of the Amorites is first — his defeat (Num 21:21-31) was the breakthrough moment that silenced Israel's objection to crossing into Canaan. yōšēb bĕḥešbôn mōšēl mēʿărôʿēr — 'who lived in Heshbon, who ruled from Aroer.' The geographic precision (Arnon River, Jabbok River, half of Gilead) is an official boundary description, the language of land grants. The Mosaic victories function as the legal precedent for the Canaanite allotments.
12.3: The eastern Arabah — from Kinnereth (Sea of Galilee) to the Salt Sea (Dead Sea). ûmimmizraḥ haʿărābāh covers the full Jordan rift valley under Sihon's control. This eastern survey is the legal background for ch.13's trans-Jordan allotments; the boundaries here are not narrative decoration but the formal basis for tribal land titles.
12.4: Og of Bashan: min-yeter hārĕpāʾîm — 'one of the last of the Rephaim.' The Rephaim were the giant peoples of the pre-Israelite Canaan (Gen 14:5; 15:20; Deut 2:20-21; 3:11). Og's famous iron bed (Deut 3:11) measured 9 cubits × 4 cubits — whether a bed or a sarcophagus, it was preserved as a monument to the victory. His territory reached to Hermon; the defeat of this last Rephaite king is the culmination of a campaign against supernatural-sized opponents that began in the wilderness.
12.5: Og's territory: Mount Hermon, Salecah, all of Bashan. The border with the Geshurites and Maacathites is significant: these peoples are not driven out (Josh 13:13), and Geshur will later be David's maternal homeland (2 Sam 3:3) — political geography and later narrative are already entangled in this boundary description.
12.6: mōšeh ʿebed YHWH ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl hikkûm — 'Moses the servant of the LORD and the Israelites struck them.' Sihon and Og are assigned to Moses; the trans-Jordan victories are Mosaic inheritance. wayyittĕnāh mōšeh yerûššāh — 'Moses gave it as a possession.' Yerûššāh (possession, inherited property) is legally stronger than mere conquest; the trans-Jordan tribes hold by Mosaic grant, not just military occupation.
12.7: The Cisjordan list begins: wĕʾēlleh malkê hāʾāreṣ ʾašer hikkāh yĕhôšuaʿ ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl. The parallel structure (Mosaic victories / Josuaic victories) is deliberate: Joshua completes what Moses began. The geographic frame — Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley to Mount Halak in the south — defines the full conquest zone announced in ch.1:4.
12.8: The land's varied terrain is listed: hill country, western foothills (šĕpēlāh), the Arabah, mountain slopes (ʾăšēdôt), wilderness, and Negev. These six zones correspond to the campaign zones of chs.10-11. The six peoples list (Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) is the standard Deuteronomic enumeration (Deut 7:1) — the list signals that YHWH's mandate to dispossess these specific peoples has been carried out.
12.9: The Cisjordan list proper begins: king of Jericho (one), king of Ai (one). The formulaic melek ... ʾeḥād ('king of X, one') is the signature structure of the list. Starting with Jericho and Ai grounds the list in the paradigmatic first two victories of chs.6-8; every subsequent entry is measured against those standards.
12.10: Jerusalem and Hebron. Jerusalem's king Adonizedek was defeated in ch.10 but the city itself was not yet taken (Josh 15:63; Judg 1:21 — it remains Jebusite until David). Hebron will become Caleb's inheritance (ch.14) and later David's first capital. The list includes the king as the defeated party; city occupation is a separate question.
12.11: Jarmuth, Lachish — part of the southern coalition defeated in ch.10. Lachish appears in later Assyrian records (the Lachish Reliefs of Sennacherib, ANET 287-288) as one of Judah's strongest fortresses; its prominence in the conquest list reflects its genuine strategic importance.
12.12: Eglon, Gezer. Gezer's king Horam was defeated (ch.10:33) but Gezer itself was not fully taken (Josh 16:10: 'the Canaanites who lived in Gezer were not driven out'). The list records the king's defeat, not necessarily the city's complete occupation — a distinction the allotment chapters will clarify.
12.13: Debir, Geder. Debir's double appearance (here; ch.15:15-17 as Othniel's conquest) reflects either a reconquest after Canaanite resettlement or variant traditions preserved side by side. The list does not attempt to resolve this; it records the Joshua-era defeat.
12.14: Hormah (ḥormāh, from ḥērem, 'devoted to destruction') — the name itself encodes the ḥērem ideology; its place-name likely commemorates an earlier battle (Num 14:45; 21:3). Arad — the Negev fortress whose king also attacked Israel in Num 21:1-3.
12.15: Libnah, Adullam — both in the Shephelah. Adullam will become famous as David's cave-refuge (1 Sam 22:1), setting up a future narrative connection already embedded in the geography of conquest.
12.16: Makkedah, Bethel. Bethel's prominence in patriarchal narrative (Gen 12:8; 28:19 — where Jacob named it 'house of God') gives its appearance in the conquest list theological weight: the place where YHWH promised Abraham and Jacob the land is now taken.
12.17: Tappuah, Hepher. Geographic completeness: the list is cataloguing the western foothills and northern Shephelah, ensuring no gap in the victory record.
12.18: Aphek, Lasharon. Aphek will be significant later: it is the Philistine staging ground when the ark is captured (1 Sam 4:1) — conquest-era and monarchic-era narratives share geography.
12.19: Madon, Hazor. Hazor is the last of the major northern cities listed (the one Joshua burned, ch.11:11); its appearance in this comprehensive list anchors the northern campaign's climax in the victory inventory.
12.20: Shimron-meron, Achshaph — northern Galilean cities of the Jabin coalition (ch.11:1). Their inclusion confirms the list's geographic sweep: south to north, every major coalition city enumerated.
12.21: Taanach, Megiddo — the great northern fortress cities controlling the Jezreel Valley passes. Megiddo (later har Megiddon = Armageddon, Rev 16:16) was not fully taken — Judg 1:27 notes Manasseh failed to drive out Taanach and Megiddo's Canaanites. The gap between the list's claim and later reality is intentional: the list asserts the kings' defeat, not permanent occupation.
12.22: Kedesh, Jokneam in Carmel — the Galilean hill country and the ridge leading to the coast. These are the northernmost cities in the Cisjordan list, completing the geographic arc from Jericho (v.9) in the south to Galilee in the north.
12.23: Dor in the Naphoth-dor coastal plain, Goiim in Gilgal — filling out the Mediterranean coast. The inclusion of coastal cities alongside the inland cities marks the scope as truly comprehensive, even as subsequent chapters acknowledge Canaanite persistence in many of these areas.
12.24: kol-mĕlākîm šĕlōšîm wĕʾeḥād — 'all the kings, thirty-one.' The number closes the list with deliberate finality. Thirty-one defeated kings document total YHWH-given victory across every region of Canaan. The number itself is not mystically significant, but its precision as a closed inventory signals that the conquest narrative is complete: all that needed to be said about conquest has been said; what follows (chs.13-22) is inheritance distribution.

13.1: wîhôšuaʿ zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm — 'Joshua was old, advanced in days.' The idiom bāʾ bayyāmîm ('come into days') appears elsewhere only of Abraham (Gen 24:1) and David (1 Kgs 1:1); it signals a leader at the end of his productive season. YHWH's immediate response — 'much land remains to be possessed' — frames the problem: the conquest is unfinished, and the leader is aging. The tension between incomplete possession and approaching death drives chapters 13-24.
13.2: zōʾt hāʾāreṣ hanniśʾāret — 'this is the land that remains.' The admission that land remains contradicts the conquest summaries of 11:23 and 21:43 only if those summaries are taken as absolute rather than judicial. The ideal ('all the land was taken') and the realistic ('land remains') coexist — the former is a theological verdict on YHWH's faithfulness; the latter is a historical acknowledgment of incomplete execution.
13.3: The Philistine pentapolis: Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron. hammiṣraʾ ʾašer ʿal-pĕnê miṣrayim — 'the Shihor, which is before Egypt' — marks the southwestern boundary. The Philistine cities are counted as Canaanite territory (liknāʿanî teḥāšēb) — a legal categorization that will later matter for how their continued occupation is understood.
13.4: Southward: Canaanite territory and Mearah of the Sidonians. The inclusion of Sidonian territory (Phoenician coast) within the land's ideal boundaries reflects the Gen 15:18-21 promise ('from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates'). The actual possession never reached these limits; the promise remains partially unfulfilled, pointing forward to the ultimate Davidic/Solomonic expansion (1 Kgs 4:21) and beyond.
13.5: Lebanon from Baal-gad to Lebo-hamath — the northern boundaries of the ideal land. The Giblites (haggĕblî) are the inhabitants of Byblos (Gebal), a major Phoenician port. These northern territories remain unoccupied throughout the monarchy period; their inclusion in the 'remaining land' acknowledges the gap between promise and possession.
13.6: ʾănî ʾōrîšennem mippĕnê bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'I will drive them out before Israel.' YHWH's first-person commitment — 'I will do it' — shifts responsibility back to divine action. Joshua's role is now to divide the land by lot (raq happilah lĕyiśrāʾēl bĕnaḥălāh); the driving out will happen as Israel obeys within its allotments. The promise structure: YHWH acts as Israel advances.
13.7: wĕʿattāh ḥalleq ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt bĕnaḥălāh — 'now divide this land as inheritance.' The imperative and the noun naḥălāh (inheritance) together signal a major structural transition: from conquest narrative to inheritance distribution. Naḥălāh (appearing ~200× in the OT) denotes permanent familial patrimony — land held not by right of conquest alone but by covenantal grant, passed through generations.
13.8: Review of the trans-Jordan inheritance given by Moses. The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh received their allotments east of the Jordan (Num 32); this chapter confirms those grants. The Mosaic inheritance is the precedent and model for the Josuaic distribution now beginning.
13.9: Trans-Jordan geography: Aroer by the Arnon, the city in the middle of the valley, the Medeba plateau. The mishor ('plateau/tableland') is the characteristic terrain of Moab and trans-Jordan Reuben — flat, elevated plain east of the Dead Sea. The geographic details are the property records of Reubenite land tenure.
13.10: All the cities of Sihon up to the Ammonite border. ʿad-gĕbûl bĕnê ʿammôn — 'to the border of the Ammonites.' The Ammonites are intentionally excluded; Deut 2:19 forbids Israel from taking Ammonite territory ('I have given it to the sons of Lot as a possession'). These land grants respect the existing distribution of peoples even as they claim Amorite territory.
13.11: Gilead, the territory of Geshurites and Maacathites, and all Mount Hermon. wĕhakkol haggĕšûrî wĕhammaakatî — the Geshurites and Maacathites are included in the allotment description but not in the actual possession, as v.13 will immediately note. The legal grant and the actual possession diverge; the gap is acknowledged but not resolved.
13.12: kol-mamleket ʿōg babbāšān ʾašer-mālaḵ bĕʿaštārôt ûbĕʾedreʿî hûʾ nišʾar miyeter hārĕpāʾîm — 'the whole kingdom of Og in Bashan, who reigned at Ashtaroth and Edrei; he alone remained of the remnant of the Rephaim.' The Rephaim identification is repeated from ch.12:4, maintaining the motif: this territory was won from the last of a dying race of giant peoples. The conquest of trans-Jordan was a conquest of the uncanny as much as the military.
13.13: wĕlōʾ hōrîšû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-haggĕšûrî wĕʾet-hammaʿăkātî — 'the Israelites did not drive out the Geshurites or the Maacathites.' The first of the failure notices that punctuate the allotment chapters. wayyēšeb haggĕšûrî wĕhammaakatî bĕqereb yiśrāʾēl ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — 'they live among Israel to this day.' The 'to this day' formula marks the note as etiological — explaining a visible present reality by reference to a past decision. Geshur's survival will eventually produce Maacah, who becomes David's wife and Absalom's mother (2 Sam 3:3; 13:37) — an unconquered people becomes a dynastic entanglement.
13.14: raq lĕšēbeṭ halēwî lōʾ-nātan naḥălāh — 'only to the tribe of Levi he did not give an inheritance.' The raq ('only/except') marks Levi as the single anomaly in the distribution system. ʾiššê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hûʾ naḥălātô — 'the fire-offerings of YHWH God of Israel are their inheritance.' The naḥălāh of YHWH's fire-offerings (ʾiššê) replaces territory; Levi's sustenance comes from the altar, not the land. This principle — that priestly service to YHWH constitutes an inheritance — is the theological foundation for the NT principle that ministers of the gospel are supported by those they serve (1 Cor 9:13-14).
13.15: The Reubenite allotment begins. wayyittēn mōšeh lĕmaṭṭēh bĕnê rĕʾûbēn lĕmišpĕḥōtām — 'Moses gave to the tribe of Reuben according to their clans.' The distribution unit is the mišpāḥāh (clan/extended family group), not the individual; the inheritance is corporate and generational.
13.16: Reuben's territory: Aroer by the Arnon, the city in the middle of the valley, and the Medeba plateau. The precise boundary descriptions use the landmark-and-feature format of ANE boundary texts (parallel to Egyptian and Hittite treaty boundary clauses). Each named landmark is a legally recognized point in the property description.
13.17: Cities within Reuben's allotment: Heshbon (Sihon's capital), Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon. The presence of baal-names in the allotted territory (Beth-baal-meon, Bamoth-baal) reflects Canaanite place-names that Israel inherited without immediately renaming — the toponym preserves the religious history of the land even as it changes ownership.
13.18: Jahaz, Kedemoth, Mephaath — sites along Reuben's eastern border. Jahaz was where Sihon attacked Israel (Num 21:23; Deut 2:32), making its inclusion in Reuben's allotment a direct conversion of battlefield to farmland.
13.19: Kiriathaim, Sibmah, Zereth-shahar on the hill in the valley — Reuben's hill country. Sibmah was known for its vineyards (Isa 16:8-9; Jer 48:32) — the allotment assigns productive agricultural land, not merely boundary markers.
13.20: Beth-peor, the slopes of Pisgah, Beth-jeshimoth. Beth-peor is freighted with memory: it was at Peor that Israel committed apostasy with Moabite women (Num 25:3; Deut 4:3). Moses was buried 'in the valley opposite Beth-peor' (Deut 34:6). Reuben's inheritance includes sites of Israel's deepest failures — the land is taken in full, shame included.
13.21: All the cities of the plateau (ʿārê hammiššôr) and the kingdom of Sihon. The formula kol-mamleket sîḥōn melek hāʾĕmōrî ʾašer mālaḵ bĕḥešbôn — 'the whole kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites who reigned at Heshbon' — signals that Reuben inherits not just territory but sovereignty: what was a Canaanite kingdom becomes an Israelite tribal holding.
13.22: wĕʾet-bilʿām ben-bĕʿôr haqqōsēm hārĕgû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl beḥereb — 'Balaam son of Beor the diviner, the Israelites killed with the sword.' The term qōsēm (diviner, one who practices qĕsāmôt) labels Balaam by his means of knowledge, not just his role. In Num 22-24 Balaam functions as a prophet (nābîʾ-like figure) who speaks YHWH's words; here he is demoted to qōsēm — one whose methods are prohibited in Israel (Deut 18:10). The retroactive reclassification reflects his ultimate fate: a man who blessed Israel was killed as a collaborator in Midian's seduction of Israel (Num 31:8,16). The NT references to Balaam (2 Pet 2:15; Jude 11; Rev 2:14) follow this pejorative labeling.
13.23: The Jordan and its border constitute Reuben's western boundary. gĕbûl bĕnê rĕʾûbēn wayyihyeh hayyardēn ûgĕbûl — 'the Jordan and its bank was their border.' The Jordan is both boundary and identity marker: the trans-Jordan tribes are defined by their position east of the river, which will become a point of tension in ch.22 (the altar dispute).
13.24: The Gadite allotment begins. wayyittēn mōšeh lĕmaṭṭēh gād lĕmišpĕḥōtām — structural parallel to the Reubenite grant formula.
13.25: Gad's territory: Jazer, all the cities of Gilead, and half the Ammonite territory 'to Aroer which is before Rabbah.' The Ammonite adjacency makes Gad the eastern frontier tribe, bordering the perennial Ammonite opponents. Gilead's association with Gad is so strong that 'Gilead' becomes a shorthand for the trans-Jordan tribes (Judg 5:17; 10:18).
13.26: From Heshbon to Ramath-mizpeh and Betonim; from Mahanaim to the border of Debir. Mahanaim is Jacob's site of angelic encounter (Gen 32:2), where he named the place 'two camps'; it becomes an Israelite administrative and military base (2 Sam 2:8; 17:24; 1 Kgs 2:8). Jacob's patriarchal sites become Gadite cities.
13.27: In the valley: Beth-haram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, Zaphon — these are the Jordan valley floor settlements, fertile lowlands east of the river. Succoth was Jacob's first settlement returning from Laban (Gen 33:17), another patriarchal site incorporated into the tribal map.
13.28: The Gadite inheritance concludes: cities and their villages. The total is measured against the Mosaic grant formula: zōʾt naḥălat bĕnê-gād lĕmišpĕḥōtām — 'this is the inheritance of the Gadites by their clans.' The clan-unit distribution ensures generational continuity.
13.29: Half-Manasseh's trans-Jordan territory. wayyittēn mōšeh laḥăṣî šēbet hamnașṣeh — Moses distributes to the half-tribe, reflecting the unusual division of Manasseh between trans-Jordan (half) and Cisjordan (half). This split-tribe arrangement creates structural complexity in the later allotment chapters (Josh 17) as both halves are allocated separately.
13.30: Half-Manasseh's territory from Mahanaim through all Bashan: kol-baššān kol-mamleket ʿōg babbāšān wĕkol-ḥawwōt yāʾîr — 'all Bashan, the whole kingdom of Og, and all the tent-villages of Jair.' The Havvoth-jair ('tent-villages of Jair') represent a form of semi-nomadic settlement, named for the Manassite hero Jair (Num 32:41) — personal-name places that encode the history of conquest in the landscape.
13.31: Half of Gilead and Og's cities (Ashtaroth and Edrei) go to half-Manasseh. lĕbnê mākhîr ben-manašše laḥăṣî — 'to the descendants of Machir son of Manasseh, for half of them.' Machir is the dominant Manassite clan; the allotment recognizes clan-level distinctions within the tribe.
13.32: ʾēlleh ʾašer niḥal mōšeh bĕʿarābōt môʾāb — 'these are what Moses distributed in the plains of Moab.' The trans-Jordan distribution is retrospective: Eleazar and Joshua had not yet begun; the Cisjordan allotments are still ahead. The chapter closes the Mosaic distribution before opening Joshua's.
13.33: wĕlišēbeṭ halēwî lōʾ-nātan mōšeh naḥălāh — 'to the tribe of Levi Moses gave no inheritance.' The Levitical exception closes the chapter and mirrors v.14. YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hûʾ naḥălātām — 'YHWH God of Israel is their inheritance.' The hûʾ ('he is') is emphatic — not just any inheritance but YHWH himself as the inalienable possession. This theological principle is picked up in Ps 16:5 ('YHWH is my chosen portion and my cup') and pervades the NT understanding of God as the believer's ultimate inheritance (Eph 1:14,18; 1 Pet 1:4).

14.1: wĕʾēlleh ʾašer niḥălû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan ʾašer niḥălû ʾōtām ʾelʿāzār hakōhēn wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn wĕrāʾšê hāʾăbôt — 'these are what the Israelites received as inheritance in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the fathers' [houses] distributed to them.' Three-party oversight: priest (covenant mediator), commander (covenantal executor), and tribal elders (community representatives). The process is ecclesiastical, military, and civic simultaneously — no single party controls the distribution.
14.2: bĕgôrāl naḥălātām kāʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH bĕyad-mōšeh — 'by lot was their inheritance, as YHWH commanded through Moses.' The gôrāl (lot) is the divine decision-mechanism that removes human favoritism from the distribution. Proverbs 16:33 theologically grounds this: 'The lot is cast into the lap, but every decision is from YHWH.' The lot is not a random procedure but an oracular one — YHWH assigns each tribe its place.
14.3: Moses had already given the two and a half trans-Jordan tribes their portions; Levi receives no land. The structured recapitulation (Levi noted here and in v.4b) is not redundancy but legal clarity: the nine and a half tribes' lots are about to begin; the exceptions are established first so the reader knows the scope of what follows.
14.4: kî-hāyû bĕnê yôsēp šĕnê maṭṭôt mĕnaššeh wĕʾeprayim — 'for the sons of Joseph were two tribes, Manasseh and Ephraim.' The Joseph division is the mechanism by which twelve tribal lots fill twelve land portions despite Levi's exclusion. Jacob's deathbed elevation of Ephraim and Manasseh to tribal status (Gen 48:5: 'they shall be mine... as Reuben and Simeon they shall be mine') is the legal basis for this arithmetic. The Levitical cities and pasture lands (v.4b) are distinct from tribal allotments — Levi's distributed presence throughout the land embodies the nation's priestly calling.
14.5: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh kēn ʿāśû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyaḥlĕqû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — 'as YHWH commanded Moses, so the Israelites did, and they divided the land.' The obedience formula closes the introduction and opens the Caleb narrative. The allotment process is an act of covenant obedience, not administrative convenience.
14.6: wayyiggĕšû bĕnê yĕhûdāh ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ bĕgilgāl wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw kālēb ben-yĕpunneh haqqĕnizzî — 'the men of Judah approached Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite said to him.' Caleb the Kenizzite is a non-Israelite by ancestry (the Kenizzites are listed in Gen 15:19 among the peoples of the land) who is fully incorporated into Judah. His access to the allotment as a full heir demonstrates that covenant membership — not ethnic lineage — determines inheritance rights. The appeal to 'what YHWH said to Moses' (ʾet-ʾašer dibber YHWH ʾel-mōšeh) grounds his claim in prophetic promise, not merely military service.
14.7: ben-ʾarbaʿîm šānāh ʾānōkî bišloaḥ mōšeh ʿebed YHWH ʾōtî — 'I was forty years old when Moses the servant of the LORD sent me.' The age specificity (forty at the reconnaissance, eighty-five now, v.10) structures the narrative: forty-five years from promise to fulfilment. Caleb's report: kaʾašer ʿim-lĕbābî — 'just as it was in my heart,' meaning the report he gave was his genuine conviction, not diplomatic accommodation. The ʾăšer ʿim-lĕbābî phrase connects sincerity of report with sincerity of faith.
14.8: wĕʾaḥay ʾašer ʿālû ʿimmî himmasîû ʾet-lēb hāʿām wĕʾānōkî millēʾtî ʾaḥărê YHWH ʾĕlōhāy — 'my fellow scouts made the heart of the people melt, but I fully followed after YHWH my God.' The key verb: millēʾtî from mālaʾ (be full, fill) + ʾaḥărê (after, behind). Millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH is the book's distinctive idiom for wholehearted covenantal loyalty — used of Caleb in Num 14:24; 32:12; Deut 1:36; here and in vv.9,14. The idiom images following as 'filling the space behind' YHWH — not reluctant compliance but total engagement of will.
14.9: wayyiššābaʿ mōšeh bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr ʾim-lōʾ hāʾāreṣ ʾašer dārakāh raglĕkā bāh lĕkā tihyeh lĕnaḥălāh — 'Moses swore on that day: the land where your feet have walked shall be your inheritance.' The oath grounds Caleb's claim in prophetic promise (Num 14:24; Deut 1:36) ratified by Mosaic oath. Forty-five years of waiting has not diminished the claim; the oath remains binding because it was sworn before YHWH.
14.10: wĕʿattāh hinnēh heḥěyāh YHWH ʾōtî kaʾašer dibber — 'now, look — YHWH has kept me alive just as he promised.' Heḥěyāh (kept alive, from ḥāyāh) is the same verb used of YHWH preserving life through the wilderness; Caleb attributes his forty-five-year survival not to constitution but to divine faithfulness. mizzeh ʾarbaʿîm wĕḥāmēš šānāh — forty-five years since the Sinai promise, twenty-five of which were conquest campaigns. The arithmetic of grace: the promise was made; the time has passed; YHWH has delivered.
14.11: kōḥî ʿōdennû hayyôm kaʾašer kōḥî bĕyôm šĕloaḥ mōšeh ʾōtî — 'my strength today is still as my strength was on the day Moses sent me.' The claim is remarkable: eighty-five years old, and Caleb asserts physical parity with his forty-year-old self. Whether read literally (YHWH's supernatural preservation) or rhetorically (relative vigor, not comparative measurement), the statement is a theology of divine sustenance — Caleb's vitality is evidence of YHWH's faithfulness to his promise.
14.12: wĕʿattāh tĕnāh-lî ʾet-hāhār hazzeh — 'now give me this hill country!' The imperative is bold — Caleb is not requesting but claiming. Hāhār hazzeh ('this hill country') is Hebron and its Anakite-held heights. Caleb had seen the Anakites and was unafraid at Kadesh (Num 13:33); now at eighty-five he asks for the hardest territory, the place where the giants live. ûlay YHWH ʾittî wĕhôraštîm kaʾašer dibber YHWH — 'perhaps YHWH will be with me, and I shall drive them out just as YHWH has said.' The ûlay ('perhaps, maybe') is not doubt but covenantal humility — Caleb frames the conquest as contingent on divine presence, not guaranteed by his vigor.
14.13: waybārekhēhû yĕhôšuaʿ wayyittēn ʾet-ḥebrôn lĕkālēb ben-yĕpunneh lĕnaḥălāh — 'Joshua blessed him and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as inheritance.' The priestly blessing (bārak) before the grant marks this as a covenant act, not merely an administrative transfer. Hebron — Kiriath-arba, the city of Arba the greatest of the Anakites — is given to the man who was fearless before Anakites forty-five years earlier. Promise and fulfillment are calibrated: the specific fear that disqualified the first generation is overcome by the specific faith of the one who refused to fear.
14.14: ʿal-kēn hāyĕtāh ḥebrôn lĕkālēb ben-yĕpunneh haqqĕnizzî lĕnaḥălāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh yaʿan ʾašer millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'therefore Hebron has belonged to Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite as inheritance to this day, because he wholeheartedly followed YHWH God of Israel.' The causal chain is explicit: the inheritance is the consequence of wholehearted following. Millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH (appearing for the final time) is the book's summary verdict on Caleb's life: one who filled the space behind YHWH completely. The 'to this day' formula anchors the etiological claim — Hebron as Calebite territory is a historical fact requiring explanation, and the explanation is theological.
14.15: wĕšem ḥebrôn lĕpānîm qiryat-ʾarbaʿ — 'the former name of Hebron was Kiriath-arba.' The rename from 'city of Arba' (the greatest Anakite) to 'Hebron' (from ḥābar, 'friend/confederate') may reflect Hebrew renaming practice after conquest. Arba's name and greatness are preserved even in the record of his people's defeat — the text does not erase the enemy's memory but notes it within the victory account. Wattišqōt hāʾāreṣ mimmilḥāmāh — 'and the land had rest from war.' The šāqaṭ (rest/quiet) formula closes this section as it closed ch.11:23; each major phase of the narrative ends with land-rest as the theological verdict: YHWH's wars, when completed, produce peace.

21.1: vayigshu rashei avot haLviim el Eleazar hakohen: ‘the heads of the Levitical fathers came to Eleazar the priest’ — the petition is presented to the right authority (Eleazar the priest, not Joshua alone), consistent with Numbers 35’s assignment of the Levitical cities as a priestly matter. The Levites’ request initiates the last major land-distribution act. Unlike the tribal allotments, the Levitical cities are not a homeland but a dispersal: forty-eight cities spread through all twelve tribes, so that the priestly service function is distributed across the entire nation. The design prevents the concentration of priestly power in one location while ensuring that every tribe has access to Levitical instruction.
21.2: tzavah YHWH beyad Moshe latet lanu arim lashevet umigrasheichen: ‘YHWH commanded through Moses to give us cities to live in, with pasturelands for our livestock’ — the petition is grounded in prior divine command (Num 35:1-8), not in general need. The Levites are not asking for generosity but invoking an obligation: YHWH promised this through Moses. The migrash (pastureland, grazing area) attached to each city is a technical land term — a strip of common land surrounding the city, not for agriculture but for livestock. The Levites’ material provision is specified to prevent their dependence on tribal agriculture while preserving their availability for priestly service.
21.3: vayitnu bnei Yisrael laLviim min nachalatam al pi YHWH: ‘the Israelites gave the Levites from their inheritance at YHWH’s command’ — the giving is from tribal allotments: each tribe contributes cities to the Levites from land already apportioned to them. This is the mechanism of Numbers 35:8 (in proportion to the inheritance each tribe receives, so the cities it gives). The obedience formula al pi YHWH (at the mouth of YHWH) marks the transaction as covenant compliance, not tribal generosity. Israel is not doing the Levites a favor but honoring a divine arrangement.
21.4: vayetze hagoral lemishpachot haQehati: ‘the lot fell for the Kohathite families’ — the three Levitical clans (Kohath, Gershon, Merari) receive their cities by lot, introducing the element of divine determination into the geographic distribution. The Kohathites (Moses and Aaron’s clan) receive first; within the Kohathites, the Aaronite priestly line receives thirteen cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin — the southern tribes closest to Jerusalem, anticipating the future centralization of priestly service at the Davidic temple. The distribution is not random but providentially positioned.
21.5: The remaining Kohathite families (non-priestly Kohathites who carried the ark and furnishings in the wilderness) received ten cities from Ephraim, Dan, and half-Manasseh. The geographic distribution of non-priestly Kohathites in the central and northern territories corresponds to the areas with the highest subsequent need for Levitical teaching. The later turbulence of the northern kingdom (1 Kings 12:31 — Jeroboam’s non-Levitical priests) will confirm that the loss of Levitical presence had devastating consequences.
21.6: The Gershonites received thirteen cities from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan — the northern and Transjordan territories. The Gershonites had carried the tabernacle’s coverings and curtains in the wilderness; their placement in the north distributes the priestly tradition through the tribal areas most geographically distant from the future temple. The provision for Levites in remote areas is an infrastructure decision: the covenant is not a Jerusalem-only institution.
21.7: The Merarites received twelve cities from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun — spanning both sides of the Jordan. The Merarites carried the tabernacle’s structural components (boards, bars, pillars) in the wilderness; their placement in the Transjordan tribes and the northern plain ensures that the tribal territories that would otherwise have the weakest Levitical connection are served. The geographic intentionality of the distribution becomes apparent when mapped: no tribal territory is without Levitical cities.
21.8: vayitnu bnei Yisrael et he'arim ha'eleh vegidrotheichen laLviim kal asher tzivah YHWH beyad Moshe: ‘the Israelites gave these cities and their pasturelands to the Levites, just as YHWH commanded through Moses’ — summary formula closing the introduction before the detailed city lists. The double attribution — to Israel’s act and to YHWH’s prior command — is characteristic of Joshua’s narrative theology: human obedience and divine institution are simultaneous, not competing.
21.9: The city list begins with Judah and Simeon — the tribes whose territory overlaps and who together provide the first set of Aaronite cities. The narrative precision of the city lists (naming each city individually rather than describing regions) creates a verifiable record: the Levitical distribution is not a literary ideal but a documented historical reality. The specificity also serves a mnemonic function: these cities with their Levitical connections were part of Israel’s institutional memory.
21.10: The descendants of Aaron receive the first lot — the priestly line within the Kohathites. The narrative re-identifies them as Kohathite Levites to clarify the clan structure: all Aaronites are Kohathites, but not all Kohathites are Aaronites. The structural precision matters because the distinction between priestly Kohathites (Aaron’s line) and non-priestly Kohathites (other Kohathite families) is the fundamental division of Levitical function: proximity to the altar is reserved for Aaron’s sons.
21.11: et Qiryat Arba avi haAnak hi Chevron: ‘Kiriath-arba, which is Hebron — Arba was the forefather of Anak’ — Hebron’s double identification (both names, with genealogical note) is consistent with Joshua’s practice of noting the pre-Israelite names of significant sites. Hebron is significant because it was Caleb’s personal allotment (14:13-14) and David’s first capital (2 Sam 2:1-4). The Levitical city and Caleb’s personal holding coexist: the city itself (with its pasturelands) goes to the Levites; the fields and villages of the surrounding territory go to Caleb (v12).
21.12: vesedeh hair vegidroteiha natan Calev ben Yefuneh biachuzato: ‘but the fields and villages they gave to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his personal possession’ — the distinction between the city proper (including its immediate pasturelands) and the surrounding agricultural territory is precise. Caleb retains his promised inheritance; the Levites receive the city as a residence and service base. The two claims do not conflict because they operate at different scales: the city for Levitical habitation, the territory for Calebite agriculture. The careful demarcation reflects the actual administrative complexity of land tenure in ancient Israel.
21.13: uvnei Aharon hakohen natan et Chevron ir miqlashot haroteach: ‘to the descendants of Aaron the priest they gave Hebron — a city of refuge for the manslayer’ — the overlap of Levitical city and city of refuge is deliberate: in the priestly territories, the places of asylum are also places of priestly presence. The manslayer who flees to a city of refuge is in a space where priestly mediation is immediately available — not merely protected from the blood-avenger, but in the presence of those who can adjudicate the case and provide atonement.
21.14: Jattir and Eshtemoa with their pasturelands — cities in the southern hill country of Judah. The names of these cities become part of the record of David’s distribution of plunder to the elders of Judah after Ziklag (1 Sam 30:27-28): Eshtemoa appears in that list, connecting the later Davidic history to the Levitical city network. The Levitical cities were centers of community life, not isolated priestly enclaves.
21.15: Holon and Debir — cities in the Judahite hill country. Debir (Kiriath-sepher) was taken by Othniel, Caleb’s nephew, as the first judge-act of the conquest period (Josh 15:15-19). Its assignment here as a Levitical city means that the site of the first conquest-by-faith becomes a center of priestly presence and instruction. The geography of redemptive history accumulates meaning: places where YHWH acted become places where YHWH is taught.
21.16: Ain, Juttah, Beth-shemesh — completing the nine cities from Judah and Simeon for the Aaronite priests. Beth-shemesh (House of the Sun) will later be the destination of the ark when it is returned by the Philistines (1 Sam 6:12-19). The event at Beth-shemesh — where men were struck dead for looking into the ark — demonstrates that the Levitical city designation did not guarantee correct priestly practice; the presence of a Levitical city is the institutional provision, but faithfulness to its covenant function is always contingent.
21.17: Gibeon and Geba from Benjamin — significant sites. Gibeon was the city of the Hivite deception (ch 9) and will become the location of the tabernacle during David’s reign (1 Chr 16:39; 21:29). Geba is a strategic pass in Benjamin’s territory that will appear in later military narratives. The Levitical cities are often at geographic and strategic crossroads — not coincidentally, since the need for priestly presence and legal adjudication would be highest at places of significant movement and exchange.
21.18: Anathoth and Almon from Benjamin — Anathoth is the priestly city where Jeremiah was born (Jer 1:1). The prophet’s identification as “the son of Hilkiah, of the priests who were in Anathoth” (Jer 1:1) places his origin within the Levitical city network.
