Jeremiah’s conflict with the temple establishment and his radical critique of Judah’s covenant failures emerges from within the priestly tradition, not outside it — a priest’s son speaking covenant truth that the temple priests refused to hear.
21.19: kol arei bnei Aharon hakohanim shlosha esre ir umigrasheichen: ‘all the cities of the priestly line of Aaron: thirteen with their pasturelands’ — the summary closes the Aaronite section. Thirteen cities for the priests; the number is not round (unlike the Kohathite non-priestly ten, the Gershonite thirteen, and the Merarite twelve) because priestly cities follow tribal availability. The careful accounting — tribe by tribe, subtotal by subtotal — reflects a genuine administrative record of the Levitical distribution.
21.20: The non-priestly Kohathites begin their section. “The rest of the Kohathite Levites” distinguishes them from Aaron’s descendants: these are the families who carried the ark and the furniture of the inner sanctuary (Num 4:4-15). Their placement in Ephraim, Dan, and half-Manasseh positions the carriers of the ark in the central hill country, which will later be the territory of the divided monarchy’s northern kingdom — a providential distribution for the period when the ark’s significance will be most contested.
21.21: et Shekem ir miqlat laroteach behar Efrayim: ‘they gave them Shechem — a city of refuge — in the hill country of Ephraim’ — Shechem as a non-priestly Kohathite city and city of refuge places the second city of asylum in Ephraim, the dominant northern tribe. Shechem’s long history (Gen 12:6, 33:18, ch 34, the Dinah incident; Josh 24, the covenant renewal) makes it a natural center for the Levitical presence in the north. The assembly of Israel in ch 24 will gather here, and the Levitical presence is the institutional framework within which that assembly is possible.
21.22: Kibzaim and Beth-horon from Ephraim — four cities total for the non-priestly Kohathites in Ephraim. Beth-horon (both Upper and Lower) is a strategic pass connecting the central hills to the coastal plain and the site of significant military actions (Josh 10:10-11; 1 Sam 13:18; 1 Macc 3:16). Levitical cities at strategic passes serve as waypoints of priestly presence along major travel routes.
21.23: Eltekeh and Gibbethon from Dan — cities in the Shephelah (lowland) region between the hill country and the coast. Dan’s territory was notoriously difficult to hold (15:63; Judg 1:34) and eventually caused the Danite migration northward (Judg 18). The Levitical cities in Dan were in a contested zone, and the tribe’s failure to control its territory meant the Levitical infrastructure there was compromised from early on.
21.24: Aijalon and Gath-rimmon from Dan — completing the four Danite cities for the non-priestly Kohathites. Aijalon is the valley where the sun stood still (Josh 10:12). The connection between miraculous divine intervention and Levitical city is characteristic of Joshua’s narrative: the sites of great acts become places where the covenant is taught and the acts are remembered.
21.25: Taanach and Gath-rimmon from half-Manasseh — two cities. The name Gath-rimmon appears twice in vv24-25 (from Dan and from half-Manasseh) and 1 Chronicles 6:69 changes the Manassite city to Ibleam, suggesting a scribal error in one of the two occurrences. The textual variant is one of the few points where the Levitical city lists show divergence between Joshua and 1 Chronicles 6, providing evidence for copyist variation in lists of geographic names.
21.26: kol arim asarah umigrasheichen limishpachot bnei Qehat hanotirim: ‘all the cities of the remaining Kohathite families: ten with their pasturelands’ — the subtotal closes the non-priestly Kohathite section. Ten cities for the carrying-families of the inner sanctuary, plus thirteen for the Aaronite priests, equals twenty-three Kohathite cities total — almost half the forty-eight. The disproportionate Kohathite share reflects their double function: priestly service (Aaron’s line) and ark-bearing (the rest of Kohath).
21.27: ulivnei Gershon mimishpachot haLevi minchatzi shevet haMenashe et Golan baBashan ir miqlat: ‘to the Gershonite Levites, from the other half-tribe of Manasseh: Golan in Bashan — a city of refuge’ — Golan in Bashan is the third city of refuge (of six), placed in the Transjordan. The Gershonites receive the northern and Transjordan territory, distributing the priestly tradition to the areas furthest from the future Jerusalem temple. Golan’s modern prominence (the Golan Heights) reflects its strategic importance, which ancient Israel’s land distribution recognized by placing a Levitical city there.
21.28: Kishon and Daberath from Issachar — cities in the Jezreel Valley. The Jezreel Valley is one of the most fertile and strategically significant regions of ancient Canaan, the site of the battle of Megiddo (Judg 5) and numerous subsequent military engagements. Levitical cities in the Jezreel Valley place priestly presence at the agricultural and military heart of the northern territory.
21.29: Jarmuth and En-gannim from Issachar — completing the four Issachar cities for the Gershonites. En-gannim (“spring of gardens”) near the Jezreel Valley’s access point continues the pattern of Levitical cities at geographically significant locations. The spring (ein) names in the Levitical city list reflect the practical requirement that communities of any size need reliable water sources — a mundane material consideration woven into the sacred institutional structure.
21.30: Mishal and Abdon from Asher — cities in the coastal foothills of the northern coastal territory. Asher’s territory along the Phoenician coast was notoriously difficult to secure from indigenous populations (Judg 1:31-32), and the Levitical cities in Asher were in a mixed zone where the covenant instruction would be most needed and most challenged.
21.31: Helkath and Rehob from Asher — completing the four Asher cities. The Gershonite cities in Asher and Naphtali position the priestly carrying-families in the northernmost tribal territories, the region that will first fall to Assyrian conquest (2 Kings 15:29). The Levitical network in the north is both the institutional provision for those territories and a measure of what was lost when Assyria depopulated them.
21.32: veQedesh baGalil ir miqlat laroteach: ‘Kedesh in Galilee — a city of refuge’ — Kedesh of Galilee is the fourth city of refuge, in the northern territory that Jesus will inhabit (“Galilee of the Gentiles”, Matt 4:15). The city of refuge in Galilee makes the northern territory, with its highest concentration of non-Israelite population, a place where the asylum principle applies. The geographic reach of the miqlat cities — from Hebron in the south to Kedesh in the far north, and from Bezer in the Transjordan to Shechem in the central hill country — reflects the intent that the asylum provision cover all of the land.
21.33: kol arei haGershuni lemishpachtam shlosha asher ir umigrasheichen: ‘all the cities of the Gershonites by their families: thirteen with their pasturelands’ — the Gershonite subtotal matches the Aaronite total (thirteen each), though covering a different geographic range. The northern distribution of the Gershonites creates a priestly presence network across the territories that will later constitute the northern kingdom of Israel — where the eight Levitical cities of the Gershonites should have been centers of covenant instruction preventing the Jeroboam apostasy.
21.34: The Merarites receive cities from Zebulun — beginning the last Levitical clan’s distribution. The Merarites carried the structural components of the tabernacle (boards, bars, pillars, bases) in the wilderness — the heaviest loads, the load-bearing elements. Their cities in Zebulun and Reuben and Gad cover the tribal territories flanking the Jezreel Valley on the north and the Transjordan. Jokneam is at the southwestern entrance to the Jezreel Valley; Kartah is uncertain in location. The structural-carrier families settle in the structural hinge-points of the land’s geography.
21.35: Dimnah and Nahalal from Zebulun — completing the four Zebulun cities for the Merarites. Nahalal is the site from which Zebulun could not drive out the Canaanites (Judg 1:30); a Levitical city in a zone of incomplete conquest is a measure of the ongoing challenge of covenant faithfulness in mixed zones. The Levitical infrastructure existed; the tribal will to maintain separation was inconsistent.
21.36: Bezer and Jahaz from Reuben — Bezer being the fifth city of refuge, in the Transjordan. Bezer (bezer = ore/strong place) in the wilderness of the plateau was the city of asylum for the Transjordan region. Jahaz was the site of Israel’s victory over Sihon king of the Amorites (Num 21:23) — the battle that opened the Transjordan conquest. The location of a Levitical city at a site of decisive divine victory is consistent with the pattern of placing priestly instruction at sites where YHWH acted decisively.
21.37: Kedemoth and Mephaath from Reuben — completing the four Reubenite cities. Kedemoth is associated with Moses’s wilderness preaching (Deut 2:26) and the failed negotiation with Sihon. The accumulation of historic associations in the Levitical city names is significant: these are not arbitrary geographic assignments but sites whose histories are intertwined with Israel’s foundational narrative. The Levitical presence in these cities connects the priestly instruction to the remembered acts of YHWH.
21.38: umimat Gad Ramot baGilad ir miqlat laroteach: ‘from Gad: Ramoth in Gilead — a city of refuge’ — Ramoth Gilead is the sixth and last city of refuge, in the Gadite territory of the Transjordan. Ramoth Gilead will later be the site of repeated Israelite-Aramean conflict (1 Kings 22:1-38; 2 Kings 8:28-29; 9:1-15), the city Ahab dies trying to reclaim and where Elijah anoints Jehu. The city of refuge that was to protect the innocent becomes the site of Ahab’s unjust death — irony within the book’s later history.
21.39: Heshbon and Jazer from Gad — Heshbon being the former capital of Sihon king of the Amorites, whose conquest opened the Transjordan (Num 21:25-26). The assignment of Sihon’s capital to the Merarites as a Levitical city is a transformative act: the seat of pagan kingship becomes a center of Levitical instruction. The same transformation principle governs the broader conquest narrative: places of pagan power are consecrated to YHWH’s service.
21.40: kol ha'arim livnei Merari lemishpachtam hanotirim mimmishpachot haLevi vayehi goralham arim shtem esreh: ‘all the cities assigned to the Merarites by their families — the remaining Levitical families — were twelve’ — the Merarite subtotal closes the clan-by-clan distribution. Twelve cities for the structural-carriers, completing the tallying that has proceeded with administrative precision through the chapter. The four subtotals (13 + 10 + 13 + 12 = 48) account for all the Levitical cities.
21.41: kol arei haLviim bithoch achuzat bnei Yisrael arbaim ushmone ir umigrasheichen: ‘the total number of Levitical cities within Israelite territory: forty-eight cities with their pasturelands’ — the grand total. Forty-eight is four times twelve: four clans (Aaron, non-priestly Kohath, Gershon, Merari) times twelve tribes (or a variant allocation), though the actual distribution is not uniform across tribes. The number’s divisibility by twelve creates a symmetry: the covenant community structured by twelve receives a priestly service network with the same numerical resonance. Numbers as structural markers carry theological weight in the priestly organization of Israel.
21.42: he'arim ha'eleh ir ir umigrasheichen saviv lahem ken lehol he'arim ha'eleh: ‘every one of these cities had its surrounding pasturelands — it was the same for all these cities’ — the uniformity clause closes the administrative section. The migrash (pastureland) attached to every Levitical city ensures that each one is functionally complete: the Levites can maintain their livestock and sustain their communities without dependence on individual tribal hospitality. The structural provision for the priestly tribe is comprehensive and equitable.
21.43: vayiten YHWH leYisrael et kol ha'aretz asher nishba latet la'avotam: ‘the LORD gave Israel all the land that he had sworn to give their ancestors’ — the first of three fulfillment declarations that close chapters 21-23. The land-promise to Abraham (Gen 12:7; 15:18-21; 17:8), Isaac (Gen 26:3), and Jacob (Gen 28:13) is now fulfilled in the tribal allotments and Levitical cities just described. The word nishba (swore by oath) frames the fulfillment in terms of divine fidelity: this is not contingent provision but oath-keeping.
21.44: vayanuach YHWH lahem misaviv kekhol asher nishba la'avotam: ‘the LORD gave them rest on every side, just as he had sworn to their ancestors’ — the second fulfillment declaration introduces menuha (rest, the same word as Deut 12:9 and the Sabbath-rest motif that runs through the OT). The promise was not just land but rest in the land — cessation of the warfare and displacement that characterized the wilderness generation. The rest here is partial and provisional (the incompletely conquered Canaanites remain); Hebrews 4:8 argues that the incompleteness of Joshua’s rest proves a greater rest remains.
21.45: lo nafal davar mikol hadavar hatov asher diber YHWH el beit Yisrael hakol ba: ‘not one word of all the good things that YHWH spoke concerning the house of Israel failed; all came to pass’ — the third and most comprehensive fulfillment declaration. Lo nafal davar (not one word fell) uses the verb nafal (fall) metaphorically: words are things that stand or fall; YHWH’s words stand forever (Isa 40:8: “the word of our God will stand forever”). Joshua 21:45 is the OT’s most comprehensive testimony to divine promise-faithfulness, and 2 Cor 1:20 (“all the promises of God find their Yes in him”) is its NT equivalent and ultimate basis.

22.1: Joshua summons the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh — the Transjordan tribes who had agreed to help with the conquest before receiving their territory east of the Jordan (Num 32; Josh 1:12-18). The formal summoning signals a formal occasion: this is the dismissal ceremony, the completion of the obligation. The narrative takes the word-keeping of these tribes as the model of covenant fidelity to be honored and commended before the whole assembly.
22.2: atem shemartem et kol asher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH etchem: ‘you have kept all that Moses the servant of YHWH commanded you’ — Joshua’s opening commendation is total: et kol, all that was commanded. This is the highest possible evaluation — complete covenant fidelity. The Transjordan tribes, who might have been tempted to return to their own territory once it was secured, chose instead to fulfill their commitment through the years of the western conquest. Their loyalty is now formally recognized before the whole congregation.
22.3: lo azavtem et acheichem zeh yamim rabim ad hayom hazeh: ‘you have not deserted your brothers these many days, right up to this day’ — the time qualification (“these many days”) acknowledges the sacrifice involved: years away from home, wives, children, and livestock in their own territory, while serving in the western campaign. Faithfulness that endures over time, not just in the initial enthusiasm of commitment, is the quality Joshua commends. The same quality governs the NT call to perseverance: “let us not grow weary of doing good” (Gal 6:9).
22.4: veattah heniyach YHWH Eloheichem le'acheichem ka'asher diber lahem: ‘now the LORD your God has given rest to your brothers as he promised them’ — the dismissal is grounded in divine fulfillment: the rest has been given. The Transjordan tribes can go home not because they chose to leave early but because the purpose of their service is complete. Joshua’s release of them is simultaneous with YHWH’s fulfillment of the rest-promise. Timing the release to the fulfillment of the purpose is itself an act of covenant honor.
22.5: rak shimru me'od la'asot et hamitzvah ve'et hatorah asher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH le'ahavah et YHWH Eloheichem ulelechet bekol derachav velishmor mitzvotav udovqah vo ule'ovdo bechol levavchem uvechol nafshchem: ‘only be very careful to observe the commandment and the law… to love YHWH your God, walk in all his ways, keep his commandments, cling to him, and serve him with all your heart and soul’ — the parting charge is the fullest statement of covenant obligation in Joshua, a near-verbatim anticipation of the Shema’s full form. The five verbs (love, walk, keep, cling, serve) cover the full range of covenantal response. Dabaq (cling) is the same verb used in Gen 2:24 (“a man shall leave his father and mother and cling to his wife”) — covenant faithfulness is as intimate as marriage.
22.6: vayevarechem Yehoshua vayishlachem vayelechu el ohaleihem: ‘Joshua blessed them and sent them on their way, and they went to their tents’ — the blessing and dismissal completes the formal ceremony. The Transjordan tribes depart with Joshua’s blessing — the same blessing that Moses gave in Deut 33 before his death. The narrative places Joshua in the Moses-role: the one who blesses the departing people at the completion of the redemptive act.
22.7: The narrative notes a particular complexity: Moses had given the eastern half of Manasseh land in Bashan, but Joshua had given the western half of Manasseh land in Canaan proper. The same tribe straddles both sides of the Jordan — a geographic expression of the mixed situation of the Transjordan arrangement. The blessing was for both halves, the charge was the same, but the territories are separated by the river that Israel crossed as a covenant act.
22.8: shuvú el ohaleihem becharmishah rabah: ‘return to your homes with great wealth’ — the dismissal includes material provision: livestock, silver, gold, bronze, iron, clothing — the plunder of the western campaign divided with the Transjordanites. The instruction to divide the plunder with your brothers is a cross-reference to the principle of Num 31:27 (split the plunder between the soldiers who went to battle and the rest of the congregation). Those who serve on the front lines share their gain with the community; those who remain share with those who went.
22.9: The Transjordan tribes depart from Shiloh — the tabernacle’s location in the west — and cross back into Gilead, their own territory. The phrase “the land of their possession, which they received according to the commandment of YHWH through Moses” emphasizes the legitimacy of their eastern territory: it was YHWH’s assignment through Moses, not a second-best compromise. The narrative is careful to not treat the Transjordan settlement as a failure or an inferior arrangement.
22.10: vayavou el gelilot haYarden asher be'eretz Kena'an vayivnu bnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachazi shevet haMenashe sham mizbeach al haYarden: ‘they came to the region of the Jordan in the land of Canaan and built an altar there by the Jordan’ — the altar is built on the Canaanite side of the Jordan (“the land of Canaan”) before crossing over — deliberately visible to the western tribes. The location matters: it is not hidden in Gilead but positioned as a visible marker at the border. The intent (as revealed in v24-28) is to be seen by both sides.
22.11: The report reaches the Israelites: the Transjordan tribes have built an altar “at the frontier of the land of Canaan in the region about the Jordan, on the side belonging to the people of Israel” — the geography of the altar’s location is already potentially inflammatory. An altar on the Canaanite side of the Jordan could mean an attempt to establish a rival sacrificial site, directly violating the centralization principle of Deut 12 (you shall not offer your burnt offerings in every place that you see).
22.12: vayishme'u bnei Yisrael vayiqahel kol adat bnei Yisrael Shiloh la'alot aleihem latzava: ‘the entire assembly of the Israelites gathered at Shiloh to go up against them in war’ — the immediate response of the western tribes is military: the altar constitutes a potential covenant breach serious enough to justify civil war. The assembly at Shiloh — the location of the tabernacle — grounds the military threat in covenantal authority: the community gathers at the place of YHWH’s presence to prosecute YHWH’s cause. The speed of the response demonstrates how seriously the covenant centralization of worship was taken.
22.13: Before going to war, the Israelites send a delegation — Phinehas and ten leaders. The substitution of diplomacy for immediate war is itself a covenantal act: the law required attempted reconciliation before conflict. Phinehas is the ideal delegate: he is the priest who demonstrated covenantal zeal at Peor (Num 25:7-8) and whose covenant of peace (Num 25:12-13) made him YHWH’s faithful instrument against covenant violation. He represents both the prosecution of covenant faithfulness and the possibility of restoration.
22.14: The ten leaders — one from each tribe of Israel — represent the covenantal whole in the delegation. The number ten for the tribal representation alongside Phinehas reflects the structure of Numbers 13-14, where tribal representatives went ahead. The delegation is an act of solidarity: the covenant community acts together, through its representative leaders, rather than through mob action or unilateral military decision.
22.15: The delegation arrives in Gilead and addresses the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-Manasseh. The dramatic confrontation is between Israelites — not between Israel and Canaanites. The danger of the situation is precisely that the covenant community is fracturing from within. The altar at the Jordan, whatever its intent, has created a crisis of communal solidarity that is more threatening than external enemies.
22.16: mah hama'al hazeh asher me'altem be'Elohei Yisrael: ‘what is this breach of faith (ma'al) that you have committed against the God of Israel?’ — ma'al is the same term used of Achan’s theft (7:1) and of unauthorized use of sacred things in Leviticus 5. The accusation is specific and serious: this is not mere civil conflict but a trespass against the sacred. The delegation presents the strongest possible interpretation of the altar’s significance — a charitable impulse requires giving the accused the chance to explain, not presuming guilt — while naming what would be true if the worst interpretation were correct.
22.17: hameut lanu et avon Peor: ‘is the sin of Peor too small for us?’ — the reference to the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25) invokes the most recent devastating covenant failure, which killed 24,000 people and which Phinehas himself had stopped with his spear. The delegation’s appeal to Peor’s memory is simultaneously a warning (that’s what covenant violation produces) and a confession: “we have not fully cleansed ourselves of it.” The community’s moral accountability for past covenant failures is presented as an ongoing weight.
22.18: veattim tashuvu hayom me'acharei YHWH vehayah atem tomredu hayom baYHWH umachar el kol adat Yisrael yiqtzof: ‘and if you rebel against the LORD today, tomorrow he will be angry at the whole assembly of Israel’ — the delegation articulates the principle of corporate solidarity in covenant: one tribe’s sin affects the whole assembly. This is not arbitrary collective punishment but the nature of covenant membership: the community is one body, and infection in one part spreads to the whole (cf. 1 Cor 5:6: “a little leaven leavens the whole lump”). The concern for the whole assembly’s welfare drives the confrontation.
22.19: ve'im teme'ah artzechem ivru lachem el eretz achuzat YHWH asher shaken bo mishkan YHWH: ‘if your land is unclean, come over into the land of the LORD’s possession where the LORD’s tabernacle dwells’ — the remarkable offer: if the Transjordan territory cannot support proper worship, the western tribes will make room for you among us. The offer reveals that the delegation’s primary concern is covenant fidelity, not territorial defensiveness. The principle that access to YHWH’s tabernacle matters more than tribal territory is a covenant priority that the NT confirms: “do not give up meeting together” (Heb 10:25) — proximity to the assembly of God’s people is worth significant sacrifice.
22.20: halo Achan ben Zerah ma'al ma'al bachareim veal kol adat Yisrael hayah qatzef: ‘did not Achan son of Zerah commit a trespass regarding the devoted things, and wrath came upon the whole congregation of Israel?’ — the Achan precedent is invoked precisely: one man’s ma'al brought YHWH’s anger upon the whole assembly, and “he was not the only one who died for his iniquity.” The delegation is not threatening the Transjordan tribes but warning them about the corporate consequences of what YHWH’s judgment looks like when covenant breach goes unaddressed.
22.21: vaya'anu bnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachazi shebet haMenashe: the Transjordan tribes answer the delegation — their response will be decisive for the outcome. The three-tribe unity in the response mirrors the three-tribe unity of the altar’s construction; they act as one in explanation as they did in building. The answer begins with the solemn divine invocation of v22 before the explanation of v24.
22.22: el Elohim YHWH el Elohim YHWH hu yodea veYisrael hu yeda: ‘The Mighty One, God, the LORD! The Mighty One, God, the LORD! He knows, and let Israel know!’ — the triple divine name invocation (el, elohim, YHWH) is the most solemn oath formula in the text. The repetition of the triple name twice creates a six-element invocation of divine witness. The declaration “he knows” (hu yodea) appeals to divine omniscience as the ultimate court of appeal: if they have sinned, God knows; if they have not sinned, God knows. The accusation that requires YHWH as witness is the highest possible formal denial.
22.23: The denial is categorical: if the altar was for burnt offerings or grain offerings or sacrifices — if it was an attempt to establish an independent sacrificial site — then “may the LORD himself call us to account.” The conditional self-imprecation (calling YHWH’s judgment on themselves if they are guilty) is the formal structure of oath-taking in ancient Near Eastern legal contexts. By swearing this oath, they place themselves under divine judgment for lying — the highest possible guarantee of truthfulness.
22.24: ume'da'agah midavar patzinu et zot: ‘but we did this out of anxiety (midavar da'agah), for a specific reason’ — the anxiety is about future exclusion: “your children may in time say to our children, ‘What have you to do with the LORD, the God of Israel?’” The concern is intergenerational covenant exclusion — the fear that the Jordan river will become a theological boundary as well as a geographic one. The future-generation concern reveals covenantal thinking: they are building for their descendants’ covenant security, not for their own present advantage.
22.25: gevul sam YHWH beinenu uveneichem bnei Reuven uvnei Gad et haYarden ein lachem cheleq baYHWH: ‘the LORD has made the Jordan a border between us and you — you have no portion in the LORD’ — this is the feared future exclusion, put in the mouths of the western tribes’ children. The Jordan as theological boundary rather than merely geographic boundary would mean that the Transjordan tribes’ covenant status depends on their geographic location rather than their YHWH-allegiance. The altar is a physical counter-argument to this feared interpretation.
22.26: vayomru nivneh lanu et mizbeach lo le'olah velo lezevach: ‘we said, let us build an altar — but not for burnt offerings or sacrifices’ — the explicit denial that the altar is for sacrifice distinguishes it from a rival sanctuary. The altar as witness rather than altar as worship-site is a category that Deuteronomy 12 does not address: the centralization law prohibits sacrificial worship outside the chosen place, not commemorative or testimonial monuments. The Transjordan tribes are drawing a legal distinction between a sacrificial altar and a witness-altar.
22.27: ki ed hu beinenu uveneichem uvein doreinu acharenu la'avod et avodat YHWH lefanav: ‘it is a witness between us and you and between our generations after us, that we do perform the service of the LORD’ — the altar as ed (witness/testimony) is a category familiar from ancient Near Eastern treaty monuments: masseboth (standing stones) and boundary markers served as covenant witnesses (cf. Gen 31:44-52, Laban and Jacob’s witness-heap). The altar’s function is not sacrificial but testimonial — a physical declaration of covenant participation available for future generations to point to.
22.28: veyomru eleynu ue'el doreinu machar veamarnu re'u et tavnit mizbeach YHWH: ‘and in the future when they say to us or to our descendants: look, the copy of the altar of YHWH’ — the witness-altar is designed as an argument for intergenerational covenant inclusion. If future children ask “what right do you have?”, the altar is the answer: our fathers built this as a copy of the altar of YHWH; we have the same relationship to the God of Israel as the western tribes. The altar is a piece of tangible evidence that can be pointed to in a dispute about covenant standing.
22.29: chalilah lanu mimreod laYHWH ulesubvav hayom me'acharei YHWH livnot mizbeach le'olah laminchah velazevach milevad mizbeach YHWH Eloheinu asher lifnei mishkano: ‘far be it from us to rebel against the LORD and turn away from him today, to build an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices, other than the altar of the LORD our God that stands before his tabernacle!’ — the conclusion is the categorical denial combined with the affirmation: there is one altar, the one before the tabernacle; we have not built another. The chalilah lanu (far be it from us) is the strongest possible Hebrew expression of rejection and horror at the suggested possibility.
22.30: Phinehas and the leaders heard the Transjordan tribes’ explanation and were pleased. The narrative satisfaction of the delegation’s acceptance is the resolution of the tension built since v12. The theological issue (potential covenant breach) is resolved by the Transjordan tribes’ explanation; the community is not divided. Phinehas — who stopped the Peor apostasy with violent action — here is the instrument of reconciliation through hearing and discernment. Both actions serve covenant fidelity; the choice between them depends on what the situation actually requires.
22.31: hayom yadanu ki betochenu YHWH asher lo me'altem baYHWH hama'al hazeh az hitzaltem et bnei Yisrael miyad YHWH: ‘today we know that the LORD is among us, because you have not committed this breach of faith against the LORD — now you have rescued the people of Israel from the hand of the LORD’ — Phinehas’s declaration reads the Transjordan tribes’ faithfulness as evidence of divine presence among the whole people. Their non-sin is not merely a private relief but a communal rescue: the whole assembly was in danger of divine wrath for a ma'al that turned out not to have occurred. Faithfulness in a potential crisis situation rescues more than the faithful — it protects the whole community.
22.32: Phinehas and the leaders return from Gilead to Canaan and bring back a good report to the Israelites. The narrative structure is complete: crisis (v10-12), investigation (v13-29), resolution (v30-31), report (v32-33). The structure mirrors diplomatic and legal processes that are recognizable across cultures: confrontation, inquiry, adjudication, reconciliation. The covenant community resolves its internal crisis through process, not through preemptive violence.
22.33: vayitav hadavar be'einei bnei Yisrael vayevarachu Elohim bnei Yisrael: ‘the report pleased the Israelites, and they praised God’ — the community’s response to the resolution is worship, not merely relief. The conflict that threatened civil war is resolved by the Transjordan tribes’ fidelity, and the proper response is praise to YHWH. The connection between covenant resolution and worship is consistent: “Therefore, if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you… first go and be reconciled to your brother, then come and offer your gift” (Matt 5:23-24).
22.34: vayiqre'u livnei Reuven uvnei Gad et hamizbeach ki ed hu veinenu: ‘the Reubenites and Gadites named the altar Ed — “Witness” — saying, “It is a witness between us that the LORD is God’” — the naming closes the narrative with the altar’s function permanently inscribed in its name. The altar named Ed (witness) stands as a permanent testimony to covenant unity across the Jordan — not a rival worship site but a monument to the shared covenant that transcends the river boundary. Every future generation that sees the altar knows: the Transjordan tribes are part of the covenant people of YHWH.

23.1: vayehi miyamim rabim achar asher heniyach YHWH leYisrael mikol oyveihem misaviv: ‘a long time afterward, when the LORD had given rest to Israel from all their surrounding enemies’ — Joshua’s farewell speech is set in old age, after the rest has been established. The menuha (rest) is both military and covenantal: the land is secured, the Levitical cities distributed, the covenant renewed at Ebal. Joshua speaks from the vantage point of accomplished mission. Hebrews 4:8 cites this rest as insufficient: “for if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on.” The rest of the completed conquest points beyond itself.
23.2: Joshua summons all Israel — their elders, heads, judges, and officers — the full leadership structure of the covenant community. The formality of the summons matches the gravity of what follows: a farewell speech of a leader who has completed his work, knows he is dying, and needs to leave the community with the essential word. The succession of Moses and Joshua both end with farewell addresses (Deut 31-34; Josh 23-24): the pattern of the dying leader’s final testimony is foundational to the covenant tradition.
23.3: atem re'item et kol asher asah YHWH Eloheichem lekhol hagoyim ha'eleh mifinechem ki YHWH Eloheichem hu hanilochem lachem: ‘you yourselves have seen all that the LORD your God has done to all these nations — for it is the LORD your God who has fought for you’ — the appeal to direct experience (atem re'item, you yourselves have seen) grounds the farewell in testimony rather than argument. Joshua does not deduce or reason to divine faithfulness; he points to what the community witnessed. This is the consistent biblical mode of covenant instruction: redemptive history is not a doctrine to be accepted but a testimony to be received from witnesses.
23.4: re'u hipeladeti lachem et hagoyim hanorotim ha'eleh lenachalah leshivtechem: ‘I have allotted to you as an inheritance for your tribes these remaining nations’ — Joshua distinguishes the nations already defeated from those that remain. The promised land has been allotted even though not all of it is yet secured; Israel holds the land by right of divine allocation, not by the completion of military conquest. This is the “already and not yet” structure: the inheritance is theirs by divine gift, fully realized through ongoing obedience.
23.5: vaYHWH Eloheichem hu yehdofem mifinechem veyorish otam mifinechem vishatem et artzam ka'asher diber lahem YHWH Eloheichem: ‘the LORD your God will himself drive them out from before you and dispossess them — you shall possess their land, as the LORD your God promised you’ — the future completion of what is already begun rests on divine initiative (hu yehdofem, he himself will drive them). Israel’s task is faithfulness; the driving-out is YHWH’s action. The distinction between human covenant fidelity and divine accomplishment is maintained even in the military language.
23.6: vachazaqtem me'od lishmor vela'asot et kol hakatuv besefer torat Moshe lebilti sur mimenu yamin usmol: ‘be very strong to keep and to do all that is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, turning aside from it neither to the right nor to the left’ — Joshua echoes his own commission (1:7: “do not turn from it to the right or to the left”). The charge he received at YHWH’s call he now passes to all Israel at his death. The sefer torat Moshe (Book of the Law of Moses) is the written Torah as the permanent deposit of covenantal obligation. Obedience to written Torah — not to oral tradition or to national interest — is the standard.
23.7: lebilti lavo bago'im ha'eleh hanorotim ha'eleh ushvem lo tizkiru: ‘so that you may not mix with these nations remaining among you, nor make mention of the names of their gods’ — the separation principle: not merely avoiding worship of other gods, but not even invoking their names. The prohibition on naming (lo tizkiru) reflects the covenantal significance of naming: to invoke a name is to acknowledge a power, to enter a relationship of dependence. The same principle appears in Paul’s instruction: “whatever is true, whatever is honorable… think about these things” (Phil 4:8) — cognitive separateness from false allegiances precedes behavioral separateness.
23.8: ki im beYHWH Eloheichem tidbaqun ka'asher asitem ad hayom hazeh: ‘but you shall cling to the LORD your God, as you have done to this day’ — dabaq (cling) is the verb of intimate attachment: Gen 2:24 (“a man shall cling to his wife”), Ruth 1:14 (“Ruth clung to her”). The covenant relationship with YHWH requires the same total, exclusive, intimate attachment as marriage. The phrase “as you have done to this day” is both commendation and standard: continue what has characterized you so far. The farewell speech assumes continuity with the achieved faithfulness of the conquest generation.
23.9: vayorish YHWH mifinechem goyim gedolim ve'atsumim: ‘the LORD drove out before you great and strong nations’ — the completed-tense summary of what YHWH did is the basis for what YHWH will continue to do. The God who drove out great nations for Israel is not less capable now; the appeal to divine track record is an appeal to divine character, which does not change. The rhetorical function is identical to Paul’s argument in Rom 8:32: “He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?”
23.10: ish echad miachem yirdof elef ki YHWH Eloheichem hu hanilochem lachem ka'asher diber lachem: ‘one man of you puts a thousand to flight, because the LORD your God fights for you, as he promised you’ — the arithmetic of divine empowerment: one warrior plus YHWH equals a thousand opponents. The mathematics is not military but covenantal — it measures not force-ratios but the asymmetry between human weakness and divine strength. Lev 26:8 promises the same disproportionate effectiveness: “five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand.”
23.11: venishmartem me'od le'nafshoteichem le'ahavah et YHWH Eloheichem: ‘be very careful to love the LORD your God’ — the charge to love (ahavah) frames covenant obedience as affection, not merely legal compliance. The Shema’s command to love YHWH with all heart, soul, and strength (Deut 6:5) is the ground of all specific obligations; Joshua’s farewell charge returns to this foundation. The specific dangers (vv12-13) follow from this positive command: they are threats to the love-relationship, not merely to legal standing.
23.12: vechi shuv tashuvu udevaqtem beyeter hagoyim ha'eleh hanorotim ha'eleh itchem uhitchatantem bahem uveim bahem veyim bachem: ‘for if you turn back and cling to the remnant of these nations remaining among you and make marriages with them’ — the prohibited mixing is precisely at the point where covenant love is expressed most intimately: marriage. The Deuteronomic prohibition of intermarriage (Deut 7:3-4) is grounded not in ethnic purity but in covenant fidelity: intermarriage with those who worship other gods will turn your heart away (Deut 7:4). Dabaq (cling) appears here negatively — clinging to the nations rather than to YHWH is the betrayal of the marital metaphor of v8.
23.13: yada tede'u ki lo yoshif YHWH Eloheichem lehoresh et hagoyim ha'eleh mifinechem vehay lahem lepach ulemokesh uleshot betzidchem ulecicotzim be'einechem: ‘know for certain that the LORD your God will no longer drive out these nations before you — they shall be a snare and a trap for you, a whip on your sides and thorns in your eyes until you perish from this good land’ — the covenantal warning is vivid and cumulative: pach (snare), mokesh (trap), shot (whip), tzikotzim (thorns). The fourfold image of entanglement, blinding, and pain captures the progressive degradation of a community that allows idolatrous influence into its life. The language is repeated in Judg 2:3, where the fulfillment of the warning is announced.
23.14: vehinei anochi holech hayom bederech kol ha'aretz veyeda'atem bechol levavchem uvechol nafshechem ki lo nafal davar echad mikol hadvarim hatovim asher diber YHWH Eloheichem aleichem hakol ba lachem: ‘behold, I am going today the way of all the earth, and you know in your hearts and souls, all of you, that not one word has failed of all the good things that the LORD your God promised concerning you — all have come to pass’ — Joshua’s death-announcement (“I am going today the way of all the earth”) precedes the final testimony. The dying man’s witness to divine faithfulness is the most credible: a person about to stand before YHWH has no reason to exaggerate divine goodness. Lo nafal davar (not one word fell) — repeating 21:45 at the farewell speech’s climax — is the book’s central claim and theological heart.
23.15: vehayah ka'asher ba aleichem kol hadavar hatov asher diber YHWH Eloheichem aleichem ken yavi YHWH aleichem et kol hadavar hara ad hashmiido otchem me'al ha'adamah hatovah hazot asher natan lachem: ‘just as all the good things that the LORD your God promised concerning you have come to pass, so the LORD will bring upon you all the evil things until he has destroyed you from off this good land’ — the covenant is symmetric: the same divine faithfulness that fulfilled the blessings will fulfill the curses. This is not punitive cruelty but the consistent application of covenant logic: YHWH’s word is reliable in both directions. Hebrews 10:28-29 applies the same logic to the new covenant: if Torah-violation brought certain judgment, how much more will rejection of the Son.
23.16: baavrechem et brit YHWH Eloheichem asher tzivah etchem vahalacitem va'avaditem elohim acherim vehishtachavitem lahem vacharah af YHWH bachem ve'avadtem meherah me'al ha'aretz hatovah asher natan lachem: ‘if you transgress the covenant of the LORD your God, which he commanded you, and go and serve other gods and bow down to them — then the anger of the LORD will be kindled against you, and you shall perish quickly from off the good land that he has given you’ — the final verse of the farewell speech is the conditional that governs everything: covenant transgression → idolatry → divine anger → loss of the land. The entire history of Israel from Joshua to the exile is the story of this conditional being fulfilled. The “good land” (ha'aretz hatovah) frames both the gift (v15, asher natan lachem) and the potential loss: what is given as covenant gift is forfeited by covenant breach. The farewell ends where it began — with the covenant as the absolute center of Israel’s existence.

18.1: wayyiqqāhălû kol-ʿăḏat bənê-yiśrāʾēl šilōh — 'the whole assembly of Israel gathered at Shiloh.' The transfer of the cult center from Gilgal (where the Jordan-crossing monuments stood) to Shiloh marks a new phase: the tent of meeting (ʾōhel môʿēd H168) is erected in permanent territory. nikkəbāšāh hāʾāreṣ (H3533) — 'the land was subdued/conquered'; kābaš carries the sense of trampling down, bringing under foot — not just military defeat but enforced submission (cf. Gen 1:28 where Adam is commanded to kibšūhā, subdue the earth). The use here closes the conquest narrative: Canaan is under Israel's foot at last.
18.2: wayyiwwāṯərû bənê-yiśrāʾēl šebaʿ šəḇāṭîm ʾăšer lōʾ-ḥāleqû ʾet-naḥălāṯām — 'seven tribes of Israel remained who had not divided their inheritance.' yāṯar (H3498) means to remain over, be left as a surplus — a residual. The verb ḥālaQ (H2505, divide/apportion) is the dominant verb of the allotment chapters. The nāḥălāh (H5159, inheritance) they have not divided is legally theirs — the delay is procrastination, not dispossession.
18.3: Joshua's rebuke: ʿad-mātay ʾattem mitrappîm lāḇōʾ lārešet ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — 'until when are you slack/negligent about going in to possess the land?' rāpāh (H7503) means to let droop, to become slack — related to the idiom 'let your hands fall' (cf. Jer 6:24). Joshua uses the same verb as Moses's critics used against him (Exod 5:8,17 — 'you are idle, idle!'). The irony: Israel was enslaved for reluctance to work; now they are free and reluctant to claim their freedom's full fruit. The land is a gift they must actively receive.
18.4: Joshua directs each tribe to appoint three men to survey the remaining land and write its description (kāṯaḇ H3789) in a book (sēfer H5612). The written survey is a legal document — transforming geographic description into title deed. The three-per-tribe delegation (21 men for 7 tribes) ensures proportional tribal representation in the assessment process.
18.5: The land is to be divided (ḥālaQ) into seven portions, with Judah retaining the south and Joseph the north. The prior allotments of the two dominant tribes serve as fixed boundaries within which the remaining seven must be assigned. The spatial constraint drives the need for careful measurement and proportional assignment.
18.6: wəʾānōkî ʾašlîk lākem gôrāl pōh lipnê YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — 'I myself will cast the lot for you here before YHWH our God.' gôrāl (H1486) is the lot — a stone, stick, or pebble used for decision-making. The mechanism transfers human preference entirely to divine determination: lōʾ-ḥālaQ libnê yiśrāʾēl would mean human parceling; the lot means YHWH parcels. The phrase lipnê YHWH (before YHWH) at the tent of meeting is the critical locator: this is a covenant ceremony, not a land survey.
18.7: The Levites have no portion (ḥēleq H2506) among the tribes 'because the priesthood of YHWH is their inheritance (nāḥălātām).' The Levitical exclusion from land is the deepest expression of priestly theology: God himself, mediated through sacrificial service, is their possession. ḥēleq and nāḥălāh are near synonyms here, both meaning share/allotment — the point is that Levi's share is non-territorial.
18.8: The men commissioned to survey the land go out and walk through it (hālak H1980), writing descriptions (kāṯaḇ) by cities into seven sections (ḥālaqîm). The verb šūṭ (to rove, range across) is not used here; rather hālak, to walk — a deliberate, methodical traversal. The written record anticipates the modern cadastral survey: a formal inventory of territory enabling lawful distribution.
18.9: The men traverse the land and describe it in a document with seven divisions, then return to Joshua at Shiloh. The practical geography — towns, boundaries, watersheds — is recorded to enable the lot to be applied to real territory. Without accurate description, the lot's result would be unimplementable. The combination of human survey and divine lot models the integration of careful human work and divine sovereignty.
18.10: wayyapîl yəhôšuaʿ gôrāl lahem bəšilōh lipnê YHWH — 'Joshua cast the lot for them at Shiloh before YHWH.' The lot is cast (nāpal H5307, lit. 'to fall') — the lots 'fall' rather than are 'chosen,' emphasizing that the outcome descends rather than rises. The sanctuary location (lipnê YHWH) converts the lot into a divine verdict. Prov 16:33: 'The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH.'
18.11: Benjamin's lot 'came up' (wayyaʿal gôrāl H5927) — the lot fell between the territory of Judah and the territory of Joseph. The positional description — geographically sandwiched — accurately describes Benjamin's narrow north-south strip. The language 'came up' for the lot suggests the lot was drawn from a container (shaken in a fold of cloth and drawn out).
18.12: Benjamin's northern boundary is described from the Jordan westward: wayyaʿal gəḇûlām (the boundary 'goes up,' H1366 H5927) — geographic language treats territory as a three-dimensional surface, with 'going up' meaning moving toward higher elevation or toward the watershed. gəḇûl (H1366, boundary/border) is the key term of these chapters — the line that defines the inherited portion.
18.13: The boundary descends (yāraḏ H3381) toward Luz (Bethel) and passes toward Ataroth-addar. The alternating 'going up' and 'going down' language tracks the actual topography of the central hill country. Bethel — where Jacob saw the ladder (Gen 28:19) — is on Benjamin's border with Ephraim, and its history of divine encounter gives this boundary geographic and theological weight.
18.14: The western boundary turns southward from Kiriath-baal (= Kiriath-jearim, where the ark will later rest, 1 Sam 7:2). The identification of a city by two names — its pre-Israelite name and its Israelite name — is a feature of these chapters, preserving geographic memory across the conquest transition.
18.15: The southern boundary of Benjamin runs from the edge of Kiriath-jearim westward to the spring of the Waters of Nephtoah (likely Lifta north of Jerusalem). The precise boundary description — springs, valleys, and identifiable landmarks — reflects survey accuracy preserved across centuries of transmission.
18.16: The boundary goes down to the foot of the hill facing the Valley of Ben Hinnom, north of the Valley of Rephaim. The Valley of Ben Hinnom (gêʾ ben-hinnōm) will become Gehinnom in later Jewish usage — the dump where refuse burned — and eventually lend its name to the NT concept of hell. Here it is simply a topographic marker on Benjamin's southern border.
18.17: The boundary curves from En-shemesh (Spring of the Sun) to En-rogel (Fuller's Spring) — two named springs that served as permanent geographic anchors. Spring names encode function or history; ʿên (H5869/H5878, spring) is the most reliable landmark in an arid landscape, unchanging across centuries.
18.18: The boundary continues over the slope opposite the Arabah, then descends to the Arabah. The Arabah (hāʿărāḇāh H6160) is the Jordan Rift Valley — the deepest geographic feature of the region. This corner of Benjamin's territory touches the eastern lowlands, giving the tribe access to multiple ecological zones.
18.19: The boundary passes to the north shore of the Salt Sea (yām hammeleḥ H3220 H4417) at the mouth of the Jordan. The Salt Sea (Dead Sea) terminates Benjamin's southeastern corner. meleḥ (salt) describes its chemical character; the same term appears in Ezek 47:8-11 where the Dead Sea is healed but its salt-marshes preserved.
18.20: The Jordan forms the eastern boundary. The four-sided description is complete: north = ridge above Beth-aven, west = Kiriath-jearim ridge, south = Ben Hinnom / Jordan mouth, east = the Jordan. The territorial precision serves both legal and theological functions — the lot's result is recorded as binding covenant deed.
18.21: The cities of the tribe of Benjamin are now listed by name. The distinction between gəḇûl (boundary, the linear border) and ʿārîm (cities, the discrete settlements within the territory) marks the transition from boundary description to town inventory — two complementary ways of defining inheritance.
18.22: Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Bethel — the city list begins with places whose names echo broader biblical narratives. Bethel (house of God, H1008) appears both as a boundary marker (vv13) and as a city in the list, reflecting its central importance as a cult site at the tribal border.
18.23: Avvim, Parah, Ophrah — cities in the central hill country. Ophrah will later be associated with Gideon (Judg 6:11), demonstrating how these town-list chapters of Joshua provide the geographic foundation for the narratives of Judges. The inheritance that seems merely administrative carries the seeds of the subsequent history.
18.24: Chephir-ammoni and Ophni — two more Benjaminite towns. The town list format (ʿārîm with no narrative) is legal, not literary; it functions as title record. The names' preservation across centuries of transmission — even where origins are obscure — reflects the scribal care given to covenant-land records.
18.25: Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth — notable cities. Gibeon (H1391) is where the Gibeonites made their peace with Israel (Josh 9) and where YHWH gives Solomon wisdom (1 Kgs 3:4-5). Ramah (H7414, height) is the birth and burial city of Samuel (1 Sam 1:19; 25:1). Benjamin's town list is simultaneously a legal catalog and a narrative forecast.
18.26: Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah — Mizpeh (H4709, watchtower/lookout) becomes one of the major tribal gathering places in Judges and Samuel (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 7:5-12; 10:17). The naming of a watchtower-city points to the defensive topography that characterized Benjamin's territory on the edge of the central ridge.
18.27: Rekem, Irpeel, Taralah — the list continues. These cities in Benjamin's territory fill out the picture of a densely settled, strategically located tribe — sitting athwart the main routes between north and south, between the Mediterranean coast and the Jordan Valley.
18.28: Zelah, Haeleph, Jebus (that is Jerusalem), Gibeah, and Kiriath — fourteen cities in total. The identification of Yəḇûs (H2982) as Jerusalem (Yərûšālāyim) is significant: Jerusalem is listed as a Benjaminite city, though it remained Jebusite until David's capture (2 Sam 5:6-9). Its inclusion here as an uncaptured city within Benjamin's allotment sets up the canonical anticipation of its eventual conquest and theological centrality.

19.1: The second lot (gôrāl šēnî) comes out for Simeon. The ordinal 'second' implies the lots were drawn in sequence, each new lot determining who comes next. Simeon's lot falls bəṯôk naḥălat bənê-yəhûḏāh — 'within the inheritance of the children of Judah.' The preposition bəṯôk (H8432, in the midst of, inside) signals immediately that this allotment is a sub-grant within a prior one — anomalous from the start.
19.2: Simeon's cities begin with Beersheba (bəʾēr šebaʿ H884 — Well of Seven or Well of the Oath), the southernmost city of Israel in the formulaic 'from Dan to Beersheba' (Judg 20:1). The patriarchal associations of Beersheba are dense: Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Gen 21:31), Isaac's altar (Gen 26:23-25), Jacob's departure for Egypt (Gen 46:1). Simeon inherits the southernmost sacred geography.
19.3: Hazar-shual (H2705, Fox-enclosure), Balah, and Azem — the city list for Simeon continues with mostly Negeb towns. The place names often encode ecological reality: ḥāṣēr (H2691, enclosure/settlement) appears in many compound names, describing the settled villages of the Negeb where semi-nomadic clans established permanent sites.
19.4: Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah — Hormah (H2767, H2763 root: ḥāram, devoted to destruction) marks a site that Israel vowed to destroy (Num 21:3). The name preserves the memory of a ḥērem (sacred destruction) vow. When Simeon inherits this city, the vow has already been fulfilled — the name now is a memorial of obedience rather than an ongoing commitment.
19.5: Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susah — Ziklag (H6860) will later be given to David by the Philistine king Achish (1 Sam 27:6) — at that point a city not yet fully under Israelite control. Its inclusion in Simeon's list reflects the theoretical allotment; actual possession would be established later.
19.6: Beth-lebaoth and Sharuhen — thirteen cities with their villages (ḥăṣērêhem H2691). The summary formula 'cities and their villages' distinguishes between urban centers and their dependent rural settlements — the ancient equivalent of a city and its suburbs or satellite hamlets.
19.7: Ain, Rimmon, Ether, Ashan — four more cities. Ain (H5871, spring) and Rimmon (H7417, pomegranate) are sometimes treated as a compound Ain-rimmon (cf. Neh 11:29). The fruit-bearing name Rimmon points to the agricultural richness of the Negeb's settlements when wells were maintained and terraces built.
19.8: All the villages around these cities to Baalath-beer (Ramah of the Negeb) — the summary includes unnamed villages around the named cities. ḥăṣērîm (villages, H2691) are the agricultural satellites; ḥāṣēr originally described an unwalledenclosure around which temporary structures clustered. Simeon's inheritance is defined as much by this dispersed rural network as by its named cities.
19.9: The explanation of Simeon-within-Judah: kî-hāyāh ḥēleq bənê-yəhûḏāh rāḇ mēhem — 'because the share of Judah was too great for them' (lit. too much from them). ḥēleq (H2506, portion/share) is here used of the land itself. The surplus of Judah is redistributed to the landless Simeonites — an economic solution that simultaneously fulfills Jacob's curse on Simeon (Gen 49:7: 'I will scatter them in Israel'). The lot mechanism has achieved what prophecy announced.
19.10: The third lot comes up for Zebulun. wayyaʿal gôrāl šəlîšî — 'the third lot came up.' The sequence of lot-drawing continues; each lot determines the next tribe to be placed. Zebulun's territory is in the lower Galilee — Gen 49:13 promised that 'Zebulun will live by the seashore' and 'his haven will be Sidon.' The geographic fulfillment of the patriarchal blessing is a subtext of these allotment chapters.
19.11: Zebulun's boundary: wayyaʿal gəḇûlām goes up toward the sea, toward Maralah, touching Dabbesheth. The boundary description uses the same 'going up/down' topographic vocabulary as Benjamin's (ch18), tracking the actual relief of the Galilee hill country. Zebulun's territory straddles the Jezreel Valley and lower Galilee.
19.12: The boundary turns eastward from Sarid toward Beth-shemesh. Zebulun's eastern boundary runs toward the Jordan Valley — the tribe sits between the Mediterranean coastal plain and the Sea of Galilee, controlling the important trade routes through the Jezreel Valley (the Via Maris corridor).
19.13: From there it passes eastward to Gath-hepher (H1662) and Eth-kazin, and continues to Rimmon, curving to Neah. Gath-hepher is the hometown of the prophet Jonah (2 Kgs 14:25) — its inclusion in the tribal list demonstrates how the inheritance chapters contain the geographic seeds of later prophetic narratives.
19.14: The boundary turns northward to Hannathon and ends at the Valley of Iphtah-el. The circuit of Zebulun's territory is complete: a compact tribal region in the lower Galilee, well-watered and agriculturally rich — the same region where Jesus will conduct the majority of his Galilean ministry.
19.15: Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, Bethlehem — twelve cities (a different Bethlehem from Judah's, in Zebulun's territory). The recurrence of the name Bethlehem (H1035, house of bread) in multiple tribal territories reflects how common names derived from agricultural productivity (leḥem, bread) were reused across regions.
19.16: This is the inheritance (naḥălāh) of Zebulun with its cities and villages. The closing formula for each tribe's allotment standardizes the record: inheritance (covenant term), cities (urban centers), villages (rural network). The formula's consistency implies a common source document — likely a royal cadastral record.
19.17: The fourth lot comes out for Issachar. Gen 49:14-15 described Issachar as 'a rawboned donkey crouching between its saddlebags' — a tribe comfortable with agricultural labor and content with its territory. The Jezreel Valley, which Issachar inherits, is the most fertile land in Canaan.
19.18: Issachar's cities: Jezreel (yizrəʿēʾl H3157, 'God sows/plants') — the valley itself takes its name from this city. Chesulloth, Shunem — Shunem (H7766) will be the home of the great woman who hosts Elisha (2 Kgs 4:8), demonstrating again how the allotment lists embed the geographic home of later narratives.
19.19: Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath — further Issacharite cities in the Jezreel Valley corridor. The valley these cities occupy connects the Mediterranean coast to the Jordan Valley, making Issachar's territory a major crossroads of ancient trade and military movement.
19.20: Rabbith, Kishion, Abez — Kishion (H7191) is in the territory that becomes the battle site of Deborah and Barak (Judg 4-5, the Kishon River area). The geographic precision of the allotment lists creates the stage on which subsequent narrative drama unfolds.
19.21: Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, Beth-pazzez — four cities whose names encode landscape features: ʿêyn (spring), gannîm (gardens), ḥaddāh (sharp/keen, possibly describing a distinctive spring). The spring-names are the most ancient layer of geographic memory, preserved because water sources were permanent landmarks.
19.22: The territory touches Tabor, Shahazumah, and Beth-shemesh — sixteen cities in total for Issachar. Mount Tabor (taḇôr H8396) is the prominent isolated peak at the northeastern corner of the Jezreel Valley — a major geographic landmark that will serve as the assembly point for Deborah's coalition (Judg 4:6). Its inclusion as a boundary marker acknowledges its unmistakable visual dominance.
19.23: This is the inheritance of Issachar — closing formula. Issachar's territory in the Jezreel Valley gives it the most agriculturally productive land in Canaan, fulfilling Jacob's portrait of a tribe content with its fertile portion (Gen 49:15).
19.24: The fifth lot comes out for Asher. Gen 49:20: 'Asher's food will be rich; he will provide delicacies fit for a king.' Asher's coastal territory along the Phoenician border (Carmel to Sidon) gave it access to seafaring trade and the fertile Acco plain. The lot confirms what the patriarchal blessing anticipated.
19.25: Asher's cities: Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph — the northern coastal plain. Achshaph (H407) appears in Joshua 11:1 among the northern coalition against Israel; its inclusion in Asher's allotment confirms the conquest. The transformation from enemy city to inheritance city marks the completion of each locality's history.
19.26: Alammelech, Amad, Mishal — and it reaches Carmel to the sea and to Shihor-libnath. The explicit boundary 'to the sea' (hayyāmmāh) fulfills the coastal character of Asher's territory. Mount Carmel (H3760) is the great promontory jutting into the Mediterranean — the border point between Asher and Manasseh. Elijah's contest with Baal's prophets will occur here (1 Kgs 18).
19.27: The boundary turns eastward to Beth-dagon, touches Zebulun and the Valley of Iphtah-el, northward to Beth-emek and Neiel, then continues to Cabul. Bêt-dāgôn (H1016, house of Dagon) — a place dedicated to the Philistine grain/fish deity. The city's pagan name is recorded without comment; Asher inherits the city as real estate, its prior cultic associations now subsumed under covenant ownership.
19.28: Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, Kanah — as far as Great Sidon. The northern boundary reaching to Sidon (ṣîḏōn H6721) fulfills the prophetic scope of Asher's territory (Gen 49:13 gives the seacoast to Zebulun, but Asher's northern reach toward Sidon is clearly anticipated). The tribe settles on the boundary of Phoenician civilization — a strategic and culturally complex position.
19.29: The boundary turns to Ramah and the fortified city of Tyre (ṣōr H6865), then turns toward Hosah and ends at the sea. Tyre (the Rock) is the great Phoenician maritime city — its inclusion as a boundary marker signals that Asher's northern limit was never effectively occupied. The gap between allotment and actual control characterizes these northern territories throughout the period of the Judges.
19.30: Ummah, Aphek, Rehob — twenty-two cities. Aphek (H663, fortress) appears multiple times in biblical narrative — the site of Philistine encampment before the ark's capture (1 Sam 4:1) and of other strategic engagements. The catalog of Asher's cities records both what was granted and what was never fully possessed.
19.31: This is the inheritance of Asher — closing formula. Asher's coastal territory and trade connections made it potentially the wealthiest tribe; its proximity to Phoenicia brought cultural pressure as well as commercial opportunity. The failure to drive out the Sidonians (Judg 1:31) will become a chronic spiritual vulnerability.
19.32: The sixth lot comes out for Naphtali. Gen 49:21: 'Naphtali is a doe set free that bears beautiful fawns' — a portrait of agility and eloquence. Naphtali's territory in the Upper Galilee and around the Sea of Chinnereth (Galilee) is mountainous and fertile, suited to a free-ranging tribe.
19.33: Naphtali's boundary from Heleph, the oak in Zaanannim, Adami-nekeb, and Jabneel to Lakkum, ending at the Jordan. The oak in Zaanannim (ʾēlôn bəṣaʿanannîm H436 H6815) will later appear as a landmark in Judges 4:11 where Heber the Kenite pitches his tent — the specific tree mentioned in the allotment list is the same tree under which the later narrative unfolds.
19.34: The boundary turns westward to Aznoth-tabor, continuing to Hukkok, touching Zebulun on the south and Asher on the west, and Judah at the Jordan on the east. The phrase 'Judah at the Jordan' is geographically puzzling since Judah does not reach the Jordan in the north — possibly referring to a Danite enclave or a textual variant. The three-directional tribal contact (Zebulun, Asher, Judah) describes Naphtali's landlocked position in the north.
19.35: The fortified cities of Naphtali: Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Chinnereth — Chinnereth (H3672) gives its name to the Sea of Chinnereth (= Sea of Galilee). The city sits on the northwestern shore of the lake. Hammath (H2575, hot springs) preserves the memory of natural thermal springs at the site of later Tiberias.
19.36: Adamah, Ramah, Hazor — Hazor (H2674) is the great city-state conquered in Joshua 11, whose king Jabin is a recurring antagonist. Its inclusion in Naphtali's territory is significant: the tribe inherits the most powerful Canaanite capital, yet Judges 4 shows a Jabin of Hazor still oppressing Israel — the allotment records what was granted; the narrative records the ongoing struggle to hold it.
19.37: Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor — Kedesh (H6943, sanctuary/holy place) in Naphtali will become one of the six cities of refuge designated in Joshua 20. The name (qōḏeš, holy, H6944) encodes its function as a sacred asylum site. Its designation as a refuge city is consistent with its ancient character as a sanctuary place.
19.38: Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, Beth-shemesh — nineteen cities. Beth-anath (H1043, house of Anath) and Beth-shemesh (H1053, house of the sun) carry the names of Canaanite deities — Anath (warrior goddess) and Shemesh (sun god). Naphtali inherits cities named for the gods its neighbors worshiped, which may explain the spiritual pressure Naphtali faces throughout Judges.
19.39: This is the inheritance of Naphtali — closing formula. Naphtali's upper Galilean territory, surrounding the Sea of Galilee, is the same region Isaiah identifies as the land of darkness that will see a great light (Isa 9:1-2), fulfilled in Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:12-16). The allotment of Naphtali plants the geographic seed of the gospel's opening movement.
19.40: The seventh lot comes out for Dan. Dan is the last tribe to receive its allotment through the lot; its position last in the sequence may reflect its later migration story (see v47). Gen 49:16-17: 'Dan will judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan will be a serpent by the roadside' — an ambiguous portrait of cunning and independence.
19.41: Dan's territory: Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh (city of the sun, another sun-deity name). Zorah and Eshtaol will be the hometown of Samson (Judg 13:2,25) — the great Danite judge. The allotment list situates the Samson narrative in its precise geographic home.
19.42: Shaalabbin, Aijalon (ʾAyyālôn H357, deer or gazelle place), Ithlah — Aijalon is the valley where Joshua commanded the sun and moon to stand still (Josh 10:12-13). The city of Aijalon (ʿêmeq-ʾayyālôn) gives its name to that event. It now falls in Dan's allotment, embedding the memory of the miraculous battle in the tribe's geographic inheritance.
19.43: Elon, Timnah, Ekron — Timnah (H8553) will be where Samson finds his Philistine wife (Judg 14:1), and where his father-in-law later gives her to another man (Judg 14:20). Ekron (H6138) is one of the five Philistine cities — listed in Dan's allotment though never captured. The gap between allotment and possession is starkest for Dan.
19.44: Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath — Gibbethon (H1405) appears later as a Philistine city (1 Kgs 15:27; 16:15-17). Baalath (H1191, lady/Baal-place) indicates a pre-Israelite Baalistic cult site; Dan inherits territory saturated with Canaanite religious sites, which helps explain the tribe's eventual adoption of idolatrous worship (Judg 18:30-31).
19.45: Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon — Bene-berak (H1139, sons of lightning) is later a city in the territory of Benjamin/Dan whose inhabitants were known to the Assyrian king Sennacherib (noted in his annals). The inheritance of cities later mentioned in extrabiblical documents confirms the geographic reality behind the allotment lists.
19.46: Me-jarkon and Rakkon, with the border opposite Joppa (yāpôʾ H3305). Joppa (modern Jaffa) is the main Mediterranean port of the central coast — the gateway through which Jonah will flee (Jon 1:3) and from which cedar for Solomon's temple will be transported (2 Chr 2:16). Dan's territory touches the coast at this strategic port, though the tribe never effectively controlled it.
19.47: wayyēṣēʾ gəḇûl-bənê-ḏān mēhem wayyaʿălû bənê-ḏān wayilaḥămû ʿim-lešem — 'when the territory of Dan slipped from their grasp, the Danites went up and fought against Leshem.' The verb yāṣāʾ mēhem (H3318 + H4480 prep.) means the border 'went out from them' — the territory they were allotted proved impossible to hold. The Philistines' military pressure in the coastal plain made Dan's original inheritance untenable. This verse is the honest acknowledgment that the ideal allotment did not match the realized possession. lešem = Laish (cf. Judg 18:27) — Dan's migration to the far north is recorded here in compressed form; Judges 18 expands the story in detail.
19.48: The Danites captured Leshem, put it to the sword, took possession, and called it Dan after their ancestor. wayyiqrəʾû ʾōtāh dān — the city is renamed for the tribe. The renaming of captured territory is a covenant act: the tribe claims the city as its own by inscribing its name on it. The migration means Dan's actual possession ends up far north of its original allotment, near the headwaters of the Jordan.
19.49: wayəkallû liḥlōq ʾet-hāʾāreṣ ligbûlōtêhā — 'when they had finished distributing the land according to its borders.' The verb kālāh (H3615, to complete, finish) marks the conclusion of the distribution process. ligbûlōtêhā (according to its borders) confirms the boundary-based system. The Israelites then give Joshua his inheritance — the commander who oversaw everyone else's distribution receives his last.
19.50: At the LORD's command (ʿal-pî YHWH) they give Joshua Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, which he requested. ʾašer šāʾal — 'which he asked/requested.' Joshua requests his own city; it is not assigned to him through the lot. ʿal-pî YHWH (by the mouth of YHWH) means the grant was divinely authorized. wayyiḇneh ʾet-hāʿîr — 'he built the city' — a founding act; Joshua does not merely inherit an existing city but establishes one, consistent with the portrait of a leader who creates rather than merely occupies.
19.51: ʾēlleh hannəḥālōt ʾašer niḥălû ʾelʿāzār hakkōhēn wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn wərāʾšê hāʾāḇôt lamaṭṭôt bənê-yiśrāʾēl bəgôrāl bəšilōh lipnê YHWH — the comprehensive closing formula names the three authorities: Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the ancestral tribes. bəgôrāl (by lot) and lipnê YHWH (before YHWH) confirm both the mechanism and the theological locus. waytikallênāh ḥalleqet hāʾāreṣ — 'and the apportioning of the land was complete' (kālāh again: finished). The distribution is closed.

20.1: YHWH commands Joshua to establish the cities of refuge. The command had been previewed three times in the Torah: Exod 21:13 (the principle), Num 35:9-34 (the detailed law), Deut 19:1-13 (the Mosaic exposition). Joshua 20 is the implementation — the covenant instruction given at Sinai finally applied to the land. The three-stage announcement (law → exposition → application) mirrors how Israel received all major covenant institutions.
20.2: ṯēn lākem ʾet-ʿārê hammiqlāṭ ʾăšer-dibbartî ʾălêkem bəyaḏ-mōšeh — 'designate the cities of refuge of which I spoke to you by the hand of Moses.' The phrase bəyaḏ-mōšeh (by the hand of Moses) authorizes the command by tracing its pedigree: this is not Joshua's innovation but Moses's mandate finally enacted. ʿārê hammiqlāṭ (H5892 H4733) — 'cities of shelter/asylum'; miqlaṭ (H4733) derives from the root meaning to receive or gather in (qālaṭ).
20.3: The purpose: lānûs šāmmāh rōṣēaḥ makkēh-nepeš bišgāgāh bəlîdāʿat — 'so that a killer who strikes a person accidentally without knowledge may flee there.' Two qualifiers define the eligible manslayer: bišgāgāh (H7684, by mistake/inadvertently — from šāgag, to err, go astray) and bəlî-dāʿat (without knowledge/intent — H1097 H1847). The legal distinction between accident and intent (šəgāgāh vs. zaḏôn) underlies the entire Torah's graduated penalty system. The city of refuge is explicitly not available to the willful murderer (cf. Num 35:16-21). The gōʾēl haddām (H1350 H1818, blood-avenger/kinsman-redeemer of blood) is the kin responsible for avenging unlawful death.
20.4: wəʿāmaḏ petaḥ šaʿar hāʿîr wəḏibber bəʾoznê ziqnê hāʿîr hahiʾ ʾet-dəḇārāyw wəʾāsəpû ʾōtô — 'he shall stand at the entrance to the gate of the city and state his case to the elders.' The city gate (šaʿar H8179) is the locus of public judicial proceedings in ancient Israel — where contracts, legal matters, and disputes were conducted before witnesses. The manslayer must present his case (dəḇārāyw, his words) to the zəqēnîm (H2205, elders) — the civic authority. The verb ʾāsap (H622, to gather in, receive) is the same root as miqlaṭ's related meaning: the elders 'gather him in' to the city's protection.
20.5: If the blood-avenger pursues the manslayer, the elders shall not hand him over — because 'he struck his neighbor unintentionally and was not hating him before.' The Hebrew causal chain: bişgāgāh (inadvertently) + wəlōʾ-śōnēʾ hûʾ lô mitmōl šilšôm (he was not an enemy of him previously) — the absence of prior enmity is the evidentiary test for accidental killing vs. murder. The elders function as a court of preliminary adjudication before the full assembly trial.
20.6: wəyāšaḇ bāʿîr hahîʾ ʿaḏ-ʿomḏô lipnê hāʿēḏāh lammišpāṭ ʿaḏ-môt hakkōhēn haggāḏôl ʾašer yihyeh bayyāmîm hāhēm — 'he shall live in that city until he stands before the assembly for judgment, until the death of the high priest who is serving in those days.' Three temporal markers: (1) until standing before the ʿēḏāh (H5712, assembly) for trial; (2) until the death of the kōhēn haggāḏôl (high priest); (3) after the priest's death, free to return home. The death of the high priest functions as a general amnesty — the legal logic is debated, but the pattern is clear: death of the covenant mediator releases the community from certain legal claims.
20.7: The three western cities of refuge are designated: Qeḏeš (H6943, sanctuary/holy place) in Galilee in the hill country of Naphtali; Šekem (H7927, shoulder/ridge) in the hill country of Ephraim; Qiryat Arbaʿ (H7153, city of four, = Hebron) in the hill country of Judah. The three cities span the north-south length of western Canaan — geography ensures that no potential manslayer is more than a half-day's journey from a city of refuge.
20.8: The three eastern cities (Transjordan) are designated: Beṣer (H1221, fortress/stronghold) in the wilderness plateau for Reuben; Rāmôt (H7216, heights) in Gilead for Gad; Gôlān (H1474, circuit/captive) in Bashan for Manasseh. The six cities form a network — three west, three east — ensuring symmetrical access for all twelve tribes. The Transjordan's three cities serve the two-and-a-half tribes who settled east of the Jordan in Numbers 32.
20.9: ʾēlleh hāyû ʿārê hammuʿāḏāh — 'these were the appointed cities' (môʿāḏ H4150, appointed meeting-place — the same word as in ʾōhel môʿēd, tent of meeting). The six cities of refuge are 'meeting places' of mercy and justice. The closing phrase: wəlōʾ-yāmût bəyaḏ gōʾēl haddām ʿaḏ-ʿomḏô lipnê hāʿēḏāh — 'so that he would not die by the hand of the blood-avenger until he had stood before the assembly.' The institution functions as due process — not exoneration, but the guarantee of a fair hearing before execution. The phrase gēr (H1616, sojourner/foreigner) in the last clause confirms the cities' universal scope: even the non-Israelite resident may access the asylum.

15.1: goral livnei Yehudah lemishpechotam (goral, H1486; mishpachah, H4940): 'the lot for the tribe of Judah by their clans.' Goral (lot, casting lot) is the sacred means of territorial allocation — the lot was cast before YHWH so that the distribution of the land was understood as divine determination, not human negotiation (Num 26:55-56; Josh 14:2). Mishpachot (clans/families) is the social unit between tribe (shevet) and household (bayit) — the extended kinship group that received territory collectively. Judah's territory is defined first because Judah was the preeminent tribe (Gen 49:8-12; Num 2:3), and Caleb — the faithful spy — belonged to Judah (Josh 14:6).
15.2: yam hamelach (yam, H3220; melach, H4417): 'the Salt Sea.' The Dead Sea is consistently called yam hamelach (Sea of Salt) in the OT — named for its extreme salinity, which makes it uninhabitable by ordinary marine life. It defines the southern-eastern boundary of Judah's territory. The Dead Sea sits at approximately 430 meters below sea level, the lowest point on earth's surface. In prophetic imagination it becomes the sea into which YHWH's judgment flows (Ezek 47:8-11): the salt sea that receives living water from the temple becomes fresh and teeming with fish — the reversal of the death-characteristic that gives it its name.
15.3: ma'aleh aqrabbim (ma'aleh, H4608; aqrab, H6137): 'the ascent of Akrabbim (Scorpion Pass).' Ma'aleh (ascent, going up) identifies a climbing pass through the Negeb highlands south of the Dead Sea. Aqrabbim (scorpions) names the pass for its characteristic inhabitants — the rocky Negeb terrain is still scorpion habitat. The boundary description in vv.2-12 follows a geographical survey of the land in four directions: south (vv.2-4), east (v.5a), north (vv.5b-11), west (v.12). The careful boundary demarcation reflects ANE land-grant practice: official documents specified boundaries precisely to prevent future dispute.
15.4: vehayah tochalot hagvul hayam haGadol: 'the boundary ends at the Great Sea.' The yam haGadol (Great Sea) is the Mediterranean — 'great' by contrast to the smaller inland bodies of water. The southern boundary of Judah runs from the southern tip of the Salt Sea westward through the Negeb wilderness to the Mediterranean coast. The phrase zeh yihyeh lachem gevul negev (this shall be your southern boundary) provides the formula closing a boundary section.
