The geographical precision of the boundary descriptions reflects the real administrative needs of the tribal allocations: disputed territory needed documented borders.
15.5: gevul-qedmah yam hamelach: 'the eastern border is the Salt Sea to the end of the Jordan.' The eastern boundary is the simplest — the Salt Sea (Dead Sea) itself forms a natural barrier. ufnot tzafon: 'turning northward' — the formula signals a pivot from the eastern to the northern boundary. The Jordan River's mouth into the Dead Sea is the northeast corner of Judah's territory. The natural boundaries (sea, river, desert) of Judah's territory explain its historical stability as a political unit — natural defenses that the hill country and the Shephelah reinforced.
15.6: Beit haAravah (aravah, H6160): 'Beth-arabah' (House of the Arabah). Aravah is the Hebrew name for the Jordan valley depression — the deep rift running from the Sea of Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. The Arabah is arid, below sea level, and geologically dramatic — the visible reminder of the rift created by YHWH's action in history (the Sodom and Gomorrah region was in the southern Arabah). The stone of Bohan son of Reuben (v.6) is a landmark placed by a Reubenite and used as a Judah/Benjamin boundary marker — the kind of territorial landmark typical of ancient surveying practice.
15.7: Devir (Devir, H1688): Debir appears here as a boundary landmark in the Achor Valley. Devir likely means 'back room' or 'oracle room' — related to davar (word/speak) by some interpreters, suggesting a connection with prophetic or priestly speech. The Valley of Achor (Achor = 'trouble') is named for the Achan incident (ch7: the troubling of Israel). That the boundary passes through the Valley of Trouble is significant: the site of Israel's first great covenant failure becomes a boundary marker within Judah's inheritance. Hosea will later transform Achor into a door of hope (Hos 2:15).
15.8: ge Ben-Hinnom (gei, H1516; Hinnom, H2011): 'the Valley of the Son of Hinnom.' This valley on Jerusalem's southwestern flank becomes one of the most significant geographical features in Israel's later history. Under Manasseh and Ahaz it was the site of child sacrifice to Molech (Tofet, 2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31). Josiah defiled it to prevent its further use (2 Kgs 23:10). In NT period, Ge Hinnom became Geenna (Gehenna) — the word Jesus used for the place of final judgment. The valley that began as a boundary marker between Judah and Benjamin became the topographical basis for the eschatological concept of divine judgment.
15.9: Qiryat Ye'arim (qiryah, H7151; ya'ar, H3293): 'City of the Forests.' Kirjath-jearim (Kiriath-jearim) is a significant site: the ark of the covenant rested here for twenty years after its return from the Philistines (1 Sam 7:1-2) before David brought it to Jerusalem (2 Sam 6:2-4). The 'city of forests' on the Judah-Benjamin border became the storage site of YHWH's presence-symbol during one of Israel's darkest spiritual periods — YHWH's ark located at a boundary city, neither in the central shrine nor in captivity, waiting for the reign of the king after YHWH's own heart.
15.10: har Seir (seir, H8165): 'Mount Seir' — not Edom's mountain but a ridge near Kirjath-jearim in Judah. The name Seir (hairy/rough, related to se'ar/hair) appears for multiple locations in the OT. The boundary continues westward through the hill country toward the coastal plain, passing through Beth-shemesh (House of the Sun — where the Philistines returned the ark, 1 Sam 6:12-15). The boundary towns along this north-western edge of Judah will be the frontier zone of Philistine-Israelite conflict throughout the period of the judges and early monarchy.
15.11: veyatza gevul el-keter har haefer: 'the border goes out toward the shoulder of Mount Ekron.' The northern boundary of Judah ends at the Mediterranean coast, including the Philistine cities. Yatza (go out, extend) is the standard direction verb for boundary descriptions — the boundary 'goes out' from landmark to landmark. The Philistine coast (Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza — vv.45-47) will remain contested territory throughout the period of the monarchy. The formal allocation of the coastal plain to Judah did not translate into effective control; the Philistines maintained their hold despite the legal grant.
15.12: yam haGadol ugevul: 'the Great Sea and its coast.' The western boundary is simply the Mediterranean coastline — YHWH's natural boundary marker. The four-direction survey of Judah's territory (south, east, north, west) gives the territory a shape: from the Negeb wilderness in the south, the Dead Sea in the east, the northern hill country, and the Mediterranean coast. The ideal extent of Judah's territory encompasses the entire southern half of Canaan; actual effective control varied enormously across the periods of judges, united monarchy, divided monarchy, and exile.
15.13: ish haAnakim (anaq, H6061): 'the man of the Anakim.' Caleb's special inheritance — a portion within Judah's allotment — is granted because of his faithfulness at Kadesh (Num 14:24; Josh 14:9-12). Ish haAnakim refers to the Anakim (the giant population), and Arba was their father — Hebron was literally 'the city of Arba, the father of Anak.' Caleb requested and received the most fortified, most fearsome part of the territory — the mountain city defended by the giants that the faithless spies had cited as evidence of impossibility. The man who said 'we can certainly do it' (Num 13:30) goes first to the hardest task and succeeds.
15.14: vayoresh misham Kalev et-shloshut bnei ha'Anaq (yarash, H3423): 'Caleb drove out from there the three sons of Anak.' Yarash (dispossess, drive out, take possession) is the conquest's governing verb — the word that describes what YHWH commanded Israel to do throughout Canaan. The three sons of Anak (Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai) were the same figures that the twelve spies had reported forty-five years earlier (Num 13:22). Caleb, at eighty-five years old (Josh 14:10), did what the entire first generation had been afraid to do: he drove them out. The length of the list (three named sons) emphasizes the thoroughness of the dispossession.
15.15: Devir (Devir, H1688): 'Debir.' Debir (also called Kirjath-sefer in this verse) is the second city Caleb attacks — moving south from Hebron into the Shephelah. Kiriyat-sefer (City of the Book/Scroll, H7153) is the older name for Debir. The name may indicate a scribal center or archive — a city associated with written records. That the city of scribes/records was one of the first to be taken in Caleb's territory is suggestive: the conquest begins with the city of writing. Debir will later become a Levitical city (Josh 21:15), its archives serving the covenant community's priestly learning.
15.16: natan lo et-Achsah bito le'ishah (natan, H5414): 'he gave him Achsah his daughter as wife.' Caleb's offer — his daughter as prize for the conqueror of Kirjath-sefer — is the ANE convention of prize-marriage for military achievement. The offer is not degrading by the standards of the time but honors both the daughter (she will be given to a man of demonstrated valor) and the enterprise (the conquest is urgent enough to merit the highest incentive). Achsah (ankle-bracelet, from akhes/H5895) is one of the named women of the conquest narrative whose action proves decisive.
15.17: Otniel ben-Qenaz achi Kalev: 'Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's brother.' Othniel will become Israel's first judge (Judg 3:9-11), the prototypical judge who delivers Israel from oppression when they cry to YHWH. His introduction here — as the man who conquered the City of Books — establishes his military credentials before the period of the judges begins. Kenaz is the name of both Othniel's father and Caleb's father (Josh 14:6) — their relationship is either brothers or uncle-nephew depending on how achi is read. The overlap between Caleb's clan and the first judge introduces the Kenizzite tradition that underlies early Judahite leadership.
15.18: vatisiteihu litshol me'et aviyha sadeh (sut, H5496; sha'al, H7592): 'she urged him to ask her father for a field.' Sut (urge, incite) is a strong word — Achsah pressured Othniel to request a field from Caleb. The detail that she then dismounts her donkey herself to make the request directly suggests that Othniel's request was either insufficient or she decided to advocate personally. Vatitzneach me'al hachamor (she dismounted from the donkey) — the act of dismounting before asking was a gesture of respect before a superior. Achsah's negotiating initiative is remembered in the narrative as admirable resourcefulness, not impropriety.
15.19: gullot mayim (gullah, H1543; mayim, H4325): 'springs of water.' Achsah's request for both upper and lower springs — in addition to the field in the Negeb — is a request for the most valuable resource in the semi-arid south: water. Gullot (springs, from galal/to roll — the rolling up of water from the ground) are natural spring sources, not cisterns. Caleb grants her 'upper springs and lower springs' — the complete water supply for the dry Negeb territory. The narrative's detail about water rights reflects the genuine agricultural concern: land without water in the Negeb is worthless; land with springs is life-giving. Caleb's generosity with the water mirrors YHWH's generosity with the land.
15.20: zot nachalat matteh bnei Yehudah lemishpechotam (nachalah, H5159; matteh, H4294): 'this is the inheritance of the tribe of Judah by their clans.' Nachalah (inheritance, possession) is the OT's most important land-ownership term — it carries the sense of perpetual, inalienable possession. The land is not purchased or earned but inherited as YHWH's gift to the covenant community. Matteh (tribe, also staff/rod) links the tribe to its symbol of authority. The formula 'by their clans' (lemishpechotam) applies the allocation to the extended kinship groups who will actually settle and work the territory.
15.21: ve'areihem min-qetzeh matteh bnei Yehudah: 'their cities from the extreme south of the tribe of Judah.' The Negeb district cities (vv.21-32) — approximately twelve cities in the extreme south — represent Judah's southernmost settlements, bordering the wilderness. Many of these cities appear in later narratives: Beersheba (v.28) was the traditional southern boundary of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'), Ziklag (v.31) was David's city as a Philistine vassal (1 Sam 27:6), and Hormah (v.30) was the site of Israel's first victory after the wilderness period (Num 21:3). The city list is not merely administrative but encodes the geography of Israel's early history.
15.22: Qinah veChatzevon ve'Avadah: Kinah, Dimonah, and Adadah. The southern Negeb cities are mostly unidentified today — their locations are debated and few have been conclusively excavated. The list preserves the administrative geography of early Judah, giving modern archaeology reference points for the distribution of early Iron Age settlements in the Negeb. The preservation of these place names — many of them obscure — reflects the faithfulness of the biblical tradition in recording what was administratively true at the time of composition, even when the names have since been lost.
15.23: veQedesh veChatzor veYitnan: Kedesh, Hazor, Ithnan. Qedesh (holy place) appears as both a personal name and a place name throughout the OT — the name indicates a site with cultic significance, designated as set-apart. Multiple places named Kedesh appear in the tribal territories, reflecting the widespread practice of designating cultic sites with this generic name. The repetition of Hazor (which appears in multiple districts) reflects either a naming tradition for multiple settlements or textual conflation over the centuries of transmission.
15.24: Zif veTelem ve'Bealot: Ziph, Telem, Bealoth. Ziph (v.24, 55) appears twice in Judah's city list — once in the Negeb district and once in the hill country district. The Ziph of the hill country (v.55) is the one associated with David's fugitive period (1 Sam 23:14-24; 26:1-3) — the Ziphites twice betrayed David's hiding place to Saul. The administrative geography of Judah's city list thus provides the backdrop for the David narratives: the specific districts and cities named in Joshua 15 appear repeatedly in Samuel, confirming the Sitz im Leben of the tribal records.
15.25: Chatzor Chadatah veQeriyot Chetzron hi Chatzor: 'New Hazor and Kerioth-hezron (that is, Hazor).' The parenthetical identification ('that is, Hazor') distinguishes this settlement from other Hazors. The naming practice of 'new' towns (chadatah, new/fresh) alongside older settlements reflects the growth and reorganization of settlements over time. Keriyot (towns) may be related to the Kerioth that appears in later prophetic judgment oracles (Jer 48:24,41 for Moab; Amos 2:2) — the geographic name persisting across contexts as a common noun for settlements.
15.26: Amam veShema veMotzadah: Amam, Shema, Moladah. Moladah (moledah, birth/lineage) reappears in the Simeonite city list (Josh 19:2) and was reoccupied after the exile (Neh 11:26). The border zone between Judah and Simeon was fluid: Simeon's tribal territory was within Judah's (Josh 19:1 'within the inheritance of Judah'), so many cities appear in both lists. The administrative overlap reflects the demographic and political absorption of Simeon by Judah that characterized the early period — Simeon as a tribe effectively disappears into Judah in the later biblical record.
15.27: veChatzar Gadah veCheshmon uBeit-Pelet: Hazar-gaddah, Heshmon, Beth-pelet. Chatzar (enclosure, village) is a common element in Negeb place names (Hazar-gaddah, Hazar-shual, Hazar-susah) — the word indicates a walled enclosure or hamlet rather than a full city (ir). The Negeb was populated by such semi-nomadic settlements surrounding water sources. The use of chatzar rather than ir for many of these southern settlements reflects the genuinely pastoral and semi-nomadic character of the southern Negeb landscape.
15.28: Beeir Sheva uvYizta ve'Yeruet: Beer-sheba, Biziothiah, Baalah. Beeir Sheva (Well of Seven / Well of the Oath) was the most important city of the Negeb — the traditional southern boundary of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba,' Judg 20:1). The well associated with Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Gen 21:31-32) and with Jacob's theophany on the way to Egypt (Gen 46:1-4) is the first named city in the Negeb district. Beer-sheba is the Negeb's administrative and cultic center — the place where the patriarchal tradition gave Israel its sense of southern identity.
15.29: Baalah veIyim ve'Atzem: Baalah, Iim, Ezem. Baalah (lady/mistress, feminine of Baal) appears as both a place name and an alternate name for Kirjath-jearim (Josh 15:9-10). Multiple Balahs in the tribal lists reflect the pre-Israelite cultic geography: sites named for Baal (the Canaanite storm deity) were common throughout the land. The Israelite administrative geography did not immediately rename Baal-sites — many retained their pre-conquest names well into the monarchy period. Prophetic critique of the Baal names came later (Hos 2:17: 'she will call me "my husband" and no longer call me "my Baal"').
15.30: veEltolad veKesil vecHormah: Eltolad, Chesil, Hormah. Chormah (devoted to destruction, from charam/H2763 — the cherem) is named for the total destruction of its Canaanite population: the word is the verbal noun of herem (the sacred ban). Hormah appears in Numbers 14:45 and 21:3 — twice in connection with decisive military engagements. The conquest name (cherem) preserved in the place name is a permanent reminder of the theological character of the conquest: YHWH's judgment on the Canaanite cities was not mere military conquest but sacred destruction.
15.31: Tziqlag veMatmannah veSansannah: Ziklag, Madmannah, Sansannah. Tziqlag (Ziklag) becomes historically significant in the David narratives: the Philistine king Achish gave it to David as his base during his Philistine service (1 Sam 27:6), and the text notes 'it has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day' — a reference to the time of composition, when it was still a Judahite royal possession. The detailed city list in ch15 thus provides the administrative framework within which the David narratives operate; the geography was precisely remembered.
15.32: veLevavot veShilchim ve'Ayin veRimon: Lebaoth, Shilhim, Ain, Rimmon. The formula at the close of the Negeb district: 'all the cities with their villages' — twenty-nine cities total. The round number signals the completion of an administrative district count. Iyr ve'chatzereicha (cities and their villages) is the standard phrase for urban settlement with dependent agricultural hamlets — the city provides defense, the surrounding villages provide agricultural production. The Negeb district's twenty-nine cities represent the administrative reality of early Judahite settlement in the south.
15.33: baShfelah (Shefelah, H8219): 'in the Shephelah (Lowlands).' The Shephelah is the foothills between the coastal plain and the Judean highlands — a transitional zone of gentle hills, wadis, and fertile valleys. It was the most contested region of Judah's territory throughout the monarchic period: the main valleys (Aijalon, Sorek, Elah, Guvrin) provided access routes between the Philistine coast and Jerusalem. David's fight with Goliath was in the Elah Valley (1 Sam 17:2) — a Shephelah corridor. The distinction between Shephelah (lowland hills) and har (mountain/highland) reflects the precise geographic consciousness of the biblical writers.
15.34: Eshtaol uTzor'ah veAshnah: Eshtaol, Zorah, Ashnah. Zorah and Eshtaol are the towns associated with Samson (Judg 13:2,25; 16:31) — the strongman judge whose territory was precisely the Shephelah border region between Judah/Dan and the Philistines. The geographical precision of the Samson narratives corresponds to the city list here: the places named in Joshua 15 appear as living settings in Judges 13-16. The city lists of Joshua are not merely administrative records but the geographical substructure of Israel's entire narrative tradition.
15.35: Yarmut ve'Adullam: Jarmuth and Adullam. Adullam (adulam, possibly 'witness of the people') is significant in the David narratives: the Cave of Adullam was David's first refuge when fleeing Saul (1 Sam 22:1-2), and it gathered his first band of followers — those who were distressed, indebted, and discontented. The cave that became the nucleus of the future king's following lay within Judah's Shephelah territory, specifically in this district. Jarmuth was one of the five Amorite kings who allied against Gibeon (Josh 10:3-5), making this district the zone of that famous battle.
15.36: Sharaim ve'Aditayim vehaGederah: Shaaraim, Adithaim, Gederah. Sha'arayim (two gates) is mentioned in the Goliath narrative (1 Sam 17:52) — the Philistines fled 'to Shaaraim' after David's victory. The city-list entries in vv.33-36 thus correspond precisely to the Elah Valley area of the first Shephelah district. The sixteen cities of this district (v.36) with their villages represent the northern Shephelah, the most frequently contested land in Judah's territory due to its access routes and agricultural fertility.
15.37: Tz'nan veChadashah uMigdal-Gad: Zenan, Hadashah, Migdal-gad. The second Shephelah district (vv.37-41) covers the central and southern foothills. Migdal (tower/stronghold, H4024) appears as a prefix in numerous place names: Migdal-gad, Migdol (Exod 14:2), Migdal-el (Josh 19:38) — the tower was the most important defensive feature of an ancient walled town, so its name became part of the town's identity. The Shephelah's defensive towers guarded the valley approaches into Judah's interior.
15.38: Dilean vehaMizpeh veYoqte'el: Dilean, Mizpeh, Joktheel. Mitzpah (watchtower, lookout point, from tzafah/H6822 — to watch/spy) appears frequently as both a place name and a common noun. Multiple Mizpehs exist in the tribal territories, all named for their elevated, watchful position. Mizpeh served as an assembly point in crisis situations (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 7:5-6) — the watch-point becoming the gathering-point when Israel needed to come together. The name's function (watching, observing) reflects the strategic position of hills in the pre-modern Levant.
15.39: Lachish veBozqat ve'Eglon: Lachish, Bozkath, Eglon. Lakhish is one of the most archaeologically significant cities in the Shephelah — excavations at Tell Lachish reveal continuous occupation from the Early Bronze Age. Lachish was the second most important city in Judah after Jerusalem during the monarchy and was heavily fortified. It was the focus of the Assyrian siege under Sennacherib (depicted in the Lachish reliefs, now in the British Museum) and was one of the last Judean cities to fall to Babylon (Jer 34:7). Its appearance in the city list alongside Eglon (the Amorite king Joshua defeated in ch10) grounds the list historically.
15.40: veKabbon veLachmam veKitlish: Cabbon, Lahmas, Chitlish. The second Shephelah district continues with settlements whose later biblical attestation is sparse — they represent the ordinary agricultural hinterland of the major cities like Lachish. The lack of later narrative attention to these towns does not reduce their historical reality: they were inhabited, enumerated, and administered. The biblical text's willingness to record obscure settlements alongside famous ones reflects the genuine administrative character of the source material behind the city lists.
15.41: veGederot Beit-Dagon veNa'amah uMaqedah: Gederoth, Beth-dagon, Naamah, Makkedah. Beit-Dagon (House of Dagon) is named for the Philistine grain deity — another pre-Israelite Canaanite name preserved in the Israelite administrative geography. Makkedah (v.41) is the cave site where Joshua executed the five Amorite kings who had hidden from the battle (Josh 10:16-17) — the execution site becomes a named settlement within Judah's territorial records. The sixteen cities of the second Shephelah district close with a formula identical to the first.
15.42: Livnah veEter veAshan: Libnah, Ether, Ashan. Livnah (white, from lavan/whiteness) was a Levitical city (Josh 21:13) and a place of prophetic significance: the Levitical city on the border of the Shephelah and the coastal plain. Libnah revolted against Judah during Jehoram's reign (2 Kgs 8:22), suggesting a distinct local identity even within the Judahite tribal framework. Ether and Ashan appear also in Simeon's city list (Josh 19:7), again reflecting the fluid boundary between Judah and Simeon in the southern Shephelah.
15.43: veYiftach veAshna vaNetziv: Iphtah, Ashnah, Nezib. The third Shephelah district (vv.42-44) covers the southern foothills connecting to the Negeb. The orderly progression through districts — northern Shephelah, central Shephelah, southern Shephelah — reflects careful geographical organization in the original administrative records. The repetition of similar names in different districts (two Ashnabs, for example) confirms independent sources behind the city list rather than a single invented compilation — a scribe creating a list would not repeat names so confusingly.
15.44: veQe'ilah veAchziv veMaresheh: Keilah, Achzib, Mareshah. Qe'ilah (Keilah) is associated with David: he rescued it from the Philistines (1 Sam 23:1-12) only to discover that its inhabitants would hand him over to Saul if given the chance. The city David saved was prepared to betray him — a microcosm of the covenant community's relationship with its shepherd. Mareshah was a major administrative center in the Shephelah and a border fortress — Asa defeated the Cushite army near Mareshah (2 Chr 14:9-10).
15.45: Eqron uchotzeroteiha uvir'oteiha (chatzerot, H2691; ir'ot/gevurot, H1380): 'Ekron with its towns and villages.' The Philistine pentapolis cities (Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza — vv.45-47) are formally allocated to Judah but were effectively under Philistine control throughout the period of the judges and early monarchy. Chatzerot (towns, hamlets — the agricultural dependent villages) and ir'ot (villages, from the root 'forest/grove') represent the two levels of rural settlement: the fortified hamlet and the open village. The inclusion of the Philistine cities in Judah's formal allocation acknowledges the ideal extent of the land grant even when effective control was absent.
15.46: meEqron veyammah kol asher al-yad Ashdod vechatzeroteiha: 'from Ekron to the sea, all that lay near Ashdod and its villages.' The Philistine coastal plain between Ekron and Ashdod is allocated to Judah — the coastal strip that includes the most fertile agricultural land in the region (the Shephelah lowlands transition to the coastal plain). Ashdod was one of the Philistine pentapolis cities and the site where the ark of the covenant was taken after the battle of Ebenezer (1 Sam 5:1-7). The detailed allocation of this contested region reflects the legal ideal; the narrative tradition documents the gap between ideal and reality.
15.47: Ashdod chatzeroteiha viviroteiha Azah chatzeroteiha viviroteiha: 'Ashdod with its towns and villages; Gaza with its towns and villages.' Gaza (Azzah) and its territory through the Wadi of Egypt (nahal Mitzrayim, the traditional southwestern boundary of Canaan) closes the Shephelah allocation. Gaza was the southernmost and most important of the Philistine pentapolis — the city where Samson's story ends (Judg 16:21) and where the Philistine temple of Dagon was pulled down. The allocation of Gaza to Judah was ideal geography; effective control of Gaza eluded Israel throughout the biblical period.
15.48: uvehar (har, H2022): 'in the hill country.' The hill country (Judean highlands) district opens the third and most important geographical zone of Judah's territory. Har (mountain, hill, hill country) designates the elevated limestone plateau running north-south through Judah — the natural fortress that made Judah the most defensible territory in Canaan. The hill country cities (vv.48-60) represent the heartland of Judah, including Hebron (v.54), the patriarchal center. The Judean highlands became the nucleus of the Davidic monarchy and the site of Jerusalem itself.
15.49: Dannah veQiryat-Sanah hi Devir: 'Dannah and Kiriath-sannah (that is, Debir).' The identification of Kiriath-sannah with Debir connects it to Caleb's conquest (vv.15-17) — the city has multiple names: Kiriath-sefer (City of the Book), Kiriath-sannah, and Debir. The multiple names may reflect different naming traditions from different periods, preserved in the administrative record. Sanah (possibly 'thorn' or 'branch') as an alternative name suggests a different local tradition about the city's character than the 'book' association of Kiriath-sefer.
15.50: ve'Anav veEshtemo veAnim: Anab, Eshtemoh, Anim. Anab (v.50) is notable in the context of the Anak: Anav likely means 'grapes' but its proximity to Anak (the giant people) may not be coincidental. Joshua had killed the remaining Anakim in the hill country, including at Anab (Josh 11:21). Eshtemoh (Eshtemoa) became a Levitical city (Josh 21:14) and appears in the David narratives: David sent gifts to Eshtemoa from the Ziklag spoil (1 Sam 30:28). The Levitical presence in the hill country district reflects the deliberate distribution of Levites throughout Judah's heartland.
15.51: veGoshen veChalon veGiloh: Goshen, Holon, Giloh. Goshen here is a Judahite city (not the Egyptian region), probably named by settlers who remembered the name from the Egyptian experience. The naming of a Canaanite city after a place associated with Israelite slavery is an act of memory: the land of abundance that Egypt had been (Goshen was the fertile delta region) is now re-placed in the inheritance of Judah. Ahithophel, David's wise counselor who betrayed him and later hanged himself (2 Sam 15:12; 17:23), was from Giloh.
15.52: Arav uDumah veEshan: Arab, Dumah, Eshan. Dumah (silence/stillness) as a place name reflects a location with a distinctive quality — perhaps the silence of a hidden valley or the stillness of a water source. The same name appears in the prophetic oracles against foreign nations (Isa 21:11 'the oracle concerning Dumah') and as a son of Ishmael (Gen 25:14), reflecting the multiple uses of the name across different traditions. The Judean hill country's city names reflect a mixture of geographical description, cultic heritage, and personal commemoration.
15.53: veYanum uveit-Tappuach ve'Afekah: Janum, Beth-tappuah, Aphekah. Beit-Tappuach (House of the Apple) reflects the horticultural character of the Judean highlands: the mountain climate and limestone soil supported apple and other fruit orchards. The naming of towns after their agricultural products (House of the Apple, House of the Vineyard, etc.) is common in the Judean hill country city list — a practical recording of what made each site agriculturally valuable. Tappuach (apple/quince) appears also as a border town of Ephraim (Josh 16:8; 17:8).
15.54: Chumtah veQiryat Arba hi Chevron veTs'ior: 'Humtah, and Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), and Zior.' Kiryat Arba (City of Four or City of Arba) is identified with Hebron — the city named for its 'four' components (possibly four quarters or four associated settlements) or for Arba, father of the Anakim (v.13). Hebron (Chevron, H2275 — from chaver/partner, association, fellowship) was the patriarchal center: Abraham purchased the cave of Machpelah there (Gen 23), and it was the first capital of David's kingdom (2 Sam 2:1-4). Its appearance in the hill country list as both Kiriath-arba and Hebron represents the dual naming tradition.
15.55: Maon veKarmel veZif veYuttah: Maon, Carmel, Ziph, Juttah. Ma'on (dwelling place) and Karmel (garden/orchard of God) are hill country towns associated with Nabal (1 Sam 25:2-3): 'there was a man in Maon whose business was in Carmel.' The David narratives are again embedded in the geography of Joshua 15 — the specific towns of the fifth hill country district provide the setting for the Abigail story. Ziph in this district (v.55) is the hill country Ziph of the David-Saul chase narratives (1 Sam 23:14-24), distinct from the Negeb Ziph of v.24.
15.56: veYizre'el veYoqde'am veZanoach: Jezreel, Jokdeam, Zanoah. The Jezreel here is a hill country city in Judah, distinct from the famous Jezreel Valley in the north (the site of Ahab's vineyard, Elijah's confrontation with Baal, and Jehu's coup). The existence of a southern Jezreel (Yizreel, God sows) within Judah's hill country, while the northern Jezreel dominated the great valley, reflects the common practice of reusing meaningful place names across different tribal territories.
15.57: haQa'in Giv'ah veTimnah: Kain, Gibeah, Timnah. Giv'ah (hill) appears repeatedly as a place name throughout the Judean and Benjaminite highlands — the hill is the natural feature that defines settlement sites in this terrain. Multiple Gibeahs exist: Gibeah of Judah (here), Gibeah of Benjamin (site of the Benjaminite war, Judg 19-20), Gibeah of Saul (1 Sam 10:26). The proliferation of Gibeah-names reflects the pervasive hill-country terrain of the central highlands; the specific Gibeah is usually identified by its tribal affiliation.
15.58: Chalhul Beit-Tzur uGedor: Halhul, Beth-zur, Gedor. Beit-Tzur (House of the Rock) was a major administrative center and fortress in the Judean hill country — excavations reveal occupation from the Middle Bronze Age and significant fortification during the Maccabean period (the Apocrypha records battles there). The fortress function of Beth-zur in later periods reflects its strategic importance at the junction of the north-south mountain ridge road and the lateral roads toward Hebron. The city list records a geographic reality that archaeological evidence confirms.
15.59: uMa'arat veBeit-Anot veEltekon: Maarath, Beth-anoth, Eltekon. The sixth hill country district closes with six cities. Some LXX manuscripts include an additional district not in the MT between vv.59 and 60, containing cities in the Jerusalem area (including Tekoa, Ephrathah/Bethlehem, and Kiriath-jearim) — a textual tradition suggesting that an entire Bethlehem district may have been accidentally omitted from the MT. The possible omission of a Bethlehem district from the MT is particularly striking given Bethlehem's later theological significance as David's birthplace and the anticipated birthplace of the Messiah (Mic 5:2).
15.60: Kiryat-Baal hi Kiryat-Ye'arim uhaRavah: 'Kiriath-baal (that is, Kiriath-jearim) and Rabbah.' Kiriath-jearim's alternate name — Kiriath-baal (City of Baal) — reveals its pre-Israelite cultic character: a city dedicated to the Canaanite deity. The identification of the Baal-city with the City of Forests (Kiriath-jearim) demonstrates how place-name traditions overlap: the same city carries both its Canaanite religious name and its geographical name. That the ark of the covenant rested at a city whose alternate name was 'City of Baal' (1 Sam 7:1-2) is a detail the text records without comment — YHWH's presence was not deterred by a Baal place-name.
15.61: uvamidbar Beit haAravah Middin veSechacah: 'in the wilderness, Beth-arabah, Middin, and Secacah.' The wilderness district (vv.61-62) identifies settlements in the Judean desert east of the central highland ridge — the barren zone between the mountains and the Dead Sea. Beit ha'Aravah (House of the Desert Depression) and Secacah and others may correspond to sites near the Dead Sea, including possibly the Qumran area where the Dead Sea Scrolls community later established itself. The Judean wilderness was never uninhabited: its caves, cliffs, and seasonal water sources supported herding communities and, in later periods, religious communities seeking separation from the world.
15.62: veNivshan veir haMelach ve'Ein Gedi: 'Nibshan, the City of Salt, and En-gedi.' Ein Gedi (Spring of the Kid) is the most significant site in the wilderness district — a dramatic spring and oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea, where fresh water cascading down the cliff face creates a tropical microclimate in the desert. En-gedi is where David hid from Saul in the caves (1 Sam 23:29; 24:1-7) — the wilderness oasis as the place of the fugitive king's refuge. The Song of Songs begins with 'I am a cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi' (Song 1:14) — the oasis's fertility inspiring poetic comparisons.
15.63: vehaYevusi yeshvei Yerushalayim lo-yachlu bnei Yehudah lehorisham (yavas, H2983; Yerushalayim, H3389; yarash, H3423): 'the Jebusites, who inhabited Jerusalem, the children of Judah could not drive out.' Lo-yachlu lehorisham (could not dispossess them) is the chapter's most theologically significant phrase: despite the formal allocation of Jerusalem to Judah, and despite earlier military action (Judg 1:8 records burning the city), the Jebusites were not dispossessed. Jerusalem remained Jebusite until David captured it (2 Sam 5:6-7). The Jebusite stronghold at the heart of the land allocation is the clearest example of the gap between the ideal grant and the actual possession — a gap that required the Davidic king to finally close.

16.1: vayetze hagoral libnei Yosef (goral, H1486; bnei Yosef, H3130): 'the lot went out for the children of Joseph.' Joseph's two sons — Ephraim and Manasseh — together constitute the largest tribal territory in the central highlands. The singular 'lot for the children of Joseph' (before the separate Ephraim/Manasseh descriptions) reflects the administrative unity of the Joseph tribes: they received a common territory that was subsequently divided between the two half-tribes. Min haYarden biriycho (from the Jordan at Jericho) — the territory begins at the Jordan crossing site, the most sacred geographical location of the conquest narrative.
16.2: veyatzva miBeitel Luz: 'it goes out from Bethel to Luz.' Bethel and Luz are identified as the same place (Gen 28:19: 'he called the name of that place Bethel, but the name of the city was Luz at first'). The pre-Israelite name Luz (nut-tree or almond-tree) was replaced by the Israelite name Bethel (House of God) after Jacob's theophany. The boundary's passage through Bethel is significant: Bethel was both the site of the patriarchal promise (Gen 28:10-22) and, after the division of the kingdom, the site of Jeroboam's golden calves (1 Kgs 12:28-29). The sacred site that anchored the promise to Jacob became the site of the northern kingdom's idolatry.
16.3: veyarad Yama el-gevul haYafleiti: 'it goes down westward to the border of the Japhletites.' The southern boundary of the Joseph territory descends westward from the central highland to the Shephelah and coastal plain. The naming of boundary groups ('the border of the Japhletites') reflects the ethnic geography of the pre-conquest period — populations whose names are preserved only in boundary descriptions like this one. The specificity of these ethnic boundary markers suggests the antiquity of the boundary traditions: later redactors would not have invented obscure group names, but preservers of ancient records would have copied what they received.
16.4: vayinchalu bnei Yosef Menasheh ve'Efrayim: 'so the children of Joseph — Manasseh and Ephraim — received their inheritance.' The summary formula precedes the detailed descriptions. The order Manasseh-Ephraim here reverses the order in which Jacob blessed them (Gen 48:14-20 — Ephraim first, Manasseh second) — a reminder that Jacob deliberately elevated Ephraim over Manasseh. Ephraim will indeed be the dominant tribe of the northern kingdom ('Ephraim' becomes a synonym for the northern kingdom in prophetic literature), while Manasseh's territory was divided between two sides of the Jordan.
16.5: gevul bnei Efrayim lemishpechotam: 'the border of the children of Ephraim by their clans.' Ephraim's territory occupies the central highlands south of Manasseh — the most important agricultural zone in the northern territories. The hills of Ephraim are lower and more accessible than the northern highlands, with numerous springs and wadis supporting intensive agriculture. Shiloh — the tabernacle site during the period of the judges (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4) — lies within Ephraim's territory, making this tribe the host of the central sanctuary for the pre-monarchic period.
16.6: veyatza hagvul yamah el-Michmeta mitzafon: 'the border goes out westward to Michmethath on the north.' The boundary description of Ephraim is brief compared to Judah's — the tribe's territory is smaller and the boundaries simpler. Michmethath (possibly 'hiding place' or 'ambush') was on the border between Ephraim and Manasseh. The verb yatza (goes out, extends) and yafne (turns) are the standard boundary-description verbs, showing a consistent administrative vocabulary throughout the allotment chapters.
16.7: veyarad miYanoach Atarot uNa'arat: 'it goes down from Janoah to Ataroth and Naarath.' The eastern boundary of Ephraim descends toward the Jordan valley. Ataroth appears also in the Gad-Reuben territory (Num 32:3,34), suggesting a common name for multiple settlements. The eastern descent of Ephraim's territory toward the Jordan created the boundary with Benjamin below and Manasseh above — a complex three-way territorial junction managed through careful boundary delimitation.
16.8: miyTappuach yeled hagvul yamah nachal Qanah: 'from Tappuah the border goes westward to the brook Kanah.' Nachal Qanah (Brook Kanah, possibly 'brook of reeds') is a significant wadI on the western boundary of Ephraim, ultimately draining to the Mediterranean. The wadi serves as both a natural boundary and a water source — the dry-season stream bed provides a visible demarcation. The mention of Tappuah (apple/quince-tree town) as a border town connects to the detailed account of Manasseh's border with Tappuah in Josh 17:7-8.
16.9: ve'haarym hamivdalot libnei Efrayim betoch nachalat bnei Menasheh: 'and the cities that were set apart for the children of Ephraim within the inheritance of Manasseh.' The arrangement of Ephraim having separate cities within Manasseh's territory — enclave settlements across tribal boundaries — is an unusual administrative feature reflecting the political complexity of the Joseph tribes. The enclave cities (all with their villages) reflect the intermingling of the two tribes in actual settlement patterns as opposed to the ideal territorial boundary.
16.10: velo-gershu et-haKena'ani hayoshev beGazer (garash, H1644; lo-garshu, H3808+1644): 'they did not drive out the Canaanites who lived in Gezer.' Lo-gershu (they did not drive out) is the chapter's key term and the beginning of a pattern that will characterize the entire allocation of the northern tribes: partial obedience in the face of fortified Canaanite cities. Gezer was one of the most heavily fortified cities in the Shephelah — a mighty Canaanite stronghold that remained Canaanite until Solomon's time, when Egypt conquered it and gave it to Solomon as a dowry (1 Kgs 9:16). mas oved (mas, H4522; oved, H5647): 'forced labor/corvée' — the Canaanites were not expelled but enslaved as laborers, a tolerated compromise that violated YHWH's explicit command (Deut 7:2).

17.1: ki hu hayah gibbor milchamah (gibbor, H1368; milchamah, H4421): 'for he was a man of war.' Manasseh is the firstborn of Joseph, and his firstborn son Machir is specifically noted as 'a man of war' — the military reputation that earned him the trans-Jordan allocation of Gilead and Bashan (Num 32:39-40). Gibbor milchamah (warrior/mighty man of war) is a compound title: gibbor denotes exceptional strength or valor, milchamah designates the military context. Machir's military prowess is what qualified his descendants for the most exposed territory — the east side of the Jordan, bordering Ammon and Aram.
17.2: livnei Menasheh haNivtarim lemishpechotam: 'for the rest of the children of Manasseh by their clans.' The west-bank Manasseh territory is allocated to the remaining clans: Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, and Shemida — six sons/clans of Manasseh receiving the western half-tribe territory. Shechem (Shekhem, shoulder/ridge) is among these — the strategically located city at the pass between Ebal and Gerizim (the covenant ceremony site of ch8). The name Shechem appears as both a person (Manasseh's descendant) and the city that his descendants presumably occupied — the personal name reflecting the clan's territorial association.
17.3: uvTzlofchad ben-Chefer lo-hayu lo vanim ki im-vanot (vanot, H1323): 'Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons but only daughters.' Zelophehad's daughters (Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah) return to claim the inheritance established for them in Numbers 27:1-11, when they argued before Moses that their father's portion should not be lost simply because he had no sons. YHWH ruled in their favor — a landmark legal decision in ancient Israel that preserved daughters' inheritance rights in the absence of sons. Their reappearance in Joshua 17 confirms that the Mosaic ruling is now implemented in the actual land allocation.
17.4: va'tikrevanah lifnei El'azar hakohen (qarav, H7126): 'they drew near before Eleazar the priest.' Qarav (draw near, approach) has both ordinary and liturgical uses — approaching a judge or official, but also drawing near to YHWH in worship. The legal petition before Eleazar the priest (the successor of Aaron) and Joshua replicates the earlier case before Moses and Eleazar in Numbers 27:2. The continuity of the legal procedure — women bringing a test case before the appointed leadership — demonstrates that Joshua's governance followed Mosaic precedent. Ketsivah YHWH et-Mosheh (as YHWH commanded Moses) — the legal basis is the divine precedent.
17.5: vayiplu chelqei Menasheh asarah (chalaq, H2506; asarah, H6235): 'there fell ten portions to Manasseh.' The addition of Zelophehad's five daughters to the five other Manassite clans produces ten portions — an accounting that demonstrates the daughters' claim is legally equivalent to the clan-heads'. The number ten gives Manasseh the largest allocation among the half-tribes, matching the size of the territory (the fertile central highlands plus the Gilead/Bashan east of the Jordan). The daughters' inheritance is not a concession but a genuine tribal allocation calculated alongside the men's.
17.6: ki nachu bnot Menasheh bechelev benav: 'for the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance together with his sons.' The explicit statement of the daughters' equal participation in the inheritance is the narrative's confirmation that the Numbers 27 ruling was fully implemented. Nachalah (inheritance) for the daughters is the same word used throughout for the tribal allocations — not a secondary or lesser form of possession but the same covenantal land-grant. The social innovation of daughters' inheritance rights in ancient Israel — unusual in the ancient Near East — is given permanent place in the land allotment records.
17.7: vayehi gevul Menasheh me'Asher el-Michmetat: 'the border of Manasseh was from Asher to Michmethath.' Manasseh's western territory borders Asher in the north and extends south to the Ephraim boundary (v.10). The boundary description of west-bank Manasseh is complex because its territory interdigitated with Issachar's and Asher's territories through the system of enclave cities (vv.11-13). The geographical complexity reflects the actual settlement patterns: the Joseph tribes and the northern tribes occupied overlapping territory in the Jezreel Valley region.
17.8: leMenasheh hayetah eretz Tappuach: 'to Manasseh belonged the land of Tappuah.' The apple-tree town (Tappuah) and its surrounding land is allocated to Manasseh, but the city itself is on Ephraim's border (16:8). The distinction between land (eretz) and city (ir) reflects the ANE administrative reality: territorial control over agricultural land and administrative control of a city could be held by different parties. The city of Tappuah belonged to Ephraim's clans even though the agricultural land around it was Manasseh's — a practical arrangement for a border community.
17.9: veyarad hagvul nachal Qanah: 'the border goes down to the brook Kanah.' The Kanah wadi (appearing also in Ephraim's boundary, 16:8) forms the natural boundary between the two Joseph tribes in the western lowlands. The same stream serves double duty as a boundary feature for both Ephraim (northern edge) and Manasseh (southern edge). The cities within Manasseh's territory that were among Ephraim's cities (v.9b) represent the same administrative complexity as Ephraim's enclaves in Manasseh (16:9) — a reciprocal arrangement that defies simple territorial mapping.
17.10: Efrayim mitzafon uleMenasheh mitzafon: 'Ephraim is on the north side, Manasseh is on the north side.' The formula for the complex northern boundary: the territory runs from Asher on the west to Issachar on the east, with both Joseph tribes sharing access. The Sea of Galilee-Jezreel Valley-Carmel range forms the northern boundary of the central territories. Manasseh's territory in the Jezreel Valley plain will be the site of numerous decisive battles in Israel's history: Megiddo (the watchtower of the valley), Jezreel, Beth-shan.
17.11: vayehi limnasheh bivIssachar uvAsher Beit She'an uchnoteha: 'Manasseh had in Issachar and Asher, Beth-shean and its villages.' The enclave cities of Manasseh within Issachar and Asher territories (vv.11-12) include some of the most strategically important cities in the land: Beth-shean (at the junction of the Jezreel Valley and the Jordan Valley), Ibleam, Dor, En-dor, Taanach, and Megiddo. Beit She'an (House of Rest or House of Shean) was a major Egyptian administrative center — the walls of Saul's body were hung on its walls by the Philistines (1 Sam 31:10-12). The city's strategic value at the valley junction made it perpetually contested.
17.12: velo-yachlu bnei Menasheh lehorisham (lo-yachlu, H3808+3201; yarash, H3423): 'the children of Manasseh could not dispossess those cities.' The key phrase replicates exactly the Ephraim failure at Gezer (16:10): lo-yachlu lehorisham (they could not dispossess them). The pattern is becoming a structural feature of the northern allocation chapters: for each tribe, the formal allocation is accompanied by the note that certain cities were not taken. Taanach, Megiddo, Beth-shean — the most strategically important cities in the north — remained Canaanite. The unconquered cities are not marginal towns but the dominant urban centers that shaped the political landscape.
17.13: vayehi ki chazqu bnei Yisrael vayitnu et-haKena'ani lemas (amas, H553; mas, H4522): 'when the Israelites grew strong they put the Canaanites to forced labor but did not drive them out completely.' The pattern: strength used not for complete conquest but for economic exploitation. Chazaq (grew strong) is the same word used for 'be strong and courageous' in ch1 — but here strength produces tolerated servitude rather than obedience to YHWH's command. The repeated mas oved (forced labor) throughout the allotment chapters is the OT's documentation of Israel's systematic compromise: they replaced the commanded cherem (destruction) with the convenient mas (slave labor). The downstream consequences of this compromise fill the book of Judges.
17.14: madu'a natata li nachalah goral echad (goral, H1486; nachalah, H5159): 'why have you given me only one lot and one portion as an inheritance?' The Joseph tribes' complaint about their allocation is the first recorded dissatisfaction with the land distribution. The grounds: their size (am rav, great/numerous people) and YHWH's blessing (berachtani, he has blessed me). The argument is demographic — a large, blessed people deserves proportionate territory. The complaint is not illegitimate on its face: the Joseph tribes were the largest (Num 1:32-35: Ephraim 40,500; Manasseh 32,200 = 72,700) and their territory was indeed constrained by Canaanite control of the valley cities.
17.15: im-rav atah aleh lecha haya'ar: 'if you are a great people, go up to the forest.' Joshua's answer is a challenge: if you need more space, create it. Ya'ar (forest/jungle) is the uncultivated hill country that could be cleared for agriculture. Uve'erat lecha sham be'eretz haPerizzi vehaRefaim (clear a place for yourself in the land of the Perizzites and the Rephaim) — two populations known for their presence in the highland wilderness. Joshua's response refuses to simply give them more cultivated land; he points them to the work of clearing the forested highlands. The answer is practical: expansion requires labor, not complaint.
17.16: veyomer har Efrayim lo yimtza lanu: 'the hill of Ephraim is not enough for us.' The Joseph tribes acknowledge the constraint but press further: the hill country is insufficient, and the valley cities remain Canaanite with their iron chariots (rechev barzel, H7393+1270). Rechev barzel (iron chariots) — the Iron Age military technology that gave Canaanite city-states decisive tactical advantage in the flat valley terrain. Iron chariots could not be effectively countered by infantry on the plains; they were irrelevant in the hill country's rough terrain. The Joseph tribes' complaint reveals their real fear: they cannot take the valley cities by conventional means.
17.17: am rav atah vekoach gadol lach: 'you are a numerous people and have great power.' Joshua affirms their demographic argument but reframes it: greatness requires corresponding effort, not proportionate handout. Lo-yihyeh lecha goral echad (you shall not have only one lot) — the promise of expansion, but earned, not given. The response models a leadership principle: affirm the legitimate complaint while redirecting energy toward solution rather than continued grievance. Joshua does not dismiss the Joseph tribes' concern but refuses to solve it administratively when the solution requires them to act.
17.18: ki har yihyeh lach ki ya'ar hu uve'arto vehayah lecha totzatav: 'for the hill country shall be yours, for though it is forested, you shall clear it and its far end shall be yours.' Uve'arto (clear it) — the imperative of active landtaking. Totzatav (its going-out, its far border) — the edge of the cleared land becomes the tribe's new boundary. The vision is expansionary: clearing the forest extends the inheritance, each tree felled marking new territory claimed. ki rechev barzel lo: 'though they have iron chariots.' Joshua acknowledges the military reality of the valley cities and still says the hill country is winnable. The faith-logic of the conquest: YHWH gives the land, but the people must take it — and where human strength is insufficient, YHWH's presence makes the difference (cf. Judg 4-5, where Deborah defeats iron chariots in the same valley).

24.1:
wayyeʾĕsōp yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-šibṭê yiśrāʾēl šĕkemāh — 'Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem.' The verb ʾāsap (gather, collect) is used for solemn assemblies; the site Shechem (šĕkem) carries enormous weight. Gen 12:6 places Abraham's first Canaanite altar at 'the oak of Moreh at Shechem'; Jacob buried his household's foreign gods here (Gen 35:4); the Samaritan tradition identified it as the central sacred site. Joshua frames the covenant renewal at the place where the land-promise was first given and where idols were first renounced.

wayyiqqāhălû lĕpanê hāʾĕlōhîm — 'they presented themselves before God.' The assembly is a theophanic encounter, not a political rally; standing before hāʾĕlōhîm is the vocabulary of divine court and covenant ceremony.
24.2:
kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'thus says the LORD God of Israel.' This is the prophetic messenger formula (kōh ʾāmar) — the standard opening of prophetic oracles (Amos 1:3; Jer 2:2; Ezek 2:4). Joshua is not speaking in his own name but as YHWH's messenger, introducing the Heilsgeschichte in YHWH's first-person voice. The formula signals that what follows is divinely authoritative speech, not human historical summary.

mēʿôlām ('long ago, from of old') + wayyēšĕbû ʿēber hannāhār ('they lived beyond the River') — the temporal and geographic double-frame: Israel's origins are both ancient and foreign. wayyaʿabdû ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — 'and they served other gods.' YHWH begins his self-presentation by grounding the covenant in a history that starts with Israel's ancestors in idolatry, not in their righteousness. The covenant is entirely God's initiative.
24.3: wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-ʾăbîkem ʾet-ʾabrāhām mēʿēber hannāhār — 'I took your ancestor Abraham from beyond the River.' The first-person verb-chain begins: wāʾeqqaḥ (I took), wāʾōlēk (I led), wāʾarbeh (I multiplied). YHWH's acts are the exclusive subject; Abraham and the patriarchs are objects, not agents. This syntactic choice is theological: the covenant is founded on divine initiative at every stage, not on human merit or response.
24.4: waʾettēn lĕyiṣḥāq ʾet-yaʿăqōb wĕʾet-ʿēśāw — 'to Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau.' The verb nātan (give) governs the patriarchal history — God gave the descendants, the land, and eventually the nation. wāʾettēn lĕʿēśāw ʾet-har śēʿîr lāreše't — 'I gave Esau the hill country of Seir as a possession.' The inclusion of Esau's portion acknowledges YHWH's sovereignty over all land distribution, not just Israel's; Edom's land is equally a divine grant. This forestalls any claim that YHWH is merely Israel's tribal deity.
24.5: wāʾešlaḥ ʾet-mōšeh wĕʾet-ʾahărōn waʾeggōp ʾet-miṣrayim — 'I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt.' The verb nāgap (strike, plague) is used specifically of divine plague-action (Exod 12:23; Num 14:12); it is YHWH who plagues, Moses and Aaron being his instruments. The pairing of sending with striking — 'I sent... and I struck' — maintains the divine agency throughout. The people are brought out, not by their courage or Moses's leadership, but by YHWH's direct action.
24.6: wāʾôṣîʾ ʾet-ʾăbôtêkem mimmṣrayim wattāboʾû hayyāmmāh — 'I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, and they came to the sea.' The crossing from Egypt to the sea (Reed Sea) is narrated with the same first-person divine action: wāʾôṣîʾ ('I brought out'). The Egyptians pursue with rekeb ûpārāšîm (chariots and cavalry) — exactly the military technology that Israel was forbidden to build (Deut 17:16; Josh 11:6); the contrast is made visible: YHWH defeats the chariot-and-cavalry superpower without Israel needing to match it.
24.7: wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-YHWH wayyāśem maʾăpēl bênêkem ûbên hammiṣrîm — 'they cried to the LORD, and he put darkness between you and the Egyptians.' The ʾōpel (thick darkness, deep gloom) at the sea is mentioned only here and in Exod 14:20 (the pillar of cloud/fire that came between the camps). Joshua's Heilsgeschichte preserves a detail that could be lost in the standard retelling. wayyābēʾ ʿālāyw ʾet-hayyām wayyĕkassēhû — 'he brought the sea over them and covered them.' YHWH acts as subject; the sea is his instrument.
24.8: wāʾābîʾ ʾetkhem ʾel-ʾereṣ hāʾĕmōrî — 'I brought you to the land of the Amorites.' The trans-Jordan victories (Sihon, Og) are narrated as YHWH-given: wayyillāḥămû ʾittĕkem wāʾettēn ʾōtām bĕyedkem — 'they fought against you and I gave them into your hand.' The conquest is YHWH's giving, not Israel's fighting — the same formula used throughout chs.6-11. wattîrĕšû ʾet-ʾarṣām — 'and you took possession of their land' — only after the divine giving does human possession follow.
24.9: wayyāqom bālāq ben-ṣippôr melek môʾāb wayyillāḥem bĕyiśrāʾēl — 'Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, rose up and fought against Israel.' The verb nišlaḥ ('sent for') frames Balak's use of Balaam as a mercenary curse-weapon. wayyišlaḥ wayyiqrāʾ lĕbilʿām ben-bĕʿôr lĕqallēl ʾetkhem — 'he sent and called for Balaam son of Beor to curse you.' The narrative covers Num 22-24 in two verses; the speed signals that the Balaam episode is already well-known to the audience — Joshua is invoking shared memory, not narrating events.
24.10: wĕlōʾ-ʾābîtî lišmōaʿ lĕbilʿām waybārek bārēk ʾetkhem — 'but I refused to listen to Balaam, and he blessed you again and again.' The verb ʾābāh (be willing, consent) used negatively: YHWH refused to be moved by Balak's hired curse. bārēk bārēk is the infinitive absolute construction intensifying the main verb — 'he blessed you repeatedly, thoroughly' — emphatic and iterative. The same grammatical pattern as Gen 2:17 (mōt tāmût, 'you shall surely die') but here positive: the blessing was emphatic and repeated.
24.11: wattaʿabrû ʾet-hayyardēn wattābōʾû ʾel-yĕrîḥô wayyillāḥămû bākem baʿălê yĕrîḥô — 'you crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho, and the lords of Jericho fought against you.' The Heilsgeschichte reaches the conquest narrative of the book itself: Jericho, then the coalition of peoples (Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites) — the standard seven-nation list. wāʾettēn ʾōtām bĕyedkem — 'and I gave them into your hand' — the YHWH-war formula that has governed every battle in the book.
24.12: wāʾešlaḥ lipnêkem ʾet-haṣṣirʿāh — 'I sent the hornet before you.' The ṣirʿāh (hornet, wasp) as a divine weapon appears in Exod 23:28 and Deut 7:20; here it is said to have driven out the two Amorite kings, adding a detail not in the battle narratives themselves. Whether the hornet is literal (wasp swarms as divine warfare), metaphorical (referring to Egypt's campaigns that weakened Canaan before Israel arrived), or an angelic divine agent, the point is consistent: lōʾ bĕḥarbekā wĕlōʾ bĕqaštekā — 'not by your sword or bow.' YHWH goes out of his way to deny Israel's weapons the credit.
24.13: wāʾettēn lākem ʾereṣ ʾašer lōʾ-yāgaʿtā bāh — 'I gave you a land you did not toil for.' This verse quotes Deut 6:10-11 almost verbatim ('cities you did not build, vineyards and olive groves you did not plant'). The repetition is deliberate: this is not merely history but covenant instruction. The abundance they now enjoy (cities, vineyards, olive orchards) is pure grace — they contributed nothing to its creation. The gift sets the stage for the covenant demand: what will you do with what you did not earn?
24.14: wĕʿattāh yĕrāʾû ʾet-YHWH wĕʿibdû ʾōtô bĕtāmîm ûbĕʾĕmet — 'now therefore, fear the LORD and serve him in completeness and truth.' The adverbial pair bĕtāmîm ûbĕʾĕmet qualifies the required service: tāmîm means wholeness, integrity, completeness (the same root as millēʾ ʾaḥărê, wholehearted following); ʾĕmet means faithfulness, reliability, truth. The combination demands a service that is both inwardly complete and externally consistent — no divided loyalty, no hidden compromise. wĕhāsîrû ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾašer-ʿābĕdû ʾăbôtêkem — 'put away the gods your ancestors served' — the imperative hāsîr (remove, put away) is strong; this is not a gentle suggestion but a formal covenant demand. Jacob used the same verb at Shechem (Gen 35:2).
24.15: ûʾim rāʿ bĕʿênêkem laʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH biḥărû lākem hayyôm ʾet-mi taʿăbōdûn — 'if it seems evil to you to serve the LORD, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve.' The verb bāḥar (choose, elect) places the burden of decision on Israel; YHWH does not coerce. Hayyôm ('today') is the covenant urgency word — Deuteronomy's characteristic call to immediate decision (Deut 4:40; 5:1; 26:16). wĕʾānōkî ûbêtî naʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH — 'but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.' Joshua's ʾānōkî (emphatic 'I') stands against the hypothetical choice; his household's commitment is personal and public, declared before witnesses.
24.16: ḥālîlāh lānû mēʿăzōb ʾet-YHWH laʿăbōd ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — 'far be it from us to forsake the LORD to serve other gods.' Ḥālîlāh is an oath-like repudiation formula ('God forbid! Far be it!'), used at moments of extreme horror (Gen 18:25; 44:7; 1 Sam 14:45; 2 Sam 23:17). The strength of the formula signals that the people understand the gravity of what they are being asked; they are not responding perfunctorily.
24.17: Israel's own Heilsgeschichte recital: they repeat back the covenant history YHWH narrated in vv.2-13, now in their own words. This dialogical structure (God narrates → people narrate back) mirrors ancient covenant ceremony where both parties confess the history that grounds the treaty. ki YHWH ʾĕlōhênû hûʾ hammaʿăleh ʾōtānû — 'for the LORD our God, he is the one who brought us up.' The hûʾ ('he is') is emphatic identification — YHWH, not any other power, is the agent of their history.
24.18: waygāreš YHWH ʾet-kol-hāʿammîm — 'the LORD drove out all the peoples before us.' The verb gāraš (drive out, expel) is used of expelling parties from covenant — the same word used when God expelled Adam from Eden (Gen 3:24). Israel's confession attributes the conquest entirely to YHWH: gam-ʾănaḥnû naʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH kî hûʾ ʾĕlōhênû — 'we also will serve the LORD, for he is our God.' Their commitment is grounded in his identity and acts, not in their ability to maintain it.
24.19:
wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-hāʿām lōʾ tûkĕlûn laʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH — 'Joshua said to the people: you are not able to serve the LORD.' The shock of this statement — coming immediately after the people's enthusiastic confession — is intentional. Joshua is not discouraging commitment but exposing its inadequacy: their confidence in themselves is itself the problem.

kî-ʾĕlōhîm qĕdōšîm hûʾ ʾēl-qannōʾ hûʾ — 'for he is a holy God; he is a jealous God.' Note the grammatical anomaly: ʾĕlōhîm is grammatically plural ('Gods') but the adjective qĕdōšîm is also plural, agreeing with the noun form, while hûʾ ('he') is singular, identifying YHWH as a single person. This is the intensive/majestic plural (Hebrew often uses plural forms to convey transcendent greatness). Qannōʾ (jealous) is the technical covenant term — YHWH's exclusive-loyalty demand (Exod 20:5; 34:14); breaking covenant with him is not merely an error but a personal offense against a party who will not share his rights.

lōʾ-yiśśāʾ lĕpišʿăkem wĕlĕḥaṭṭōʾtêkem — 'he will not forgive your transgressions and sins.' The word nāśāʾ (bear, lift, carry) is the standard term for God's forgiveness — lifting and carrying away sin (Gen 50:17; Ps 32:5; Mic 7:18). YHWH's refusal to bear pešaʿ (rebellion/transgression, the deliberate covenant-breaking term) or ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin, missing the mark) is presented as a consequence of his holiness and jealousy — his very character.
24.20: kî taʿăzebû ʾet-YHWH wĕʿăbadtem ʾĕlōhê nēkār — 'if you forsake the LORD and serve foreign gods.' The verb ʿāzab (forsake, abandon) is the covenant-rupture term — the same word used when men leave father and mother (Gen 2:24), but here applied to the ultimate covenantal relationship. wĕšāb wĕhērāʿ lākem — 'he will turn and harm you.' The šûb ('turn') of YHWH toward the covenant-breaker is the reversal of his protective turning toward Israel; the same divine responsiveness that brought blessing now brings judgment.
24.21: wayyōʾmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ lōʾ kî ʾet-YHWH naʿăbōd — 'the people said to Joshua: No! We will serve the LORD.' The emphatic lōʾ kî ('No! Rather...') is a strong counter-assertion. Having heard Joshua's stark warning about their inability (v.19), the people double down rather than retreat — which sets up the covenant's formal ratification in vv.22-27. Their persistence after the warning shows either genuine faith or dangerous self-confidence; the ambiguity is part of the human covenant condition.
24.22: wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-hāʿām ʿēdîm ʾattem bākem — 'Joshua said to the people: you are witnesses against yourselves.' The plural ʿēdîm (witnesses) is the legal vocabulary of Israelite court procedure; the covenant assembly is simultaneously a juridical assembly. Bākem — 'against yourselves' — the force of the preposition: their own declaration becomes the evidence against them if they defect. The people's response (ʿēdîm, 'witnesses') accepts the self-incriminating oath. This is one of the oldest attested covenant-ratification formulas in the OT.
24.23: wĕʿattāh hāsîrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēkār ʾašer bĕqirbĕkem — 'now therefore put away the foreign gods that are among you.' The command hāsîrû (put away, remove) is the same as v.14, now repeated with the revealing addition: ʾašer bĕqirbĕkem — 'which are among you.' The gods were not left behind in Egypt or Mesopotamia; they are present within the covenant community, hidden (bĕqereb = in the midst, in the interior). The covenant demand is not about past idolatry but current syncretism. wĕhaṭṭû ʾet-lĕbabkem ʾel-YHWH — 'incline your hearts to the LORD' — the verb nāṭāh (stretch, extend, incline) asks for the direction of the will, not merely an external act.
24.24: wayyōʾmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû naʿăbōd ûbĕqōlô nišmāʿ — 'the people said to Joshua: the LORD our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey.' The double commitment pairs ʿābad (serve, worship) with šāmaʿ bĕqôl (obey, lit. 'hear in the voice'). Both terms carry covenant weight: ʿābad is the vocabulary of vassal service to a suzerain; šāmaʿ bĕqôl is the Deuteronomic obedience formula (Deut 13:4; 1 Sam 15:22). The people align themselves as covenant vassals before the divine suzerain.
24.25: wayyikrōt yĕhôšuaʿ bĕrît lāʿām bayyôm hahûʾ wayyāśem lô ḥōq ûmišpāṭ biškem — 'Joshua cut a covenant with the people that day, and set for them statute and ordinance at Shechem.' Three legal terms accumulate: bĕrît (covenant, the treaty-relationship itself), ḥōq (statute, fixed rule, carved-in obligation), mišpāṭ (ordinance, judgment, binding decision). The verb kārat bĕrît ('cut a covenant') is the standard idiom, preserving the ancient practice of cutting animals to ratify treaties (Gen 15:10,17). The location Shechem is the same site as Jacob's putting-away of gods (Gen 35:4) — every Shechem scene in Genesis-Joshua involves the removal of foreign gods and the reassertion of covenant loyalty to YHWH.
24.26:
wayyiktōb yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh bĕsēper tôrat ʾĕlōhîm — 'Joshua wrote these things in the Book of the Law of God.' This is one of only a few OT passages that explicitly describes the extension of the written Torah — the covenant speech becomes part of the authoritative scroll. Sēper tôrat ʾĕlōhîm is likely the Mosaic Torah or an updated copy; Joshua's additions are authoritative because he speaks under the prophetic messenger formula (v.2) and by divine command.

wayyiqqaḥ ʾeben gĕdôlāh wayyĕqîmehā šām taḥat hāʾallāh ʾašer bĕmiqdaš YHWH — 'he took a large stone and set it up there under the oak at the sanctuary of the LORD.' The oak (ʾallāh) at the sanctuary of YHWH connects to the oak of Moreh at Shechem (Gen 12:6) — the tree where Israel's story in the land began is now the witness-stone's location. The covenant is sealed at the place where the promise was first given.
24.27: wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-kol-hāʿām hinnēh hāʾeben hazzōʾt tihyeh-bānû lĕʿēdāh — 'this stone shall be a witness against us.' The stone is assigned legal standing as a ʿēd (witness, legal testimony-giver). This is not animism — the stone does not have a soul — but the employment of covenant-witness language for an enduring physical object. The rationale: kî-hîʾ šāmĕʿāh ʾēt kol-ʾimrê YHWH ʾašer dibber ʿimmānû — 'for it has heard all the words of the LORD that he spoke to us.' Presence at the covenant assembly constitutes legal knowledge; the stone's physical presence as witness is functionally equivalent to a human witness who heard the oath. The reasoning will reappear in Luke 19:40 — 'the stones would cry out.'
24.28: wayyĕšallaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-hāʿām ʾîš lĕnaḥălātô — 'Joshua dismissed the people, each to his inheritance.' The verb šillaḥ (send away, dismiss) closes the assembly formally; the naḥălāh (inheritance) is the destination — they go to what YHWH has given, armed with the renewed covenant obligations. The chapter's governing word (naḥălāh appears throughout chs.13-22) provides the farewell note: the land-gift and the covenant-obligation are inseparable.
24.29: wayĕhî ʾaḥărê haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh wayyāmot yĕhôšuaʿ bin-nûn ʿebed YHWH — 'after these things Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died.' The title ʿebed YHWH ('servant of the LORD') was applied to Moses throughout the book (Josh 1:1,2,7,13; 8:31,33; 9:24; 11:12,15; 12:6; 13:8; 14:7; 18:7; 22:2-5); here, for the first time, it is applied to Joshua. The death report formally elevates Joshua to Mosaic status — he died as what Moses was: YHWH's servant. Ben-meʾāh wĕʿeśer šānîm — 110 years, the ideal Egyptian lifespan (Gen 50:26; Joseph also died at 110); the number signals a life of full completion.
24.30: wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô bigbûl naḥălātô bĕtimnāt-seraḥ — 'they buried him in the territory of his inheritance at Timnath-serah.' Timnath-serah is the inheritance Caleb-parallel: Joshua received his own territory after all others had been settled (Josh 19:49-50), as we noted there. His burial in his own allotment anchors his identity with the land-promise: he dies where he was given, in the hill country of Ephraim, miṣṣāpôn lĕhar-gāʿaš — 'north of Mount Gaash.' The geographic specificity marks the site as a real place that could be visited — the claim is historical, not merely symbolic.
24.31: wayyaʿăbōd yiśrāʾēl ʾet-YHWH kōl yĕmê yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkōl yĕmê hazĕqēnîm ʾašer hĕʾĕrîkû yāmîm ʾaḥărê yĕhôšuaʿ — 'Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua and all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua.' This is the book's theological assessment of the covenant's durability: it held through one generation of leadership. The implicit warning: what happens when that generation passes? Judges 2:10-12 provides the answer — 'another generation arose who did not know the LORD.' The covenant's success was personal and generational, not automatic or permanent.
24.32: wĕʾet-ʿaṣmôt yôsēp ʾašer-hĕʿělû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl mimmṣrayim qābĕrû biškem — 'the bones of Joseph, which the Israelites had brought from Egypt, they buried at Shechem.' Gen 50:25 records Joseph's oath: pāqōd yipqōd ʾĕlōhîm ʾetkhem — 'God will surely visit you' — and his command to carry his bones. Exod 13:19 records Moses taking the bones at the exodus. The burial at Shechem is the completion of a four-generation promise-chain: Joseph's faith that the God who promised Canaan would deliver it (Heb 11:22 calls this an act of faith), carried through the wilderness, fulfilled here. bĕḥelqat haśśādeh ʾašer qānāh yaʿăqōb mēʾēt bĕnê-ḥămôr — in the plot Jacob bought from Hamor's sons (Gen 33:19) — the land transaction from Genesis becomes the burial site in Joshua; purchased land and promised land converge.
24.33: wĕʾelʿāzār ben-ʾahărōn mēt wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô bigbĕʿat pînĕḥās bĕnô — 'Eleazar son of Aaron died and was buried at Gibeah of his son Phinehas.' The book closes with three burials: Joshua (the military-covenant leader), Joseph (the patriarch whose faith spanned the whole story), and Eleazar (the priestly mediator of the allotments, ch.14:1; 17:4; 19:51; 21:1). The triple burial closes the three streams of Israel's leadership — prophetic-military (Joshua), patriarchal-faith (Joseph), and priestly (Eleazar) — each now in the ground of the promised land. The priestly line continues through Phinehas (also named in Ps 106:30 for his covenant zeal); the story does not end, but the founding generation is laid to rest in the land YHWH gave them.
